DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `ctbl_role_permissions`;
CREATE TABLE `ctbl_role_permissions` (
  `id` int(11) NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `role_id` int(11) DEFAULT NULL,
  `permission_id` int(11) DEFAULT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  KEY `role_id_index` (`role_id`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `permission_id_index` (`permission_id`) USING BTREE
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=157 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8mb4;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `ctbl_user_roles`;
CREATE TABLE `ctbl_user_roles` (
  `id` int(11) NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `user_id` int(11) DEFAULT NULL,
  `role_id` int(11) DEFAULT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  KEY `user_id_index` (`user_id`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `role_id_index` (`role_id`) USING BTREE
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=12 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8mb4;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `tbl_exhibit_media`;
CREATE TABLE `tbl_exhibit_media` (
  `id` int(11) unsigned NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `exhibit_uuid` varchar(255) NOT NULL,
  `media_uuid` varchar(255) NOT NULL,
  `media_role` varchar(50) NOT NULL COMMENT 'hero_image | thumbnail',
  `is_deleted` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `created` timestamp NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  `updated` timestamp NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  `created_by` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  UNIQUE KEY `exhibit_role_unique` (`exhibit_uuid`,`media_role`,`is_deleted`),
  KEY `exhibit_uuid_index` (`exhibit_uuid`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `media_uuid_index` (`media_uuid`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `media_role_index` (`media_role`) USING BTREE
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=43 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `tbl_exhibits`;
CREATE TABLE `tbl_exhibits` (
  `id` int(11) unsigned NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `uuid` varchar(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  `type` varchar(10) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'exhibit',
  `title` longtext NOT NULL COMMENT '{string | html} title for exhibit banner (R) ',
  `subtitle` longtext COMMENT '{string | html} (default: null, no subtitle displayed)',
  `banner_template` varchar(100) DEFAULT 'banner_1' COMMENT '{''banner_1'' | ''banner_2''} (default: banner_1) ',
  `about_the_curators` longtext COMMENT '{text | html} content for the "About the Curators" page ',
  `alert_text` longtext COMMENT '{string | html} alert banner displayed below hero section (default: null, alert banner not displayed)',
  `hero_image_media_uuid` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `thumbnail_media_uuid` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `hero_image` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL COMMENT '{filename.extension} filename or path to file (default: null, hero image not displayed. image section will be displayed with a gray background if the banner template has a hero image section)',
  `thumbnail` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL COMMENT '{filename.extension} filename. exhibit thumbnail image. (default: null, thumbnail image will be derived from the ''hero_image'' if present.)',
  `description` longtext COMMENT '{string | html} the exhibit banner text',
  `page_layout` varchar(50) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'top_nav' COMMENT '{''top_nav'', ''side_nav} (default: top_nav)',
  `exhibit_template` varchar(50) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'vertical_scroll' COMMENT '{''vertical_scroll'' | ''item_centered''} (R)',
  `exhibit_subjects` longtext,
  `styles` longtext COMMENT 'JSON String',
  `order` int(11) DEFAULT '0',
  `is_student_curated` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_published` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0' COMMENT '{0,1} (default: 0)',
  `is_featured` tinyint(1) DEFAULT '0' COMMENT '{0,1) if 1, will appear in featured exhibits display (default: 0)',
  `is_embedded` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_preview` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_locked` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `locked_by_user` int(11) DEFAULT '0',
  `locked_at` timestamp NULL DEFAULT NULL,
  `is_deleted` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_indexed` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `owner` int(11) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `created` timestamp NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  `updated` timestamp NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  `created_by` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `updated_by` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  KEY `uuid_index` (`uuid`),
  KEY `is_published_index` (`is_published`),
  KEY `is_deleted_index` (`is_deleted`) USING BTREE
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=22 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `tbl_grid_items`;
CREATE TABLE `tbl_grid_items` (
  `id` int(11) unsigned NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `uuid` varchar(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  `is_member_of_grid` varchar(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  `is_member_of_exhibit` varchar(255) NOT NULL,
  `repo_uuid` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `thumbnail` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `thumbnail_media_uuid` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `title` longtext NOT NULL,
  `caption` text,
  `item_type` varchar(50) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'image',
  `mime_type` varchar(100) DEFAULT NULL,
  `media` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `media_uuid` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `text` longtext,
  `wrap_text` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '1',
  `description` longtext,
  `type` varchar(100) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'item',
  `layout` varchar(100) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'media_top',
  `media_width` int(11) NOT NULL DEFAULT '50',
  `media_padding` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '1',
  `alt_text` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `is_alt_text_decorative` tinyint(1) DEFAULT '0',
  `pdf_open_to_page` int(11) NOT NULL DEFAULT '1',
  `item_subjects` longtext,
  `styles` longtext,
  `order` int(11) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_repo_item` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_kaltura_item` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_embedded` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_published` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_locked` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `locked_by_user` int(11) DEFAULT '0',
  `locked_at` timestamp NULL DEFAULT NULL,
  `is_deleted` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `owner` int(11) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `created` timestamp NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  `updated` timestamp NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  `created_by` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `updated_by` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  UNIQUE KEY `uuid_index` (`uuid`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `is_member_of_grid_index` (`is_member_of_grid`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `is_member_of_exhibit_index` (`is_member_of_exhibit`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `is_published_index` (`is_published`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `is_deleted_index` (`is_deleted`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `media_uuid_index` (`media_uuid`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `thumbnail_media_uuid_index` (`thumbnail_media_uuid`) USING BTREE
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=386 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8;

  `updated` timestamp NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,

  `updated_by` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,

) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=86 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `tbl_media_library`;
CREATE TABLE `tbl_media_library` (
  `id` int(11) unsigned NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `uuid` varchar(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  `name` varchar(255) NOT NULL,
  `description` text,
  `alt_text` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `is_alt_text_decorative` tinyint(1) DEFAULT '0',
  `topics_subjects` longtext,
  `genre_form_subjects` longtext,
  `places_subjects` longtext,
  `media_type` varchar(50) NOT NULL,
  `mime_type` varchar(100) DEFAULT NULL,
  `item_type` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `call_number` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `filename` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `original_filename` varchar(500) NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  `ingest_method` varchar(255) NOT NULL,
  `repo_uuid` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `repo_handle` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `kaltura_entry_id` varchar(100) DEFAULT NULL COMMENT 'Kaltura entry id',
  `kaltura_thumbnail_url` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL COMMENT 'Kaltura full thumbnail URL',
  `exhibits` longtext,
  `size` bigint(20) DEFAULT NULL,
  `storage_path` varchar(500) DEFAULT NULL,
  `thumbnail_path` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `exif_data` longtext,
  `media_width` int(10) unsigned DEFAULT NULL COMMENT 'Image/video pixel width for IIIF canvas',
  `media_height` int(10) unsigned DEFAULT NULL COMMENT 'Image/video pixel height for IIIF canvas',
  `media_duration` decimal(10,3) DEFAULT NULL,
  `iiif_manifest` longtext,
  `is_deleted` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `owner` int(11) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `created` timestamp NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  `updated` timestamp NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  `created_by` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `updated_by` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  UNIQUE KEY `uuid_index` (`uuid`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `is_deleted_index` (`is_deleted`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `original_filename_index` (`original_filename`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `ingest_method_index` (`ingest_method`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `kaltura_entry_id_index` (`kaltura_entry_id`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `repo_uuid_index` (`repo_uuid`) USING BTREE
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=792 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `tbl_standard_items`;
CREATE TABLE `tbl_standard_items` (
  `id` int(11) unsigned NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `uuid` varchar(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  `is_member_of_exhibit` varchar(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  `thumbnail` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `thumbnail_media_uuid` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `title` longtext NOT NULL,
  `caption` text,
  `item_type` varchar(100) NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  `mime_type` varchar(100) DEFAULT NULL,
  `media` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `media_uuid` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `text` longtext,
  `wrap_text` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '1',
  `description` longtext,
  `type` varchar(50) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'item',
  `layout` varchar(255) DEFAULT 'media_right',
  `media_width` int(11) DEFAULT '50',
  `media_padding` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '1',
  `alt_text` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `is_alt_text_decorative` tinyint(1) DEFAULT '0',
  `pdf_open_to_page` int(11) NOT NULL DEFAULT '1',
  `item_subjects` longtext,
  `styles` longtext,
  `order` int(11) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_repo_item` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_kaltura_item` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_embedded` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0' COMMENT 'for audio and video',
  `is_published` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_locked` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `locked_by_user` int(11) DEFAULT '0',
  `locked_at` timestamp NULL DEFAULT NULL,
  `is_deleted` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `owner` int(11) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `created` timestamp NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  `updated` timestamp NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  `created_by` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `updated_by` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  UNIQUE KEY `uuid_index` (`uuid`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `is_member_of_exhibit_index` (`is_member_of_exhibit`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `is_published_index` (`is_published`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `is_deleted_index` (`is_deleted`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `media_uuid_index` (`media_uuid`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `thumbnail_media_uuid_index` (`thumbnail_media_uuid`) USING BTREE
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=371 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `tbl_timeline_items`;
CREATE TABLE `tbl_timeline_items` (
  `id` int(11) unsigned NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `uuid` varchar(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  `is_member_of_timeline` varchar(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  `is_member_of_exhibit` varchar(255) NOT NULL,
  `repo_uuid` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `thumbnail` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `thumbnail_media_uuid` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `title` longtext NOT NULL,
  `caption` text,
  `item_type` varchar(50) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'image',
  `mime_type` varchar(100) DEFAULT NULL,
  `media` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `media_uuid` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `text` longtext,
  `wrap_text` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '1',
  `description` longtext,
  `type` varchar(100) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'item',
  `layout` varchar(100) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'media_top',
  `media_width` int(11) NOT NULL DEFAULT '50',
  `media_padding` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '1',
  `alt_text` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `is_alt_text_decorative` tinyint(1) DEFAULT '0',
  `pdf_open_to_page` int(11) NOT NULL DEFAULT '1',
  `item_subjects` longtext,
  `styles` longtext,
  `order` int(11) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `date` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL COMMENT 'vertical timeline (year-month-day)',
  `is_repo_item` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_kaltura_item` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_embedded` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_published` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `is_locked` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `locked_by_user` int(11) DEFAULT '0',
  `locked_at` timestamp NULL DEFAULT NULL,
  `is_deleted` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `owner` int(11) NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  `created` timestamp NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  `updated` timestamp NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  `created_by` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  `updated_by` varchar(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  UNIQUE KEY `uuid_index` (`uuid`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `is_member_of_timeline_index` (`is_member_of_timeline`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `is_member_of_exhibit_index` (`is_member_of_exhibit`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `is_published_index` (`is_published`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `is_deleted_index` (`is_deleted`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `media_uuid_index` (`media_uuid`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `thumbnail_media_uuid_index` (`thumbnail_media_uuid`) USING BTREE
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=110 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `tbl_user_permissions`;

  `id` int(11) NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `permission` varchar(255) NOT NULL,
  `description` text NOT NULL,
  `has_permission` varchar(255) NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`)
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=35 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8mb4;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `tbl_user_roles`;

  `id` int(11) NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `role` varchar(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'admin',
  `description` text,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`)
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=5 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8mb4;

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `tbl_users`;
CREATE TABLE `tbl_users` (
  `id` int(11) unsigned NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
  `du_id` varchar(50) NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  `email` varchar(100) NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  `first_name` varchar(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  `last_name` varchar(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  `is_active` tinyint(1) NOT NULL DEFAULT '1',
  `token` varchar(500) DEFAULT NULL,
  `created` timestamp NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  `last_login` timestamp NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  PRIMARY KEY (`id`),
  UNIQUE KEY `du_id_index` (`du_id`) USING BTREE,
  UNIQUE KEY `email_index` (`email`) USING BTREE,
  UNIQUE KEY `token_index` (`token`) USING BTREE,
  KEY `is_active_index` (`is_active`) USING BTREE
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=12 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8;

(1, 'bea561ce-5746-4adb-b7d2-b9f997fbcd37', 'exhibit', 'The Loewenstien Family', 'A Story of Survival', 'banner_1', '<h3>Credits</h3>\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n<p>All material featured was assembled by Maria Lowenstein and donated by her son, Henry Lowenstein.</p>', '', 'e7c7022f-6cbd-4f90-913b-fe93a06074c3', '25b5f7a2-f28c-4c26-8654-12598d8e582a', '', '', '<div>\n    <p>In 1933, the year of Hitler\'s rise to power, approximately 160,000 Jews lived in Berlin, Germany, which was less than 4% of its population. By 1939, an estimated 80,000 Berlin Jews had managed to emigrate. Between 1941 and 1944 more than 60,000 were deported to Eastern European ghettoes and death camps. Only about 7,000 were known to have survived by 1945.</p>\n    <p>The historic documents in the Lowenstein Family Papers and Art collection tell the story of one Jewish family\'s miraculous survival amidst the horrors of the Holocaust. Two exceedingly rare documents from 1942 served as eviction notices. They order the recipients to report at a certain date and time to a government building in Berlin. In reality the notice was a summons of deportation to death camps. If obeyed, the recipient was killed. If not obeyed, the recipient most certainly did not retain the letter. That notice led to the deaths of an estimated 60,000 Jews.</p>\n    <p>The Loewensteins (Lowenstein after immigration) - Max, Maria, Karin, and Henry - lived in Berlin and, beginning in 1933, experienced the ever tightening Nazi noose. Like many other Jewish families they tried desperately to find ways to leave Germany. The beginnings of the Holocaust burst forth on Kristallnacht in 1938. Synagogues were burned and thousands of Jews were taken to concentration camps. Many were never seen again.</p>\n    <p>Thirteen-year-old Henry was lucky to be one of 10,000 children to be saved by the Kindertransport in 1939. Kindertransports were organized by British aid organizations to bring predominantly German, Austrian, and Czechoslovakian Jewish children to the United Kingdom. Henry was able to reunite with his family in 1947.</p>\n    <p>Henry\'s mother Maria, born into a Lutheran family, used her Aryan status to protect her loved ones. Her courage saved the family from deportation and certain death on numerous occasions. She brought the documents in this collection to America in 1946.</p>\n    <p>Henry Lowenstein donated them to the Ira M. and Peryle Hayutin Beck Memorial Archives. The collection is located in the Anderson Academic Commons, Special Collections and Archives.</p>\n    <div>\n    \n    <div>\n    <div>\n    <div>\n    <div>\n    <div>\n    <p>More Information Weaver, an associate professor and director of the Emergent Digital Practices program in the School of Art and Art History, combined images, objects, sounds, and videos to reanimate extinct species to awaken memories of lost life forms. This exhibition was on display at University Libraries (formerly Penrose Library) from October 14, 2010 through February 14, 2011.\nThe exhibit’s intent is to bring together separate artifacts of lost ecological memory. These artifacts are the deep colors and textures of extinct birds, sonic translations of the DNA and proteins of missing species, and books containing the published records of North America’s breathtaking wildlife. These sounds and images of lost habitats create an immersive sensory experience for the audience. (3, 'f4a8ec55-1961-43ca-a805-f7ccc957479b', 'exhibit', '#No More Pios', 'The Legacy of Settler Colonialism and the University of Denver', 'banner_3', '&lt;p&gt;The curators of this exhibition exploring the impacts of DU mascots and nicknames on Native American students, faculty, staff, allies, and community would like to acknowledge how their own experiences and positionalities influenced the research and development of this exhibit. Each curator approached this topic as non-Native people who identify as white women. They recognize the complex relationships between Indigenous peoples and universities, the unequal power dynamics between non-Native academics and Indigenous communities, and the exploitation of Indigenous peoples, their representations, and their lands.&lt;&#x2F;p&gt;&lt;p&gt;Kate Crowe (Associate Professor, Curator of Special Collections and Archives)&lt;&#x2F;p&gt;&lt;p&gt;Elia Trucks (Assistant Professor, Student Outreach Librarian)&lt;&#x2F;p&gt;&lt;p&gt;Madison Sussmann (Exhibits Manager)&lt;&#x2F;p&gt;', '<div style=\"font-size: 1.2em;\">Warning: This exhibit contains materials that are racist and may be painful or traumatizing to viewers. Please proceed with caution.</div>', '1ca7105b-74b9-4c45-aedc-2ba48e2bba30', 'dcd17821-018e-47e4-b1b7-e824f93dfe29', 'f4a8ec55-1961-43ca-a805-f7ccc957479b_1738095983301_exhibit_hero.jpg', 'f4a8ec55-1961-43ca-a805-f7ccc957479b_1737587431548_exhibit_thumbnail.jpg', '<div><div><p>Founded in 1864, the University of Denver (DU) has a history of athletic team nicknames, mascots, and university branding that draw heavily on the identity as the oldest private university in Colorado. The history of DU is also forever connected to the Sand Creek Massacre, where more than 160 Cheyenne and Arapaho people - mostly elders, women, and children - were murdered by Colorado\'s Third Cavalry in November 1864, just six months after the University\'s founding. </p><p>Like many colleges and universities in the American West, DU has appropriated and romanticized Native American peoples and the U.S. settler colonial project. While many of our alumni associate the term \"pioneer\" with positive memories of the university, for many others, this term represents the forced expropriation of Native lands. Universities use mascots and nicknames to create a sense of community, but for Native students, alumni, faculty, staff, this term is a reminder of stolen land and genocide.</p><p>This exhibit explores DU\'s history of mascots, nicknames, and branding and the changes over its 150 year history, which often took place alongside related social and cultural changes. “Pioneer” has not always been the nickname and Denver Boone was not the first mascot. DU’s traditions have changed considerably over time, and these changes – like the demands to remove \"Pioneer\" as the nickname – have largely been student-driven.</p></div><div class=\"label-wrapper\"><div class=\"label\" style=\"margin: 50px auto 20px auto; width: 40%; text-align: center; font-size: 1.25em\"><hr style=\"height: 3px; color: #8D1737; opacity: 0.75; width: 55%; margin: 1rem auto 2rem auto;\"><div>DU is long overdue for another change.</div><br><div>#NoMorePios</div><hr style=\"height: 3px; color: #8D1737; opacity: 0.75; width: 55%; margin: 2rem auto 1rem auto;\"></div></div></div>', 'top_nav', 'vertical_scroll', NULL, '{\"exhibit\":{\"introduction\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#CAC0A1\",\"color\":\"#1D1B1C\",\"fontFamily\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\"},\"navigation\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#A89D78\",\"color\":\"#1D1B1C\",\"fontFamily\":\"Neue Haas Grotesk Text Pro\",\"fontSize\":\"22px\"},\"template\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#CAC0A1\",\"color\":\"#1D1B1C\",\"fontFamily\":\"Neue Haas Grotesk Text Pro\",\"fontSize\":\"20px\"},\"heading1\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#FFFFFF\",\"color\":\"#8B2332\",\"fontFamily\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"32px\"},\"heading2\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"\",\"color\":\"#8B2332\",\"fontFamily\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"32px\"},\"heading3\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"\"},\"item1\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#FFFFFF\",\"color\":\"#000000\"},\"item2\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#FFFFFF\"},\"item3\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#A89D78\",\"color\":\"#ffffff\"}}}', 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0, NULL, 0, 0, 8, '2025-01-22 18:02:10', '2026-04-16 17:42:44', 'Madison Sussmann', 'Madison Sussmann'),

(5, 'ce8c91ee-3a3f-402b-91eb-5b81fee2deb0', 'exhibit', 'Books Challenged Through the Decades', 'DU Libraries Promote the Freedom to Read', 'banner_1', 'Curated by Madison Sussmann, Exhibits Librarian.', '', '29804098-8bee-4023-a43c-572d359f2030', '79b6a324-704a-49b7-b826-36cff273b161', 'ce8c91ee-3a3f-402b-91eb-5b81fee2deb0_1744321231351_exhibit_hero.jpg', 'ce8c91ee-3a3f-402b-91eb-5b81fee2deb0_1744321364639_exhibit_thumbnail.jpg', '<p>Book bans, challenges, and censorships span centuries. Books are challenged for various reasons, but most censorship is based on political, religious, sexual, and social justification. The larger social context of the time determines the reactions to books people read. A few historically banned books have become \"classics\" and frequently are included in middle or high school curricula. For example, customs censors promptly banned James Joyce\'s Ulysses in the United States in 1922 for being \"obscene\" when it was published. It could not be reprinted in the U.S. until 1933 when United States District Judge John M. Woolsey of the Southern District of New York ruled that the book was not obscene in a legal sense. In early 1934, it was published for the first time in the U.S. by Random House.</p>\n\n<p>The first recorded censored book in the U.S. was in 1637, when the Puritan government banned the <i>New English Canaan</i> by Thomas Morton in Quincy, Massachusetts, for critiquing Puritan customs and power structures. Then, the U.S. Congress passed the Comstock Act of 1873, which made it illegal to send \"obscene\" and \"immoral\" publications through the U.S. Post Office. Under the law, selling, distributing, and possessing \"obscene\" writings or illustrations was a misdemeanor. Anthony Comstock, head of the New York Society for the Suppression of Vice, was designated to enforce the law in the Post Office. He proceeded to arrest individuals who violated the new law. Among them was Ezra Heywood, author of <i>Cupid\'s Yokes</i>, a feminist publication that argued for a woman\'s right to control her own body.</p>\n\n<p>Along with Heywood, Comstock arrested Dr. Robigne Mortimer Bennet, a librarian, after he received a copy of <i>Cupid\'s Yokes</i> in the mail. During Heywood\'s trial, the judge applied the \"Hicklin test.\" The \"Hicklin test\" was derived from the 1868 British case <i>Regina v. Hicklin</i> decision. The prosecution set the precedent that something could be called obscene \"whether the tendency of the matter is to deprave and corrupt those whose minds are open to such immoral influences.\" Federal courts followed this standard until 1933 when Judge John M. Woolsey focused on the whole of <i>Ulysses</i> as a literary work rather than on a few of its passages.</p>\n\n<p>In 1982, there was a stand against banning books. In <i>Island Trees School District v. Pico</i>, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the First Amendment of the U.S. Constitution limits the powers of school board officials to ban books solely on content. Despite this ruling, numerous attempts throughout the U.S. remained to remove books from schools. <i>The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn</i> is an example of a book frequently challenged throughout the 1980s and 1990s.</p>\n\n<p>According to the American Library Association (ALA), when a book is <strong>challenged</strong>, there is an attempt to remove or restrict it based on the objects of a person or group. A book is <strong>banned</strong> when it is <i>removed</i> from a school or library. Most challenges begin with the intention of protecting children from \"inappropriate\" or \"offensive\" language or content. While these challenges may be made with the best intentions, censorship by librarians of constitutionally recognized free speech violates the First Amendment.</p>\n\n<p>The D.U. Libraries support free access to information and do not partake in book bans. The five books on display and the books on the nearby cart all have a history of being challenged, banned, or otherwise censored. As you will see in this exhibit, \"Books Challenged and Banned Through the Decades,\" challenges and bans are generally not state or nationwide. They are particular small-scale events driven by vocal individuals and groups affected by their social context. For example, when <i>The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn</i> was challenged in 1885, the argument was that it was \"immoral.\" From 1956 to 2022, there were movements to ban the novel due to being \"racist.\"</p>', 'top_nav', 'vertical_scroll', NULL, '{\"exhibit\":{\"introduction\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#FFFFFF\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\"},\"navigation\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#4A8580\",\"color\":\"#ffffff\",\"fontFamily\":\"Trebuchet MS\",\"fontSize\":\"\"},\"template\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#ffffff\",\"color\":\"#000000\",\"fontFamily\":\"Trebuchet MS\",\"fontSize\":\"16px\"},\"heading1\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#E9F3F2\"},\"heading2\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#4A8580\",\"color\":\"#ffffff\",\"fontFamily\":\"Trebuchet MS\",\"fontSize\":\"30px\"},\"heading3\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"\"},\"item1\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#E9F3F2\"},\"item2\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#4A8580\",\"color\":\"#ffffff\"},\"item3\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"\"}}}', 2, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0, NULL, 0, 0, 8, '2025-02-04 16:53:08', '2026-04-16 17:42:44', 'Madison Sussmann', 'Madison Sussmann'),
(6, '087b09e9-ab83-4599-bfe0-127d6fc459ee', 'exhibit', 'Hanya Holm: When Modern Dance Came to Colorado', '', 'banner_1', '', '', 'ca1f7829-e936-459d-9ca3-140320d2305b', '4ece15f4-f06c-4f84-b2d2-dd93753c998c', '087b09e9-ab83-4599-bfe0-127d6fc459ee_1739744690622_exhibit_hero.jpg', '087b09e9-ab83-4599-bfe0-127d6fc459ee_1739744700936_exhibit_thumbnail.jpg', '<p>Hanya Holm is popularly known for her innovative choreography in Broadway musicals like My Fair Lady and Kiss Me, Kate. She is also revered as one of the \"big four\" founders of modern dance, alongside Martha Graham, Doris Humphrey, and Charles Weidman.</p><p>Holm first came to Colorado in 1933 when she taught at the Perry Mansfield Dance Camp in Steamboat Springs. Beginning in 1941, she returned every summer for 43 years, making the state a destination for anyone who wanted to learn about modern dance.</p><p>Holm’s influence on dance in Colorado was extensive; she taught Freidann Parker and Lillian Covillo (co-founders of Colorado Ballet), Alfred Brooks and Maxine Munt (co-founders of the Changing Scene Theatre), Vera Sears (teacher and later director of the Children’s Dance Theatre at the University of Denver).</p><p>The exhibit features images of Holm throughout her career as a dancer, choreographer, and educator, as well as never-before-exhibited images of Holm in personal settings. The images in the exhibit are primarily from the collections of <a target=\"_blank\" href=\"https://specialcollections.du.edu/object/2c2c1422-a728-4a2a-9444-ceb0a11edf2d\">Maxine Munt</a> (a student of Holm) and <a target=\"_blank\" href=\"https://specialcollections.du.edu/object/2fbea091-2b7a-4456-b31d-8e5ed0d9bf46\">Marshall Brooks</a> (husband of one of Holm’s company members and close family friend).</p>', 'top_nav', 'vertical_scroll', '', '{\"exhibit\":{\"introduction\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#E6E6E6\",\"color\":\"#030303\",\"fontFamily\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"22px\"},\"navigation\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#ffffff\",\"color\":\"#030303\",\"fontFamily\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"24px\"},\"template\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#FFFFFF\",\"color\":\"#030303\",\"fontFamily\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"22px\"},\"heading1\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#F8EDDF\"},\"heading2\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#A82F88\",\"color\":\"#FFFFFF\",\"fontFamily\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"47px\"},\"heading3\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#CFDC5A\",\"color\":\"#FFFFFF\",\"fontFamily\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"47px\"},\"item1\":{\"fontSize\":\"26px\"},\"item2\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#E8DFEB\"},\"item3\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#F8FAE4\"}}}', 6, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, NULL, NULL, 0, 0, 8, '2025-02-16 17:29:22', '2026-04-16 17:42:44', 'Madison Sussmann', 'Jeff Rynhart'),

(9, '561837a9-2a43-4187-95d6-9376a48b9b85', 'exhibit', 'Latinx Poetry', 'The relationship between Nahuatl, Spanish, and English in the south-west region of the United States.', 'banner_1', 'This exhibit was curated by Mina Khadem, University of Denver Class of 2026, as part of her work in Honors Writing 1733, Section 5: &lt;i&gt;Stories from the Archives, 2024&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;. Mina developed this project while enrolled in Dr. Rob Gilmor’s course, which encourages students to engage deeply with primary sources and uncover untold stories through archival research. Her work reflects a thoughtful exploration of the materials and a commitment to bringing hidden histories to light through writing and interpretation.', '', 'dd8c690b-d865-4360-8763-7c90f25f7402', 'e0a3e489-bda0-4706-b24d-ee7fdd491914', '561837a9-2a43-4187-95d6-9376a48b9b85_1744319440983_exhibit_hero.jpg', '561837a9-2a43-4187-95d6-9376a48b9b85_1744319446020_exhibit_thumbnail.jpg', '<p>Latinx poetry has been greatly ignored throughout history, as Spanish (in reference to Spain) writings have overshadowed other Spanish and indigenous languages’ presence in art. This research project is intended to introduce Latinx poetry to DU through an academic perspective, demonstrate how Nahuatl, the language spoken by the Aztecs, Spanish, and English have evolved together overtime through prose, and acknowledge the lasting emotions felt by the people who were forced to assimilate to American culture as a result of historic border movements across the south-west region of the United States. Furthermore, this project attempts to explore Latinx poetry through the perspective of analyzing relationships within linguistics to demonstrate how the intermingling between languages can result in valuable art and narratives.</p>\n\n<p>Throughout my research, I have found that Latinx poetry that incorporates multiple languages also breaks the barriers of historical stigmatization that is connected to Latinx culture and attempts to reclaim pride in tradition and linguistics; this is symbolic as it breaks away from the lasting effects of European colonization. This theme is very apparent throughout many artifacts and poems featured in this exhibit. Furthermore, because of this complex relationship, Nahuatl–a rarely spoken indigenous language–has remained relevant in the south-west region of the U.S., specifically in New Mexico, California, and Colorado, as it has used Spanish as a bridge into English-dominated arts. As Tlapoyawa explains, there is no “pure” Spanish language as it has evolved through the adoption of loanwords from both Nahuatl and English (Tlapoyawa 2023, 4-6). Therefore, this movement and evolution that the Spanish language has undergone should be celebrated and respected regardless of its country, region, or culture of origin.</p>\n\n<p style=\"font-size: 80%;\">Cover image: Mondragón, Sergio, and Margaret Randall, eds. 1963. <i>El Corno Emplumado: The Plumed Horn, no. 5 (January).<i> \"Fresco Teotihuacano.\" Courtesy DU Special Collections and Archives. With a background in anthropology and museum studies, she focuses on making complex ideas accessible and visually compelling.&lt;&#x2F;p&gt;\n\n&lt;p&gt;She created Mars: Red Rocks to the Red Planet to highlight Colorado’s vital role in Mars exploration and to draw connections between the state’s iconic landscapes and the history of space science.&lt;&#x2F;p&gt;', '', 'af86998d-b588-4b8a-87a3-dddae9d619fd', '76dd02c7-2660-43e2-b134-167c0828e4e1', '1a37ef6b-863e-45b5-8fdf-37ec54654fc9_1752514267885_exhibit_hero.png', '1a37ef6b-863e-45b5-8fdf-37ec54654fc9_1752514272097_exhibit_thumbnail.png', '<p>Coloradans have been fascinated by our neighboring red planet, Mars since the 1890s. (14, '854efac7-10d5-4c27-a59e-e5b34b70efb9', 'exhibit', 'Photographic Printing Throughout History', '19th and 20th-century Photo Printing Methods.', 'banner_1', 'Curated by Kayla Stutz as part of the &lt;em&gt;Stories from the Archives&lt;&#x2F;em&gt; WRIT 1733 class taught by Dr. Rob Gilmor.&lt;br&gt; Kayla is pursuing majors in Art and Anthropology with minors in Art History and Business Administration and she will graduate in 2028 from the University of Denver. &lt;br&gt; This exhibit was made with the intent to leave the audience with a new or expanded understanding of historic photographic printing methods, as well as cultivate a respect for the intention and handcrafted nature involved in the printing process. It is so easy to access and print photos in today’s world that it becomes important to pause and consider the initial tangibility of photos at the time and build an appreciation for the convenience of today’s technology.', '', '4db68f1d-7c1e-415d-85c7-26d8a26f15cf', '33e1685f-ffbb-4668-8c27-ec3fca94a581', '854efac7-10d5-4c27-a59e-e5b34b70efb9_1756241233272_exhibit_hero.jpg', '854efac7-10d5-4c27-a59e-e5b34b70efb9_1756242289977_exhibit_thumbnail.jpg', '<p>From the invention of the first camera obscura in ancient times to the 1839 announcement of the official invention of photography in England and France, the ability to capture images started as a phenomenon that has now become heavily ingrained in how people, places, and the world is viewed, captured, and shared (Harry Ransom Center 2019). So how is it that, in today’s world, we can so easily translate an image from a screen in our hand to become a tangible print?</p>\n\n<p>The answer to this question lies in progressive historical changes and innovations in the world of photography. This exhibit particularly focuses on the evolution of photographic printing from the late 19th to early 20th centuries, which involved technical nuance and handcrafted processes that are comparably more elaborate than the techniques of the modern world. The invention of new photographic technologies rapidly changed in this era, leading to the creation—accidental or intentional—of different and complex printing methods and variations in photo quality (Mustalish 1997).</p>\n\n<p>Each artifact included in this exhibit is demonstrative of a broader, quickly developing history that reveals a collection of fascinating discoveries, ultimately built up to be photographic printing as we know it today.</p>', 'top_nav', 'vertical_scroll', NULL, '{\"exhibit\":{\"introduction\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#ffffff\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"Times New Roman\",\"fontSize\":\"\"},\"navigation\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#ffe9cf\",\"color\":\"#d4571e\",\"fontFamily\":\"Times New Roman\",\"fontSize\":\"\"},\"template\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#ffffff\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"Times New Roman\",\"fontSize\":\"\"},\"heading1\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#d4571e\",\"color\":\"#ffe9cf\",\"fontFamily\":\"Times New Roman\",\"fontSize\":\"\"},\"heading2\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"\",\"color\":\"#d4571e\",\"fontFamily\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\"},\"heading3\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"\"},\"item1\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"\"},\"item2\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"\"},\"item3\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"\"}}}', 28, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0, NULL, 0, 0, 11, '2025-07-30 12:42:58', '2026-04-16 17:42:44', 'Kayla Stutz', 'Kayla Stutz'),

The fight for the LGBTQ+ community didn’t start wholly from the Stonewall Riots, however, as nationwide student organizations first led the charge in advocating for visibility and gay liberation. They set the stage for the Stonewall Riots to spark a mass activist movement for LGBTQ+ rights and continued to rally for the community afterwards. (Beeym, 2003). This exhibit demonstrates some of the significance students had in the LGBTQ+ community at the University of Denver. It follows the development of the DU student organization now known as the Queer Student Alliance (QSA), plus the atmosphere of the general DU community surrounding the LGBTQ+ community. Core themes central to this exhibit include community-building, resistance in the face of animosity, and education – of what it is to be queer, of what struggles gay people had to experience, and so on. (17, '3b41d755-36ec-4045-be19-d8357114c418', 'exhibit', 'The Dawn of Sororities', 'How a National Movement Made its Way to the University of Denver', 'banner_1', '', '', '9da2e21a-064e-4af1-bfc4-58460fabccab', '54970d12-3cca-4583-8393-3de45bb6fd95', '3b41d755-36ec-4045-be19-d8357114c418_1756230522373_exhibit_hero.png', '3b41d755-36ec-4045-be19-d8357114c418_1756229604521_exhibit_thumbnail.png', '<p>The turn of the twentieth century saw a new arrival and fight for women\'s rights. After the Civil War, women saw their roles begin to change and expand, as many were left in charge of the household and businesses, after their husbands and fathers went off to war. This public shift in standard gender roles provided women with the empowerment to heavily seek equal opportunities (Women’s Suffrage; McLaren 15). From 1870 to 1920, the Progressive Era brought forth a period of social reform that boosted the Women’s Suffrage movement and gave many women the courage to seek higher education.</p>\n\n<p>On college campuses, fraternities allowed men to elevate themselves on the social totem pole. As a way to provide academic support and level the collegiate playing field between men and women, women’s fraternities and sororities were created. The University of Denver was no stranger to this, and its women’s Greek Life evolved alongside the rest of the country. The National Panhellenic Conference (NPC) planted roots here, recognizing and uniting the Greek women of DU with sororities nationwide. Sororities were also allowed to have their own homes on and near campus, a concept widely encouraged by NPC but scarcely given by universities. DU Sororities were active with the University, contributing to its student life, involvement, and academic success.</p>', 'top_nav', 'vertical_scroll', NULL, '{\"exhibit\":{\"introduction\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#2566b1\",\"color\":\"#ffffff\",\"fontFamily\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"24px\"},\"navigation\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#01579b\",\"color\":\"#ffffff\",\"fontFamily\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\"},\"template\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\"},\"heading1\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"#01579b\",\"color\":\"#ffffff\",\"fontFamily\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\"},\"heading2\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"\"},\"heading3\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"\"},\"item1\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"\"},\"item2\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"\"},\"item3\":{\"backgroundColor\":\"\",\"color\":\"\",\"fontSize\":\"\",\"fontFamily\":\"\"}}}', 31, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0, NULL, 0, 0, 8, '2025-08-26 13:50:58', '2026-04-16 17:42:44', 'Madison Sussmann', NULL),

(20, '6196b295-9690-4242-b845-9619454ee74e', 'exhibit', '12 Books, 12 Cases', 'Precedents for the Freedom to Read', 'banner_1', 'This exhibit was curated by Madison Sussmann, Exhibits Librarian.', '', '0dbe3bb8-ffb4-4701-bd91-b63c80f3f4fd', 'a37422bb-9fa1-4d46-9dbf-0cd8402ef0d7', '6196b295-9690-4242-b845-9619454ee74e_1757540491533_exhibit_hero.jpg', '6196b295-9690-4242-b845-9619454ee74e_1757540497436_exhibit_thumbnail.jpg', '<p>Book challenges and bans in the United States are usually local events, whether in a school district, a library board, or a single community deciding whether readers may access a text. But each removal or reinstatement takes place within a much larger legal framework. Over the past century, federal courts have set precedents that define what counts as obscenity, what qualifies as “serious literary value,” and how far schools and libraries may go in restricting access.</p>\n\n<p>This exhibit presents twelve books that illustrate twelve landmark legal cases. From <em>Ulysses</em> in 1933 to <em>1984</em> in the 2020s, these cases show how literature has been tested in the courts and how the law has repeatedly stepped in to protect the freedom to read.</p>\n\n<p></p><p>By pairing each book with its case, visitors will see how precedent builds upon precedent. During Kristallnacht (November 9-10, 1938), Georg, along with many other Jewish men, was taken to the Oranienburg Concentration Camp. Georg was wearing slippers, night clothes, and a robe when they came for him, yet the Nazis would not permit him to change or take a coat. When Georg was released in February 1939, his fingers and toes were badly frostbitten, and he was a broken man.</p>\r\n<p>During the Kristallnacht terror, assuming that the Nazis would not return a second time after taking Georg, the Loewensteins and distant family members hid in Uncle Georg’s apartment for a few weeks.</p>', 1, '', 'item', 'media_bottom', 100, 1, NULL, 0, 1, NULL, NULL, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, NULL, 1, 8, '2025-01-14 18:05:48', '2026-04-16 17:42:44', 'Madison Sussmann', NULL),
(21, '59057b2b-c485-41c4-92f5-f26e1ccd2049', 'f60d30a4-2b58-4ef7-9376-93e10f8d7689', 'bea561ce-5746-4adb-b7d2-b9f997fbcd37', NULL, '', NULL, '', 'Letter from Georg and Alice Loewenstein thanking Marie (Maria) Loewenstein for a package. Sent from the Łodź Ghetto, dated February 16, 1942.', 'image', 'image&#x2F;jpeg', '59057b2b-c485-41c4-92f5-f26e1ccd2049_1736896046955_item_media.jpg', '3f75e2fe-4fc4-425e-9f62-0c0278964c9c', '<p>On October 24, 1941, the Nazis again came for Uncle Georg. This time his wife Alice was taken as well and both were transported to the Łódź Ghetto, the second largest ghetto in German-occupied Poland. Maria was able to mail some food and supplies to them, but communication with those in ghettos and concentration camps was severely limited. Maria received one pre-printed note from Georg and Alice, dated February 16, 1942, thanking her for a package. It would be the last the family heard from them. According to Łódź Ghetto census records compiled by Yad Vashem, Israel\'s memorial to victims of the Holocaust, Georg Loewenstein perished in the Łódź Ghetto, most likely from starvation. Alice Loewenstein was transported to the Chelmno Death Camp on May 15, 1942 where she was killed.</p>', 1, '<p>On October 24, 1941, the Nazis again came for Uncle Georg. This time his wife Alice was taken as well and both were transported to the Łódź Ghetto, the second largest ghetto in German-occupied Poland. Maria was able to mail some food and supplies to them, but communication with those in ghettos and concentration camps was severely limited. Maria received one pre-printed note from Georg and Alice, dated February 16, 1942, thanking her for a package. It would be the last the family heard from them. According to Łódź Ghetto census records compiled by Yad Vashem, Israel\'s memorial to victims of the Holocaust, Georg Loewenstein perished in the Łódź Ghetto, most likely from starvation. Within two years of Hitler’s invasion of Poland, she and her family were forced into the Zwoleń ghetto. Her family was broken up and Esther was deported to the Skarzysko-Kamienna concentration camp. During her time at the camp she experienced forced labor in a munitions factory, seven days a week, making bullets for the Germans. She was transferred from Skarzysko-Kamienna to the Tschenstochau concentration camp after a year, and was again forced into labor at a munitions factory.</p>\n\n<p>Following her time at Tschenstochau, Esther was moved to Ravensbrück, Burgau, and finally Türkheim concentration camp. In the last days of the war she was forced to march with the rest of Türkheim’s prisoners until the SS guards told them the war was over and to go wherever they wanted. She returned to the village of Türkheim where she was taken in by a German family for a few days before being liberated by the American armed forces.</p>\n\n<p>Shortly after the end of the war in 1945, Esther found and married her husband, Max Wasserman, in Germany. She made her way to America through Brooklyn, New York and was naturalized there on August 5, 1952. By the mid-1980’s Max and Esther had made their home in the Los Angeles metropolitan area. Esther Wasserman was the subject for one of Debra Howard\'s portraits of child holocaust survivors.</p>', 1, '<p>Esther Wasserman (1921- ) was born Esther Ajlman in Zwoleń, Poland on January 15, 1921. (139, '66f6ce77-5b04-45b8-aaa9-78fcb6474930', '97c24663-1480-4ec1-9035-872062a7ecfd', 'a36ddb1f-2141-4e7a-a2d5-e5241d39d4b6', NULL, '', NULL, 'Nathalie Gonenn', 'Conte and graphite on paper, 2005', 'image', 'image&#x2F;jpeg', '66f6ce77-5b04-45b8-aaa9-78fcb6474930_1743447235965_item_media.jpg', '96befe70-ee31-42ce-97b1-d33023ef21f7', '<p>Natalie Gonenn (1936- )was born Natalie Rendler in Chelm, Poland—a small town near the Ukranian border—on May 15, 1936. She was three years old when Hitler invaded Poland. Her time during the Holocaust was dominated by hiding and running. For the first month or so, she and her family hid in barns, however Nazi soldiers were patrolling the area and threatening farmers with death if they hid Jews. Luckily, one farmer dug a small bunker in the middle of a field for her and her family where they lived for several months. The farmer became scared for his family and insisted that her family leave and try to cross the river to the Ukraine. One night, they all ran to the river, got in a small boat, and attempted to cross. The Germans discovered them and began shooting, which prompted Russian troops to begin shooting from the other side of the river. The boat capsized and fortunately they were pulled out of the river on the Ukranian side.</p>\n\n<p>As the Germans advanced, their family was continually on the run. Eventually they were taken in by a Rabbi in Kiev, where they lived for a couple months. Her family fled as the Germans advanced and bombed the city. They were moved via cattle cars and experienced bombing for much of their journey deeper into Russia. They were asked to accept Russian citizenship, which her father rejected and her family was sent to a camp in Siberia. Living conditions were so bad that soon after, her father accepted Russian citizenship and her family returned to a town outside of Moscow. As the Germans advanced, her family was forced to run again and ended up in Uzbekistan. Natalie’s family spent approximately two years in Uzbekistan where her mother got sick and gave her and her brother to an orphanage. Natalie lived out the rest of the war in the orphanage and what she refers to as a sanatorium.</p>\n\n<p>After the war, her family returned to Chelm, but it was still unsafe for Jews. As such, her family left and spent time at three displaced persons camps: Stuttgart Flandern Kaserne, Berlin, and Ulm. Her family ended up moving to Palestine by way of a ship called the Galila. They arrived in 1948 and she spent eleven years there. She served in the Israeli military and met her husband, Max Gonenn, while at university. They were married in Haifa in 1959 and shortly after moved to New York where they settled and started a family.</p>', 1, '<p>Natalie Gonenn (1936- )was born Natalie Rendler in Chelm, Poland—a small town near the Ukranian border—on May 15, 1936. She was three years old when Hitler invaded Poland. Her time during the Holocaust was dominated by hiding and running. For the first month or so, she and her family hid in barns, however Nazi soldiers were patrolling the area and threatening farmers with death if they hid Jews. (140, '3914365e-e84d-4838-b969-952e49891d37', '97c24663-1480-4ec1-9035-872062a7ecfd', 'a36ddb1f-2141-4e7a-a2d5-e5241d39d4b6', NULL, '', NULL, 'Steven Metzler', 'Conte on paper, 2004', 'image', 'image&#x2F;jpeg', '3914365e-e84d-4838-b969-952e49891d37_1743447361689_item_media.jpg', '6554e993-71cc-4143-a54a-d97bfc3f3e5b', '<p>Steve (Steven) Metzler (1925- ) was born on June 10, 1925 in Deda, Romania. He grew up in a Hasidic family and experienced extreme antisemitism prior to the Holocaust including being beaten and called “Christ killer” in the first grade. When the German army arrived in his town, his family were made to house two soldiers, one of whom offered to help them escape. Steve’s father rejected the offer and within a month, all of the Jews in the area were forced into the Oradea ghetto. Shortly after arriving at the ghetto in May of 1944, Steve and his family were brought to the trains on June 1.</p>\n\n<p>Their train ride spanned four to six days and some of the elderly individuals on the train realized it was heading to Auschwitz based on the towns they rode through. The train arrived at Auschwitz-Birkenau on June 6 where everyone deboarded and were immediately forced into a selection line where Steve saw his mother for the last time as they were separated and she was ushered toward the crematorium. After selection, Steve, along with many others, were told to strip naked, had all of their hair cut off, were sprayed with disinfectant, tattooed with their prisoner number, and were marched to their bunks. Steve was selected to work with a roof repair group, which provided him the ability to observe the extent of operations at the camps. During his time at Auschwitz-Birkenau, Steve witnessed many atrocities first hand. He was forced to watch hangings of other prisoners in the evenings and saw German Shepherds being set upon other prisoners. In August of 1944, during another selection, Steve knew that he was not well enough to pass and jumped out of a second story window to hide in another bunk room while selection occurred. Near the end of 1944, Steve and his work group were driven to the Birkenau crematorium. Upon arrival they were informed that they would not be getting gassed and burned, but instead were there to drill holes for dynamite to destroy the building. Steve, along with the prisoners of Auschwitz-Birkenau were evacuated on January 15, 1945 and began a death march.</p>\n\n<p>He marched for two to three months before his group arrived at a makeshift concentration camp in a large field deep in Germany. They began the march with 16,000 people and arrived with 300. His group was forced to dig ditches around a German airfield, but he knew that for his health he would have to escape before the next march. One night, he crawled under the fence to a river, crossed several irrigation ditches, and found an abandoned barn where he waited for eight days before being liberated by the United States Armed Forces.</p>\n\n<p>After liberation, he had dysentery and was brought to a hospital where he was also confirmed to have tuberculosis. He spent two years at a sanatorium in Haunstein, Germany. In 1950, Steve arrived in America and subsequently settled in Denver, Colorado. He worked in electronics repair and construction before retiring and getting into the art business. He has a daughter and two grandchildren.</p>', 1, '<p>Steve (Steven) Metzler (1925- ) was born on June 10, 1925 in Deda, Romania. He grew up in a Hasidic family and experienced extreme antisemitism prior to the Holocaust including being beaten and called “Christ killer” in the first grade. When the German army arrived in his town, his family were made to house two soldiers, one of whom offered to help them escape. Steve’s father rejected the offer and within a month, all of the Jews in the area were forced into the Oradea ghetto. Shortly after arriving at the ghetto in May of 1944, Steve and his family were brought to the trains on June 1.</p>\n\n<p>Their train ride spanned four to six days and some of the elderly individuals on the train realized it was heading to Auschwitz based on the towns they rode through. The train arrived at Auschwitz-Birkenau on June 6 where everyone deboarded and were immediately forced into a selection line where Steve saw his mother for the last time as they were separated and she was ushered toward the crematorium. After selection, Steve, along with many others, were told to strip naked, had all of their hair cut off, were sprayed with disinfectant, tattooed with their prisoner number, and were marched to their bunks. Steve was selected to work with a roof repair group, which provided him the ability to observe the extent of operations at the camps. During his time at Auschwitz-Birkenau, Steve witnessed many atrocities first hand. He was forced to watch hangings of other prisoners in the evenings and saw German Shepherds being set upon other prisoners. In August of 1944, during another selection, Steve knew that he was not well enough to pass and jumped out of a second story window to hide in another bunk room while selection occurred. Near the end of 1944, Steve and his work group were driven to the Birkenau crematorium. Upon arrival they were informed that they would not be getting gassed and burned, but instead were there to drill holes for dynamite to destroy the building. Steve, along with the prisoners of Auschwitz-Birkenau were evacuated on January 15, 1945 and began a death march.</p>\n\n<p>He marched for two to three months before his group arrived at a makeshift concentration camp in a large field deep in Germany. They began the march with 16,000 people and arrived with 300. His group was forced to dig ditches around a German airfield, but he knew that for his health he would have to escape before the next march. One night, he crawled under the fence to a river, crossed several irrigation ditches, and found an abandoned barn where he waited for eight days before being liberated by the United States Armed Forces.</p>\n\n<p>After liberation, he had dysentery and was brought to a hospital where he was also confirmed to have tuberculosis. He spent two years at a sanatorium in Haunstein, Germany. In 1950, Steve arrived in America and subsequently settled in Denver, Colorado. He worked in electronics repair and construction before retiring and getting into the art business. He has a daughter and two grandchildren.</p>', 'item', 'media_only', 100, 1, 'A drawn portrait of an older man with a white shirt looking forward. (141, '1f6e55d7-6fa1-4778-b203-a3bf8f412fc1', '97c24663-1480-4ec1-9035-872062a7ecfd', 'a36ddb1f-2141-4e7a-a2d5-e5241d39d4b6', NULL, '', NULL, 'Jack Welner', 'Watercolor on paper, 2004', 'image', 'image&#x2F;jpeg', '1f6e55d7-6fa1-4778-b203-a3bf8f412fc1_1743447458730_item_media.jpg', 'da3956a0-a4c8-4fc1-8e5f-4cae0eb46403', '<p>Jack Welner (1920- 2019) was born Jacob Nuta Welniarz in Łódź, Poland on September 10, 1920. Jack was forced into the Łódź ghetto in the mid-late 1930s, prior to his internment at four concentration camps. During the Holocaust Jack was a prisoner at Auschwitz, Dachau, Kaufering Lager IV, and Kaufering Lager X. The exact chronology of his time at each camp is unknown, however Dachau Concentration Camp records show he was transferred from Auschwitz to Dachau on September 1, 1944. In April 1945, the remaining prisoners at Dachau were led on a death march. The SS guards leading them fled and later that day the Americans found them.</p>\n\n<p>He was liberated by the United States armed forces in Waakirchen, Germany. Jack arrived in the United States through the Port of New York on the USAT Gen. J. H. McRae on February 21, 1950 and subsequently made his way to Denver, Colorado. He was naturalized as an American citizen on July 13, 1950. Jack’s uncle, Abe Winograd, assisted Jack and his brother in their emigration to Colorado. Jack lived out the rest of his life in the Denver metropolitan area where he found his soulmate and had several children. He passed away on August 28, 2019.</p>', 1, '<p>Jack Welner (1920- 2019) was born Jacob Nuta Welniarz in Łódź, Poland on September 10, 1920. Jack was forced into the Łódź ghetto in the mid-late 1930s, prior to his internment at four concentration camps. During the Holocaust Jack was a prisoner at Auschwitz, Dachau, Kaufering Lager IV, and Kaufering Lager X. The exact chronology of his time at each camp is unknown, however Dachau Concentration Camp records show he was transferred from Auschwitz to Dachau on September 1, 1944. In April 1945, the remaining prisoners at Dachau were led on a death march. The SS guards leading them fled and later that day the Americans found them.</p>\n\n<p>He was liberated by the United States armed forces in Waakirchen, Germany. Jack arrived in the United States through the Port of New York on the USAT Gen. J. H. McRae on February 21, 1950 and subsequently made his way to Denver, Colorado. He was naturalized as an American citizen on July 13, 1950. Jack’s uncle, Abe Winograd, assisted Jack and his brother in their emigration to Colorado. Jack lived out the rest of his life in the Denver metropolitan area where he found his soulmate and had several children. He passed away on August 28, 2019.</p>', 'item', 'media_only', 100, 1, 'A drawn portrait of an older man with a grey muscle and hair. At four and a half, she and her family experienced the bombardment of Warsaw by the Germans. Shortly after the bombings, her family decided to move to eastern Poland and settle in Lida which was occupied by the Russians at that point. In 1941, Germans attacked the Soviet Union and Lida, gaining control of the city. Her family remained, and in December of 1941, Miriam and her family were forced into the Lida ghetto.</p>\n\n<p>On May 8, 1942 Germans and nazi-sympathizers rounded up all of the Jews in the ghetto, and brought them to a checkpoint where they were sent left or right. Miriam’s family was sent to the right, which turned out to be a mass grave where troops were executing Jews. Approximately 80 percent of the Jews in the Lida ghetto were murdered that day. Luckily, her father was an accomplished surgeon and was able to negotiate their escape from that fate. At that point, her parents sent her to hide with a non-Jewish family just outside of Lida, fearing that the Germans were going to come to the Ghetto and exterminate all the Jewish children. When that turned out not to be true, Miriam returned to the ghetto, but not for long.</p>\n\n<p>In November, 1942 her family left the ghetto to join the partisan fighters in the Lipiczany Forest. Miriam spent two years with her family in the forest. Her parents cut her hair and dressed her as a boy as rape was commonplace among the partisans, defectors, and others living in the area. In the early summer of 1944, the Russians liberated the partisans and individuals living in the forest. The Russians assigned them a new place to live--Szczuczyn, a Belorussian town. However, her family did not want to live under communist rule, so they escaped to Lublin, Poland. From there, her family moved to Czechoslovakia, Hungary, Romania, back to Hungary, Austria, Italy, and finally a displaced persons camp called Monte Mario in Rome. After a couple months at the displaced persons camp, they acquired visas to go to America. They left from Naples and traveled by ship to New York where they arrived February 23, 1947.</p>\n\n<p>While she had a difficult time acclimating to America, Miriam was quite successful in her life. She received her bachelors in Biology and Chemistry from NYU, where she also met her husband Henry Brysk. While she was still allowed at ballet school at the age of four, she was told that she would not be able to perform as Jews were no longer allowed to participate in public events. Her dance teacher decided that if one child could not dance, there would be no performance. As such, she was allowed to perform, but remembers restrictions on Jews gradually becoming more strict. Jews were forced to wear yellow stars beginning in 1944. Her father was forced to work in a Hungarian labor camp known as Munkacs during much of her childhood. During the early 1940s, Susan’s mother continually planned for Germany’s invasion of Hungary. She acquired Christian identification papers and sent Susan and her brother to catechism classes to learn about Christianity in case they were ever questioned.</p>\n\n<p>When the Germans arrived, her family left for their home in the countryside. They remained for around a month before hearing the Germans were targeting Jews in the countryside and subsequently returned to their apartment in Budapest. Their apartment had been designated a Jewish house so they were not required to live in the ghetto. Her mother invited several other Jews to live with them. Shortly after the German invasion, her mother was forced to work as a seamstress for a German uniform factory. While there, she befriended the German officer in charge who agreed to take her family and several other people and hide them in the cellar of his apartment building. Wuchta, the German officer, hid them for nine months with the help of his aide who would periodically check in on them. They lived on sauerkraut and horsemeat. While in the cellar the apartment building was bombed, however they survived. Her family and others in the cellar were liberated on January 18, 1945 by the Russians, when Susan was nearly eight.</p>\n\n<p>After returning to their old apartment building they found it had also been bombed, and their home destroyed. By the time she finished fourth grade, the communists had taken over and her parents felt they needed to leave to stay safe. They acquired a forged passport and made their way to Vienna, Austria. Her family spent two years in Vienna, subletting a variety of apartments. When the Korean War began, her father was concerned it was the beginning of World War 3 and arranged for escape to Canada in 1951. They settled in Montreal where Susan eventually met and married her husband, Edgar Bendor, in 1959. She and her husband moved to New York in 1960 where Susan became a social worker, always remembering the kindness she had received from strangers during her time in Budapest. She received her masters degree from Adelphi University, and PhD from Hunter City University.</p>', 'item', 'media_only', 100, 1, 'A black and white drawn portrait of an older woman from the neck up. Her parents had recently moved to Amsterdam from Germany to escape persecution. The Germans arrived in Amsterdam when Suzanne was four and a half. They lived under increasingly restrictive conditions until the Germans took them in the beginning of 1943. Her family, along with many other Jews, were taken to a train station and waited for hours before they were put onto a train. The train took them to Westerbork concentration camp where they spent five to six months before being transported to Bergen-Belsen.</p>\n\n<p>Her family arrived at Bergen-Belson in the late spring of 1943. At that point the camp was relatively empty, but it filled up quickly as new prisoners arrived daily. Suzanne and her family spent one and a half years in Bergen-Belson. Hunger was a major facet of her life in the camp and many of her memories are related to acquiring food and water for her family. While at Bergen-Belson she lived in constant fear and concern for her own life and the lives of her family. The night before the camp was evacuated by the Germans, the prisoners were told to sit near the front gate of the camp and wait for transport. She ended up sitting with her father who at that point had become weak and sick. She woke in the morning to find he had passed during the night, but he was thrown onto the transport truck with her, her brother, and her mother and they made their way to the trains. Suzanne and her family spent approximately two weeks on the train prior to being liberated by Russian forces. During her time on the train, she, her family, and many others had contracted typhus and were treated upon arrival at a small German village. Suzanne got better before her mother and brother, and lived alone and with other orphans for some time before her family got better.</p>\n\n<p>After approximately four weeks in the small village, she and her family returned to Holland. However, there was a food shortage in Holland, so she and her brother were sent to Copenhagen to live. After some time, they returned to Amsterdam, but only for a year. His family was the only Jewish family in Vârciorog. They were prompted to move to Oradea in 1935 after a priest moved to Vârciorog who preached antisemitism and told the villagers not to shop at his family\'s general store. As restrictions for Jews became more stringent, life became more difficult, and in 1944 his family was forced into the Oradea ghetto.</p>\n\n<p>They lived in the ghetto for three to four weeks before being transported to Auschwitz-Birkenau. Upon their nighttime arrival at Auschwitz, they were made to line up. The officer who was sending Jews right or left was Josef Mengele who was also placing children from 13-18 into groups for future experimentation. Luckily, Imre had recently turned 18 and was sent to the right for labor. He spent only three days at Auschwitz before being transported to Mauthausen where he also only stayed for three days. From Mauthausen, Imre was transported to Ebensee where he was put to work building tunnels and petroleum refineries in the adjacent mountains. The camp was built for 8000, but by the end of the war there were 23,000 prisoners at Ebensee.</p>\n\n<p>As Ebensee was deep in the Austrian Alps, liberation for the prisoners came later than many of the camps. Imre was liberated by American troops who provided all of the prisoners with a very rich goulash. As he and many of the prisoners were extremely malnourished, the soup made him very sick and he slipped into a coma for two weeks. After coming out of his coma, Imre set out to find members of his family—traveling from Austria to Romania, then Vienna, and then to the Belsen Displaced Persons camp.</p>\n\n<p>Imre, along with a friend, secured transport to the United States through the American consulate and arrived in New York in the late 1940s. Imre had an aunt, uncle, and cousin in New York who he lived with for a few years prior to being drafted into the American Army during the Korean War. He served as a driver for an officer and turned down the opportunity for a permanent job in the Army in order to return to the US. After returning, Imre attended the Fashion Institute of Technology and worked his way to becoming a designer in the fashion industry. He lived in the Bronx and Manhattan.</p>', 1, '<p>Imre Frankovits (Frankovitz, 1926-2010) was born Emerck Francozicci on May 8, 1926 in Vârciorog, Romania. (146, '670bc17d-5d1e-4e2d-a41f-d8c55f8006ca', '97c24663-1480-4ec1-9035-872062a7ecfd', 'a36ddb1f-2141-4e7a-a2d5-e5241d39d4b6', NULL, '', NULL, 'Ava Kadishson Schieber', 'Colored pencil and watercolor on paper, 2005', 'image', 'image&#x2F;jpeg', '670bc17d-5d1e-4e2d-a41f-d8c55f8006ca_1743449564273_item_media.jpg', 'bc3badb0-9edd-4e3a-882e-3ec4d401421e', '<p>Ava Kadishson Schieber was born Ava Hegedis on April 12, 1926 in Novi Sad, Yugoslavia. Her family was well off and also very cultured. During the 1930s she was aware of the impending war both from the news and also witnessing ships of Jews being deported down the Danube. She had heard of Kristallnacht and knew of Jewish persecution happening elsewhere. Ava was taught by her parents to be prepared for a war that would likely come. Around 1940, her family moved from Novi Sad to Belgrade as they felt it would be better for their anonymity to live in a larger city. Ava remained in Novi Sad for one year to finish her schooling while she lived with her grandmother. She then left to be with her family in Belgrade. On April 6, 1941 the Nazis invaded Yugoslavia and bombarded Belgrade for several days.</p>\n\n<p>On April 12, her birthday, the Nazis entered the city, and put up posters instructing all of the Jews to register with the Germans. Her family knew that this was not a good choice and decided to figure out how to hide. Her father left to hide in Hungary. Ava’s mother used her birth certificate to acquire ID papers as she had converted prior to marriage and her birth certificate said she was Christian. Her sister married a Greek Orthodox man and the priest predated the marriage certificate prior to the war to avoid any suspicion. Ava was the only one who could not get ID papers, so she went to hide at a farm where her father had made arrangements for her. Ava spent four years on the farm, taking on the identity of a farmhand. She did chores and would sneak back to Belgrade to visit her mother and sister when possible. During her time at the farm, she received very little food. On one of her trips back to Belgrade, her mother asked her to get rations. While waiting in line, a German soldier passed by her, and since she was not perfectly aligned with the rest of the line, hit her in the mouth with the butt of his gun, breaking her front teeth. Ava remembers that in the winter of 1942, the Jews of Belgrade were rounded up and drowned under the ice of the Danube. Ava is an artist and had been prior to the war.</p>\n\n<p>During her time on the farm, she completed a few drawings which are at the United States Holocaust Memorial in Washington, DC. Near the end of the war, she decided to return to Belgrade. She found that her sister had died as a result of being taken to war. Ava went looking for her father and grandmother in Novi Sad, however when she arrived she was informed that she was the first Jew to return. A former neighbor told her that her father and grandmother were taken to Auschwitz where they both died. Her father played in the Auschwitz orchestra that performed as people were marched to the gas chambers. She returned to Belgrade with a few belongings from her former life and began to look for a job. She ended up going to the Art Academy in Belgrade and working as a painter and animator for movie studios.</p>\n\n<p>In 1949, Ava and her mother left Yugoslavia for Israel. They arrived at the port of Haifa and quickly made their way south to Tel Aviv. She was immediately hired by a movie studio to do a similar job as she had done while in Belgrade. The man who hired her became her first husband—Yitzhak Kadishson. They were married for thirty years prior to his passing. About a decade later, a man named Nathan Schieber bought some of her art in America. They met several months after and fell in love. She decided to leave Israel and move to the Chicago area. She continued to paint, heavily influenced by her time hiding at the farm.</p>', 1, '<p>Ava Kadishson Schieber was born Ava Hegedis on April 12, 1926 in Novi Sad, Yugoslavia. Photo credit: JPL/NASA', 'image', 'image&#x2F;jpeg', '3b3fc26e-a8dd-46a5-8d25-abc2c0206f64_1752519939283_item_media.jpg', '15945d49-6589-44e4-b09a-5c18e7af7ac8', '', 1, 'These wide-angle images of Mars were overlaid onto a globe that originally featured only vague, Earth-based views of the planet, dramatically enhancing the clarity and detail of the Martian surface. Images from Mariner 6 appear in two horizontal rows near the top of the globe, while Mariner 7’s images extend from the center downward to the bottom right, including the south polar cap. The Visual Imaging Investigation, or TV experiment, aboard Mariner 6 and 7 used two cameras on each spacecraft to achieve both broad coverage and high-resolution detail. Camera A, with a wide-angle lens, captured large areas measuring 1,000 by 1,000 kilometers and could resolve features as small as 3,000 meters during close encounters. Camera B, equipped with a telephoto lens, focused on smaller 100 by 100 kilometer regions and resolved details down to 300 meters. The cameras operated alternately, with each one taking a photograph every 84 seconds, resulting in a rich and layered visual record of Mars during these historic flybys.', 'item', 'media_only', 100, 1, 'Digitally created image to demonstrate how the photos taken by Mariners 6 and 7 were pieced together to make one composite photo of Mars.', 0, 1, NULL, NULL, 2, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, NULL, 0, 8, '2025-07-14 15:06:43', '2026-04-16 17:42:44', 'Madison Sussmann', NULL),

Smith was a pioneering planetary astronomer who played a central role in shaping NASA\'s early Mars exploration efforts. He served on the imaging teams for the Mariner 6 and 7 missions, which conducted some of the first successful flybys of Mars in 1969 and returned the earliest close-up images of the planet’s surface. His involvement in these missions helped lay the foundation for scientific imaging in planetary exploration. Smith’s work was deeply connected to Colorado’s scientific community through his collaborations with researchers and institutions contributing to planetary imaging and mission planning. Photo credit: New Mexico State University.', 'video', '', '1_j4x6lqo4', 'cc04e852-43af-4c26-9be5-c669e2b7f01b', '', 1, '<p><strong>Transcript</strong><p>\n\n<p>\"Four, perhaps 64. Yeah, I think it was 1964. Bob Layton came to the lab to visit, with Clyde, and with me because they had a spacecraft headed toward Mars. They had a team that was to do an analysis of the Mars images and realized that they had nobody on their team that knew anything about Mars at all. That was the way things were done in those days. NASA essentially picked teams for their expertise in instrumentation, and so Leighton and Murray and other members of that group, including some geologists, were involved, but nobody who had any experience in looking at Mars from the Earth. And, Leighton came out to talk with us. And, he became very interested in the cumulative record that we had for Mars at that time in our photographic record and in Clyde\'s drawings. And, it was that point that we and the Mariner team and NASA itself began to realize the extreme importance of good ground-based records in tying together the very brief close-up views that a planetary probe is able to accomplish. And then tying that in what can be observed from the ground. By having essentially close-up views that you could then compare with the ground-based observations that were made at that same moment, then one might be able to infer things from the past and into the future.\"</p>', 'item', 'media_only', 100, 1, NULL, 0, 1, NULL, NULL, 3, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, NULL, 0, 8, '2025-07-16 15:22:46', '2026-04-16 17:42:44', 'Madison Sussmann', 'Madison Sussmann'),

Their primary mission was to conduct close flybys of the planet and collect data on its atmosphere, surface, and physical properties in preparation for future missions. It entered orbit around Mars on November 14, 1971, during a massive global dust storm. Once the atmosphere cleared, Mariner 9 mapped over 80% of the Martian surface, revealing unprecedented details of its geology. The mission discovered vast canyons (including Valles Marineris), extinct volcanoes (like Olympus Mons), dried riverbeds, and signs of past water activity. Mariner 9 also provided the first close-up views of Mars’ moons, Phobos and Deimos. Photo credit: The New York Times.', 'video', '', '1_oidma8om', 'c264194f-c493-46f7-a007-7cdcfcba000c', '', 1, '<p><strong>Transcript</strong></p>\n\n<p>\"By the time I had worked on both Mariners 6 and 7, and when Mariner 8 and 9 were launched, Mariner 8 ended up in the Indian Ocean somewhere, but Mariner 9 did make it to Mars. While on its way to Mars, our ground-based program discovered the outbreak of an enormous dust storm on Mars, which we realized would be planet-wide by the time Mariner 9 reached there. That was first seen by us, and then the Lowell group confirmed it. But we discovered it. And here again, this is where Clyde\'s much longer visual observing experience really came in. Clyde had seen a number of these things happen over the years, and when he saw the intensity of this storm—which I think began somewhere in the Hellas region, if I recall; I’m not sure of that—but when he saw what we had just discovered, he said, “This is gonna be a really bad one.” And it was a really bad one. The Russians, the Soviets, arrived at roughly the same time we did, and they unfortunately weren’t prepared to spend a lot of time around Mars waiting it out. And so, their mission produced very little. Fortunately, Mariner 9 was able to wait out the dust storm, and we had a very successful mission. But that again illustrates the complementarity between the space program and a good ground-based planetary program. All of that reinforced NASA’s interest in this kind of work and kept the program going for many years.\"</p>', 'item', 'media_only', 100, 1, NULL, 0, 1, NULL, NULL, 6, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, NULL, 0, 8, '2025-07-16 15:53:37', '2026-04-16 17:42:44', 'Madison Sussmann', NULL),

It captures images at five times the resolution of those from the earlier Mars Global Surveyor, enabling researchers to study the Martian surface in unprecedented detail and aiding in the selection of landing sites for both robotic and future human missions.&lt;&#x2F;p&gt;', '{}', 69, 0, 0, '2025-07-14 23:04:10', '2026-04-17 18:53:52', 8, 'Madison Sussmann', 'Madison Sussmann'),
