Among them, &lt;strong&gt;Mariners 3–9&lt;&#x2F;strong&gt; transformed our understanding of Mars. In 1965, &lt;strong&gt;Mariner 4&lt;&#x2F;strong&gt; captured the first close-up images of another planet, revealing a cratered, moon-like surface. &lt;strong&gt;Mariners 6 and 7&lt;&#x2F;strong&gt; followed in 1969 with detailed flybys, and in 1971, &lt;strong&gt;Mariner 9&lt;&#x2F;strong&gt; became the first spacecraft to orbit another planet. (94, '9ca4f505-5465-4cf8-8695-83d2a4998e1b', 'd5b80640-f03f-4000-8e3a-33cc141c23f7_1738705815736_item_media.jpg', 'The first known botanical illustration dates to around 70 CE, when the Greek botanist Pedanius Dioscorides created drawings for De Materia Medica to help readers identify medicinal plants. As printing technology advanced to include color, botanical illustration expanded in both scope and popularity, and by the eighteenth century, amid growing interest in botany, the field became increasingly professionalized. Although few women botanical illustrators or natural scientists of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries were widely recognized or published under their own names, women were nonetheless deeply involved in the natural sciences as independent illustrators and often uncredited artists. Maria Sibylla Merian and Anna Botsford Comstock, featured in this exhibit, were among those who broke convention and achieved significant recognition for their work. Over time, botanical illustration evolved to more widely include women and to embrace new styles and media. Even in the age of high definition photography, illustration remains vital for scientific study because artists can emphasize diagnostic features for identification. The practice has also expanded into fine art and fashion, as seen in the artist books by Deborah Bryan and Radha Pandey included in this exhibit, where botanical imagery tells stories, expresses perspectives, and highlights critical issues. This exhibit brings attention to women who succeeded in studying and practicing botanical art, spanning the late seventeenth century to 2022, while acknowledging that this collection represents only part of women’s enduring impact on the botanical arts.', 'A color drawing of a brown patterned moth in the top right above a plant with an orange fruit and white flowers. As printing technology advanced to include color, botanical illustration expanded in both scope and popularity, and by the eighteenth century, amid growing interest in botany, the field became increasingly professionalized. Although few women botanical illustrators or natural scientists of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries were widely recognized or published under their own names, women were nonetheless deeply involved in the natural sciences as independent illustrators and often uncredited artists. Maria Sibylla Merian and Anna Botsford Comstock, featured in this exhibit, were among those who broke convention and achieved significant recognition for their work. Over time, botanical illustration evolved to more widely include women and to embrace new styles and media. Even in the age of high definition photography, illustration remains vital for scientific study because artists can emphasize diagnostic features for identification. The practice has also expanded into fine art and fashion, as seen in the artist books by Deborah Bryan and Radha Pandey included in this exhibit, where botanical imagery tells stories, expresses perspectives, and highlights critical issues. This exhibit brings attention to women who succeeded in studying and practicing botanical art, spanning the late seventeenth century to 2022, while acknowledging that this collection represents only part of women’s enduring impact on the botanical arts.\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Format\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"still image\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Media Type\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"image\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Dimensions\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"1974 × 3059\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Copyright\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"Public Domain\"]}}],\"requiredStatement\":{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Attribution\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"University of Denver\"]}},\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/9ca4f505-5465-4cf8-8695-83d2a4998e1b/manifest/canvas/1\",\"type\":\"Canvas\",\"width\":1974,\"height\":3059,\"label\":{\"en\":[\"d5b80640-f03f-4000-8e3a-33cc141c23f7_1738705815736_item_media.jpg\"]},\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/9ca4f505-5465-4cf8-8695-83d2a4998e1b/manifest/canvas/1/page\",\"type\":\"AnnotationPage\",\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/9ca4f505-5465-4cf8-8695-83d2a4998e1b/manifest/canvas/1/page/annotation\",\"type\":\"Annotation\",\"motivation\":\"painting\",\"body\":{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/9ca4f505-5465-4cf8-8695-83d2a4998e1b/full/max/0/default.jpg\",\"type\":\"Image\",\"format\":\"image/jpeg\",\"width\":1974,\"height\":3059,\"service\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/9ca4f505-5465-4cf8-8695-83d2a4998e1b\",\"type\":\"ImageService3\",\"profile\":\"level1\"}]},\"target\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/9ca4f505-5465-4cf8-8695-83d2a4998e1b/manifest/canvas/1\"}]}]}],\"summary\":{\"en\":[\"The first known botanical illustration dates to around 70 CE, when the Greek botanist Pedanius Dioscorides created drawings for De Materia Medica to help readers identify medicinal plants. As printing technology advanced to include color, botanical illustration expanded in both scope and popularity, and by the eighteenth century, amid growing interest in botany, the field became increasingly professionalized. Although few women botanical illustrators or natural scientists of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries were widely recognized or published under their own names, women were nonetheless deeply involved in the natural sciences as independent illustrators and often uncredited artists. Maria Sibylla Merian and Anna Botsford Comstock, featured in this exhibit, were among those who broke convention and achieved significant recognition for their work. Over time, botanical illustration evolved to more widely include women and to embrace new styles and media. Even in the age of high definition photography, illustration remains vital for scientific study because artists can emphasize diagnostic features for identification. The practice has also expanded into fine art and fashion, as seen in the artist books by Deborah Bryan and Radha Pandey included in this exhibit, where botanical imagery tells stories, expresses perspectives, and highlights critical issues. This exhibit brings attention to women who succeeded in studying and practicing botanical art, spanning the late seventeenth century to 2022, while acknowledging that this collection represents only part of women’s enduring impact on the botanical arts.\"]},\"thumbnail\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/9ca4f505-5465-4cf8-8695-83d2a4998e1b/full/!400,400/0/default.jpg\",\"type\":\"Image\",\"format\":\"image/jpeg\",\"width\":400,\"height\":400}]}', 0, 5, '2025-02-04 16:42:19', '2026-04-15 15:24:00', 'Madison Sussmann', 'migration_script'),

1932</b></p>\\n\\n<p>As touring companies began to close due to the Great Depression, there was a rise of small town opera companies opening (Martin, 2009). The homegrown opera would help to revive the community, as people would flood into town every summer.</p>\\n\\n<p><em>Courtesy Colorado Historic Newspaper Collection</em></p>\"]},\"thumbnail\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/f9445028-c661-461d-b18f-eb4b4299d8f2/full/!400,400/0/default.jpg\",\"type\":\"Image\",\"format\":\"image/jpeg\",\"width\":400,\"height\":400}]}', 0, 5, '2025-07-23 17:19:32', '2026-04-15 15:24:00', 'Madison Sussmann', 'migration_script'),

(279, 'c311ff40-96f6-41e5-8270-76722eafed32', '2a0855be-99cd-482e-9f49-1d5108d32172_1757541198907_item_media.jpg', '<p>When <em>Ulysses</em> was first imported to the United States, Customs officials seized every copy for obscenity. The novel’s explicit language and experimental style challenged prevailing standards of decency. In 1933, Judge John Woolsey ruled that a book must be judged as a whole and found Joyce’s novel had literary merit. The decision, later upheld in appeal, lifted the ban and set a new benchmark for evaluating obscenity. By emphasizing overall artistic value rather than isolated passages, the ruling opened the door for modernist literature in the U.S. and provided the first major precedent protecting controversial works.</p>', 'Cover of Ulysses book', 0, NULL, NULL, NULL, 'image', 'image/jpeg', 'still image', NULL, 'c311ff40-96f6-41e5-8270-76722eafed32.jpg', '2a0855be-99cd-482e-9f49-1d5108d32172_1757541198907_item_media.jpg', 'upload', NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, '[\"6196b295-9690-4242-b845-9619454ee74e\"]', 681366, 'images/c3/11/c311ff40-96f6-41e5-8270-76722eafed32.jpg', 'thumbnails/c3/11/c311ff40-96f6-41e5-8270-76722eafed32_thumb.jpg', '{\"ImageWidth\":1065,\"ImageHeight\":1519,\"MIMEType\":\"image/jpeg\",\"FileType\":\"JPEG\",\"FileSize\":\"681 kB\",\"BitsPerSample\":8,\"CreateDate\":\"2025-09-10T15:34:32-06:00\",\"ModifyDate\":\"2025-09-10T21:34:32Z\",\"Title\":\"\",\"Creator\":[\"The Morgan Library & Museum\"],\"Copyright\":\"The Morgan Library & Museum\"}', 1065, 1519, NULL, '{\"@context\":\"http://iiif.io/api/presentation/3/context.json\",\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/c311ff40-96f6-41e5-8270-76722eafed32/manifest\",\"type\":\"Manifest\",\"label\":{\"en\":[\"2a0855be-99cd-482e-9f49-1d5108d32172_1757541198907_item_media.jpg\"]},\"metadata\":[{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Title\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"2a0855be-99cd-482e-9f49-1d5108d32172_1757541198907_item_media.jpg\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Description\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"<p>When <em>Ulysses</em> was first imported to the United States, Customs officials seized every copy for obscenity. Pico</em> (1982). In a divided decision, the Court held that the First Amendment limits the power of school boards to remove library books. While schools have discretion over curricula, the justices emphasized that library collections are a place of voluntary inquiry, and officials may not exclude books simply because they dislike the ideas expressed. The ruling reinstated <em>Slaughterhouse-Five</em> and other contested works, setting a vital precedent for intellectual freedom in education. (288, 'e4b75edb-8f11-48cc-b185-015c78deb143', 'af686a7e-fb98-4174-b293-abba4b05afe7_1757613801037_item_media.png', '<p>In 1999, Wichita Falls, Texas, passed a resolution allowing 300 citizens to petition for children’s books to be removed from library shelves. <em>Heather Has Two Mommies</em>, one of the first picture books about same-sex parents, was among the titles targeted. (501, '962b2077-c795-4bad-b6c7-2a067fd6a51e', 'Esther Wasserman', '<p>Esther Wasserman (1921- ) was born Esther Ajlman in Zwoleń, Poland on January 15, 1921. Within two years of Hitler’s invasion of Poland, she and her family were forced into the Zwoleń ghetto. Her family was broken up and Esther was deported to the Skarzysko-Kamienna concentration camp. During her time at the camp she experienced forced labor in a munitions factory, seven days a week, making bullets for the Germans. She was transferred from Skarzysko-Kamienna to the Tschenstochau concentration camp after a year, and was again forced into labor at a munitions factory.</p>\n\n<p>Following her time at Tschenstochau, Esther was moved to Ravensbrück, Burgau, and finally Türkheim concentration camp. In the last days of the war she was forced to march with the rest of Türkheim’s prisoners until the SS guards told them the war was over and to go wherever they wanted. She returned to the village of Türkheim where she was taken in by a German family for a few days before being liberated by the American armed forces.</p>\n\n<p>Shortly after the end of the war in 1945, Esther found and married her husband, Max Wasserman, in Germany. She made her way to America through Brooklyn, New York and was naturalized there on August 5, 1952. By the mid-1980’s Max and Esther had made their home in the Los Angeles metropolitan area. Esther Wasserman was the subject for one of Debra Howard\'s portraits of child holocaust survivors.</p>', 'A black and white portrait sketch of an older woman with red hair contrasting the white background and grey of her skin. In the last days of the war she was forced to march with the rest of Türkheim’s prisoners until the SS guards told them the war was over and to go wherever they wanted. She returned to the village of Türkheim where she was taken in by a German family for a few days before being liberated by the American armed forces.</p>\\n\\n<p>Shortly after the end of the war in 1945, Esther found and married her husband, Max Wasserman, in Germany. She made her way to America through Brooklyn, New York and was naturalized there on August 5, 1952. By the mid-1980’s Max and Esther had made their home in the Los Angeles metropolitan area. Esther Wasserman was the subject for one of Debra Howard\'s portraits of child holocaust survivors.</p>\"]},\"thumbnail\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/962b2077-c795-4bad-b6c7-2a067fd6a51e/full/!400,400/0/default.jpg\",\"type\":\"Image\",\"format\":\"image/jpeg\",\"width\":400,\"height\":400}]}', 0, 5, '2025-03-31 14:52:18', '2026-04-14 19:21:51', 'Madison Sussmann', 'migration_script'),
(502, '96befe70-ee31-42ce-97b1-d33023ef21f7', 'Nathalie Gonenn', '<p>Natalie Gonenn (1936- )was born Natalie Rendler in Chelm, Poland—a small town near the Ukranian border—on May 15, 1936. She was three years old when Hitler invaded Poland. Her time during the Holocaust was dominated by hiding and running. For the first month or so, she and her family hid in barns, however Nazi soldiers were patrolling the area and threatening farmers with death if they hid Jews. Luckily, one farmer dug a small bunker in the middle of a field for her and her family where they lived for several months. The farmer became scared for his family and insisted that her family leave and try to cross the river to the Ukraine. One night, they all ran to the river, got in a small boat, and attempted to cross. The Germans discovered them and began shooting, which prompted Russian troops to begin shooting from the other side of the river. The boat capsized and fortunately they were pulled out of the river on the Ukranian side.</p>\n\n<p>As the Germans advanced, their family was continually on the run. Eventually they were taken in by a Rabbi in Kiev, where they lived for a couple months. Her family fled as the Germans advanced and bombed the city. They were moved via cattle cars and experienced bombing for much of their journey deeper into Russia. They were asked to accept Russian citizenship, which her father rejected and her family was sent to a camp in Siberia. Living conditions were so bad that soon after, her father accepted Russian citizenship and her family returned to a town outside of Moscow. As the Germans advanced, her family was forced to run again and ended up in Uzbekistan. Natalie’s family spent approximately two years in Uzbekistan where her mother got sick and gave her and her brother to an orphanage. Natalie lived out the rest of the war in the orphanage and what she refers to as a sanatorium.</p>\n\n<p>After the war, her family returned to Chelm, but it was still unsafe for Jews. As such, her family left and spent time at three displaced persons camps: Stuttgart Flandern Kaserne, Berlin, and Ulm. Her family ended up moving to Palestine by way of a ship called the Galila. They arrived in 1948 and she spent eleven years there. She served in the Israeli military and met her husband, Max Gonenn, while at university. They were married in Haifa in 1959 and shortly after moved to New York where they settled and started a family.</p>', 'A drawn portrait of an older woman with short, reddish hair and a white shirt. For the first month or so, she and her family hid in barns, however Nazi soldiers were patrolling the area and threatening farmers with death if they hid Jews. They were moved via cattle cars and experienced bombing for much of their journey deeper into Russia. They were asked to accept Russian citizenship, which her father rejected and her family was sent to a camp in Siberia. Living conditions were so bad that soon after, her father accepted Russian citizenship and her family returned to a town outside of Moscow. As the Germans advanced, her family was forced to run again and ended up in Uzbekistan. (503, '6554e993-71cc-4143-a54a-d97bfc3f3e5b', 'Steven Metzler', '<p>Steve (Steven) Metzler (1925- ) was born on June 10, 1925 in Deda, Romania. He grew up in a Hasidic family and experienced extreme antisemitism prior to the Holocaust including being beaten and called “Christ killer” in the first grade. When the German army arrived in his town, his family were made to house two soldiers, one of whom offered to help them escape. Steve’s father rejected the offer and within a month, all of the Jews in the area were forced into the Oradea ghetto. Shortly after arriving at the ghetto in May of 1944, Steve and his family were brought to the trains on June 1.</p>\n\n<p>Their train ride spanned four to six days and some of the elderly individuals on the train realized it was heading to Auschwitz based on the towns they rode through. The train arrived at Auschwitz-Birkenau on June 6 where everyone deboarded and were immediately forced into a selection line where Steve saw his mother for the last time as they were separated and she was ushered toward the crematorium. After selection, Steve, along with many others, were told to strip naked, had all of their hair cut off, were sprayed with disinfectant, tattooed with their prisoner number, and were marched to their bunks. Steve was selected to work with a roof repair group, which provided him the ability to observe the extent of operations at the camps. During his time at Auschwitz-Birkenau, Steve witnessed many atrocities first hand. He was forced to watch hangings of other prisoners in the evenings and saw German Shepherds being set upon other prisoners. In August of 1944, during another selection, Steve knew that he was not well enough to pass and jumped out of a second story window to hide in another bunk room while selection occurred. Near the end of 1944, Steve and his work group were driven to the Birkenau crematorium. Upon arrival they were informed that they would not be getting gassed and burned, but instead were there to drill holes for dynamite to destroy the building. Steve, along with the prisoners of Auschwitz-Birkenau were evacuated on January 15, 1945 and began a death march.</p>\n\n<p>He marched for two to three months before his group arrived at a makeshift concentration camp in a large field deep in Germany. They began the march with 16,000 people and arrived with 300. His group was forced to dig ditches around a German airfield, but he knew that for his health he would have to escape before the next march. One night, he crawled under the fence to a river, crossed several irrigation ditches, and found an abandoned barn where he waited for eight days before being liberated by the United States Armed Forces.</p>\n\n<p>After liberation, he had dysentery and was brought to a hospital where he was also confirmed to have tuberculosis. He spent two years at a sanatorium in Haunstein, Germany. In 1950, Steve arrived in America and subsequently settled in Denver, Colorado. He worked in electronics repair and construction before retiring and getting into the art business. He has a daughter and two grandchildren.</p>', 'A drawn portrait of an older man with a white shirt looking forward. The background is a subdued blue which makes the oranges and yellows of his skin pop out.', 0, NULL, NULL, NULL, 'image', 'image/jpeg', 'still image', NULL, '6554e993-71cc-4143-a54a-d97bfc3f3e5b.jpg', '3914365e-e84d-4838-b969-952e49891d37_1743447361689_item_media.jpg', 'upload', NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, '[\"a36ddb1f-2141-4e7a-a2d5-e5241d39d4b6\"]', 886926, 'images/65/54/6554e993-71cc-4143-a54a-d97bfc3f3e5b.jpg', 'thumbnails/65/54/6554e993-71cc-4143-a54a-d97bfc3f3e5b_thumb.jpg', '{\"Software\":\"Adobe Photoshop CS4 Windows\",\"ImageWidth\":1107,\"ImageHeight\":1386,\"MIMEType\":\"image/jpeg\",\"FileType\":\"JPEG\",\"FileSize\":\"887 kB\",\"BitsPerSample\":8,\"DateTimeOriginal\":\"2018-06-28T15:13:26.000\",\"CreateDate\":\"2018-06-28T15:13:26.000\",\"ModifyDate\":\"2018-06-28T16:01:58\"}', 1107, 1386, NULL, '{\"@context\":\"http://iiif.io/api/presentation/3/context.json\",\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/6554e993-71cc-4143-a54a-d97bfc3f3e5b/manifest\",\"type\":\"Manifest\",\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Steven Metzler\"]},\"metadata\":[{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Title\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"Steven Metzler\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Description\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"<p>Steve (Steven) Metzler (1925- ) was born on June 10, 1925 in Deda, Romania. (504, 'da3956a0-a4c8-4fc1-8e5f-4cae0eb46403', 'Jack Welner', '<p>Jack Welner (1920- 2019) was born Jacob Nuta Welniarz in Łódź, Poland on September 10, 1920. Jack was forced into the Łódź ghetto in the mid-late 1930s, prior to his internment at four concentration camps. During the Holocaust Jack was a prisoner at Auschwitz, Dachau, Kaufering Lager IV, and Kaufering Lager X. The exact chronology of his time at each camp is unknown, however Dachau Concentration Camp records show he was transferred from Auschwitz to Dachau on September 1, 1944. In April 1945, the remaining prisoners at Dachau were led on a death march. The SS guards leading them fled and later that day the Americans found them.</p>\n\n<p>He was liberated by the United States armed forces in Waakirchen, Germany. Jack arrived in the United States through the Port of New York on the USAT Gen. J. H. McRae on February 21, 1950 and subsequently made his way to Denver, Colorado. He was naturalized as an American citizen on July 13, 1950. Jack’s uncle, Abe Winograd, assisted Jack and his brother in their emigration to Colorado. Jack lived out the rest of his life in the Denver metropolitan area where he found his soulmate and had several children. He passed away on August 28, 2019.</p>', 'A drawn portrait of an older man with a grey muscle and hair. The colors of his skin and the background are subdued and have an overall grayish-brown tone. He is looking straight at the viewer with a knowing expression.', 0, NULL, NULL, NULL, 'image', 'image/jpeg', 'still image', NULL, 'da3956a0-a4c8-4fc1-8e5f-4cae0eb46403.jpg', '1f6e55d7-6fa1-4778-b203-a3bf8f412fc1_1743447458730_item_media.jpg', 'upload', NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, '[\"a36ddb1f-2141-4e7a-a2d5-e5241d39d4b6\"]', 976091, 'images/da/39/da3956a0-a4c8-4fc1-8e5f-4cae0eb46403.jpg', 'thumbnails/da/39/da3956a0-a4c8-4fc1-8e5f-4cae0eb46403_thumb.jpg', '{\"Software\":\"Adobe Photoshop CS5 Windows\",\"ImageWidth\":1246,\"ImageHeight\":1596,\"MIMEType\":\"image/jpeg\",\"FileType\":\"JPEG\",\"FileSize\":\"976 kB\",\"BitsPerSample\":8,\"DateTimeOriginal\":\"2018-07-17T13:58:15.000\",\"CreateDate\":\"2018-07-17T13:58:15.000\",\"ModifyDate\":\"2018-07-17T15:44:56\"}', 1246, 1596, NULL, '{\"@context\":\"http://iiif.io/api/presentation/3/context.json\",\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/da3956a0-a4c8-4fc1-8e5f-4cae0eb46403/manifest\",\"type\":\"Manifest\",\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Jack Welner\"]},\"metadata\":[{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Title\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"Jack Welner\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Description\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"<p>Jack Welner (1920- 2019) was born Jacob Nuta Welniarz in Łódź, Poland on September 10, 1920. Jack was forced into the Łódź ghetto in the mid-late 1930s, prior to his internment at four concentration camps. During the Holocaust Jack was a prisoner at Auschwitz, Dachau, Kaufering Lager IV, and Kaufering Lager X. The exact chronology of his time at each camp is unknown, however Dachau Concentration Camp records show he was transferred from Auschwitz to Dachau on September 1, 1944. In April 1945, the remaining prisoners at Dachau were led on a death march. The SS guards leading them fled and later that day the Americans found them.</p>\\n\\n<p>He was liberated by the United States armed forces in Waakirchen, Germany. Jack arrived in the United States through the Port of New York on the USAT Gen. J. H. McRae on February 21, 1950 and subsequently made his way to Denver, Colorado. He was naturalized as an American citizen on July 13, 1950. Jack’s uncle, Abe Winograd, assisted Jack and his brother in their emigration to Colorado. Jack lived out the rest of his life in the Denver metropolitan area where he found his soulmate and had several children. He passed away on August 28, 2019.</p>\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Format\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"still image\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Media Type\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"image\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Date Created\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"2018-07-17T13:58:15.000\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Dimensions\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"1246 × 1596\"]}}],\"requiredStatement\":{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Attribution\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"University of Denver\"]}},\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/da3956a0-a4c8-4fc1-8e5f-4cae0eb46403/manifest/canvas/1\",\"type\":\"Canvas\",\"width\":1246,\"height\":1596,\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Jack Welner\"]},\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/da3956a0-a4c8-4fc1-8e5f-4cae0eb46403/manifest/canvas/1/page\",\"type\":\"AnnotationPage\",\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/da3956a0-a4c8-4fc1-8e5f-4cae0eb46403/manifest/canvas/1/page/annotation\",\"type\":\"Annotation\",\"motivation\":\"painting\",\"body\":{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/da3956a0-a4c8-4fc1-8e5f-4cae0eb46403/full/max/0/default.jpg\",\"type\":\"Image\",\"format\":\"image/jpeg\",\"width\":1246,\"height\":1596,\"service\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/da3956a0-a4c8-4fc1-8e5f-4cae0eb46403\",\"type\":\"ImageService3\",\"profile\":\"level1\"}]},\"target\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/da3956a0-a4c8-4fc1-8e5f-4cae0eb46403/manifest/canvas/1\"}]}]}],\"summary\":{\"en\":[\"<p>Jack Welner (1920- 2019) was born Jacob Nuta Welniarz in Łódź, Poland on September 10, 1920. Jack was forced into the Łódź ghetto in the mid-late 1930s, prior to his internment at four concentration camps. During the Holocaust Jack was a prisoner at Auschwitz, Dachau, Kaufering Lager IV, and Kaufering Lager X. The exact chronology of his time at each camp is unknown, however Dachau Concentration Camp records show he was transferred from Auschwitz to Dachau on September 1, 1944. In April 1945, the remaining prisoners at Dachau were led on a death march. The SS guards leading them fled and later that day the Americans found them.</p>\\n\\n<p>He was liberated by the United States armed forces in Waakirchen, Germany. Jack arrived in the United States through the Port of New York on the USAT Gen. J. H. McRae on February 21, 1950 and subsequently made his way to Denver, Colorado. He was naturalized as an American citizen on July 13, 1950. Jack’s uncle, Abe Winograd, assisted Jack and his brother in their emigration to Colorado. Jack lived out the rest of his life in the Denver metropolitan area where he found his soulmate and had several children. He passed away on August 28, 2019.</p>\"]},\"thumbnail\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/da3956a0-a4c8-4fc1-8e5f-4cae0eb46403/full/!400,400/0/default.jpg\",\"type\":\"Image\",\"format\":\"image/jpeg\",\"width\":400,\"height\":400}]}', 0, 5, '2025-03-31 14:57:45', '2026-04-14 19:21:51', 'Madison Sussmann', 'migration_script'),
(505, '0deef586-25cb-46d7-b4fe-f968e93a0f4c', 'Miriam Brysk', '<p>Miriam Brysk (1935- ) was born Miriam Miasnik in Warsaw, Poland on March 10, 1935. At four and a half, she and her family experienced the bombardment of Warsaw by the Germans. Shortly after the bombings, her family decided to move to eastern Poland and settle in Lida which was occupied by the Russians at that point. In 1941, Germans attacked the Soviet Union and Lida, gaining control of the city. Her family remained, and in December of 1941, Miriam and her family were forced into the Lida ghetto.</p>\n\n<p>On May 8, 1942 Germans and nazi-sympathizers rounded up all of the Jews in the ghetto, and brought them to a checkpoint where they were sent left or right. Miriam’s family was sent to the right, which turned out to be a mass grave where troops were executing Jews. Approximately 80 percent of the Jews in the Lida ghetto were murdered that day. Luckily, her father was an accomplished surgeon and was able to negotiate their escape from that fate. At that point, her parents sent her to hide with a non-Jewish family just outside of Lida, fearing that the Germans were going to come to the Ghetto and exterminate all the Jewish children. When that turned out not to be true, Miriam returned to the ghetto, but not for long.</p>\n\n<p>In November, 1942 her family left the ghetto to join the partisan fighters in the Lipiczany Forest. Miriam spent two years with her family in the forest. Her parents cut her hair and dressed her as a boy as rape was commonplace among the partisans, defectors, and others living in the area. In the early summer of 1944, the Russians liberated the partisans and individuals living in the forest. The Russians assigned them a new place to live--Szczuczyn, a Belorussian town. However, her family did not want to live under communist rule, so they escaped to Lublin, Poland. From there, her family moved to Czechoslovakia, Hungary, Romania, back to Hungary, Austria, Italy, and finally a displaced persons camp called Monte Mario in Rome. After a couple months at the displaced persons camp, they acquired visas to go to America. They left from Naples and traveled by ship to New York where they arrived February 23, 1947.</p>\n\n<p>While she had a difficult time acclimating to America, Miriam was quite successful in her life. She received her bachelors in Biology and Chemistry from NYU, where she also met her husband Henry Brysk. Miriam went on to receive a masters in microbiology from the University of Michigan and a PhD in Biomedical Sciences from Columbia University. She published extensively in her fields and after retiring around 2000, began to work on art and Holocaust awareness.</p>', 'A black and white drawn portrait of an older woman with very short, dark hair and large glasses. She is looking at the viewer with a slight smile. (506, '4c261974-49a6-4dc0-a853-52bcd6569757', 'Susan Bendor', '<p>Susan Julia Bendor (1937-2016) was born Susan Blum in Budapest, Hungary on February 5, 1937. While she was still allowed at ballet school at the age of four, she was told that she would not be able to perform as Jews were no longer allowed to participate in public events. Her dance teacher decided that if one child could not dance, there would be no performance. As such, she was allowed to perform, but remembers restrictions on Jews gradually becoming more strict. Jews were forced to wear yellow stars beginning in 1944. Her father was forced to work in a Hungarian labor camp known as Munkacs during much of her childhood. During the early 1940s, Susan’s mother continually planned for Germany’s invasion of Hungary. She acquired Christian identification papers and sent Susan and her brother to catechism classes to learn about Christianity in case they were ever questioned.</p>\n\n<p>When the Germans arrived, her family left for their home in the countryside. They remained for around a month before hearing the Germans were targeting Jews in the countryside and subsequently returned to their apartment in Budapest. Their apartment had been designated a Jewish house so they were not required to live in the ghetto. Her mother invited several other Jews to live with them. Shortly after the German invasion, her mother was forced to work as a seamstress for a German uniform factory. While there, she befriended the German officer in charge who agreed to take her family and several other people and hide them in the cellar of his apartment building. Wuchta, the German officer, hid them for nine months with the help of his aide who would periodically check in on them. They lived on sauerkraut and horsemeat. While in the cellar the apartment building was bombed, however they survived. Her family and others in the cellar were liberated on January 18, 1945 by the Russians, when Susan was nearly eight.</p>\n\n<p>After returning to their old apartment building they found it had also been bombed, and their home destroyed. By the time she finished fourth grade, the communists had taken over and her parents felt they needed to leave to stay safe. They acquired a forged passport and made their way to Vienna, Austria. Her family spent two years in Vienna, subletting a variety of apartments. When the Korean War began, her father was concerned it was the beginning of World War 3 and arranged for escape to Canada in 1951. They settled in Montreal where Susan eventually met and married her husband, Edgar Bendor, in 1959. She and her husband moved to New York in 1960 where Susan became a social worker, always remembering the kindness she had received from strangers during her time in Budapest. She received her masters degree from Adelphi University, and PhD from Hunter City University.</p>', 'A black and white drawn portrait of an older woman from the neck up. She has short hair and a slight smile on her face, and her wrinkles are drawn in a semi-stylized way. (507, '62fecac4-1079-4981-ad48-f90aa67caa40', 'Suzanne Whiteley', '<p>Suzanne Whiteley (1935-2019) was born Suzanne Mehler on September 26, 1935 in Amsterdam. Her parents had recently moved to Amsterdam from Germany to escape persecution. The Germans arrived in Amsterdam when Suzanne was four and a half. They lived under increasingly restrictive conditions until the Germans took them in the beginning of 1943. Her family, along with many other Jews, were taken to a train station and waited for hours before they were put onto a train. The train took them to Westerbork concentration camp where they spent five to six months before being transported to Bergen-Belsen.</p>\n\n<p>Her family arrived at Bergen-Belson in the late spring of 1943. At that point the camp was relatively empty, but it filled up quickly as new prisoners arrived daily. Suzanne and her family spent one and a half years in Bergen-Belson. Hunger was a major facet of her life in the camp and many of her memories are related to acquiring food and water for her family. While at Bergen-Belson she lived in constant fear and concern for her own life and the lives of her family. The night before the camp was evacuated by the Germans, the prisoners were told to sit near the front gate of the camp and wait for transport. She ended up sitting with her father who at that point had become weak and sick. She woke in the morning to find he had passed during the night, but he was thrown onto the transport truck with her, her brother, and her mother and they made their way to the trains. Suzanne and her family spent approximately two weeks on the train prior to being liberated by Russian forces. During her time on the train, she, her family, and many others had contracted typhus and were treated upon arrival at a small German village. Suzanne got better before her mother and brother, and lived alone and with other orphans for some time before her family got better.</p>\n\n<p>After approximately four weeks in the small village, she and her family returned to Holland. However, there was a food shortage in Holland, so she and her brother were sent to Copenhagen to live. After some time, they returned to Amsterdam, but only for a year. Then in May of 1947 Suzanne, her mother, and brother moved to America. Suzanne eventually settled in Chicago and she is the mother of two daughters and one son.</p>', 'A black and white drawn portrait of a woman with short dark hair up against a cream-colored background. The contrast of the shadows of her skin is stark. She has a content expression on her face.', 0, NULL, NULL, NULL, 'image', 'image/jpeg', 'still image', NULL, '62fecac4-1079-4981-ad48-f90aa67caa40.jpg', '5154369d-276a-4940-99e0-a4e5efc8a575_1743449272908_item_media.jpg', 'upload', NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, '[\"a36ddb1f-2141-4e7a-a2d5-e5241d39d4b6\"]', 995143, 'images/62/fe/62fecac4-1079-4981-ad48-f90aa67caa40.jpg', 'thumbnails/62/fe/62fecac4-1079-4981-ad48-f90aa67caa40_thumb.jpg', '{\"Software\":\"Adobe Photoshop CS4 Windows\",\"ImageWidth\":1246,\"ImageHeight\":1689,\"MIMEType\":\"image/jpeg\",\"FileType\":\"JPEG\",\"FileSize\":\"995 kB\",\"BitsPerSample\":8,\"DateTimeOriginal\":\"2018-06-28T16:30:20.000\",\"CreateDate\":\"2018-06-28T16:30:20.000\",\"ModifyDate\":\"2018-06-28T16:40:55\"}', 1246, 1689, NULL, '{\"@context\":\"http://iiif.io/api/presentation/3/context.json\",\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/62fecac4-1079-4981-ad48-f90aa67caa40/manifest\",\"type\":\"Manifest\",\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Suzanne Whiteley\"]},\"metadata\":[{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Title\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"Suzanne Whiteley\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Description\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"<p>Suzanne Whiteley (1935-2019) was born Suzanne Mehler on September 26, 1935 in Amsterdam. Her parents had recently moved to Amsterdam from Germany to escape persecution. The Germans arrived in Amsterdam when Suzanne was four and a half. They lived under increasingly restrictive conditions until the Germans took them in the beginning of 1943. Her family, along with many other Jews, were taken to a train station and waited for hours before they were put onto a train. The train took them to Westerbork concentration camp where they spent five to six months before being transported to Bergen-Belsen.</p>\\n\\n<p>Her family arrived at Bergen-Belson in the late spring of 1943. At that point the camp was relatively empty, but it filled up quickly as new prisoners arrived daily. Suzanne and her family spent one and a half years in Bergen-Belson. Hunger was a major facet of her life in the camp and many of her memories are related to acquiring food and water for her family. While at Bergen-Belson she lived in constant fear and concern for her own life and the lives of her family. The night before the camp was evacuated by the Germans, the prisoners were told to sit near the front gate of the camp and wait for transport. She ended up sitting with her father who at that point had become weak and sick. She woke in the morning to find he had passed during the night, but he was thrown onto the transport truck with her, her brother, and her mother and they made their way to the trains. Suzanne and her family spent approximately two weeks on the train prior to being liberated by Russian forces. During her time on the train, she, her family, and many others had contracted typhus and were treated upon arrival at a small German village. Suzanne got better before her mother and brother, and lived alone and with other orphans for some time before her family got better.</p>\\n\\n<p>After approximately four weeks in the small village, she and her family returned to Holland. However, there was a food shortage in Holland, so she and her brother were sent to Copenhagen to live. After some time, they returned to Amsterdam, but only for a year. Then in May of 1947 Suzanne, her mother, and brother moved to America. Suzanne eventually settled in Chicago and she is the mother of two daughters and one son.</p>\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Format\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"still image\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Media Type\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"image\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Date Created\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"2018-06-28T16:30:20.000\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Dimensions\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"1246 × 1689\"]}}],\"requiredStatement\":{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Attribution\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"University of Denver\"]}},\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/62fecac4-1079-4981-ad48-f90aa67caa40/manifest/canvas/1\",\"type\":\"Canvas\",\"width\":1246,\"height\":1689,\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Suzanne Whiteley\"]},\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/62fecac4-1079-4981-ad48-f90aa67caa40/manifest/canvas/1/page\",\"type\":\"AnnotationPage\",\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/62fecac4-1079-4981-ad48-f90aa67caa40/manifest/canvas/1/page/annotation\",\"type\":\"Annotation\",\"motivation\":\"painting\",\"body\":{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/62fecac4-1079-4981-ad48-f90aa67caa40/full/max/0/default.jpg\",\"type\":\"Image\",\"format\":\"image/jpeg\",\"width\":1246,\"height\":1689,\"service\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/62fecac4-1079-4981-ad48-f90aa67caa40\",\"type\":\"ImageService3\",\"profile\":\"level1\"}]},\"target\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/62fecac4-1079-4981-ad48-f90aa67caa40/manifest/canvas/1\"}]}]}],\"summary\":{\"en\":[\"<p>Suzanne Whiteley (1935-2019) was born Suzanne Mehler on September 26, 1935 in Amsterdam. (508, 'c1abd394-6eec-4881-bf33-d04feeef40bd', 'Imre Frankovits', '<p>Imre Frankovits (Frankovitz, 1926-2010) was born Emerck Francozicci on May 8, 1926 in Vârciorog, Romania. His family was the only Jewish family in Vârciorog. They were prompted to move to Oradea in 1935 after a priest moved to Vârciorog who preached antisemitism and told the villagers not to shop at his family\'s general store. As restrictions for Jews became more stringent, life became more difficult, and in 1944 his family was forced into the Oradea ghetto.</p>\n\n<p>They lived in the ghetto for three to four weeks before being transported to Auschwitz-Birkenau. Upon their nighttime arrival at Auschwitz, they were made to line up. The officer who was sending Jews right or left was Josef Mengele who was also placing children from 13-18 into groups for future experimentation. Luckily, Imre had recently turned 18 and was sent to the right for labor. He spent only three days at Auschwitz before being transported to Mauthausen where he also only stayed for three days. From Mauthausen, Imre was transported to Ebensee where he was put to work building tunnels and petroleum refineries in the adjacent mountains. The camp was built for 8000, but by the end of the war there were 23,000 prisoners at Ebensee.</p>\n\n<p>As Ebensee was deep in the Austrian Alps, liberation for the prisoners came later than many of the camps. Imre was liberated by American troops who provided all of the prisoners with a very rich goulash. As he and many of the prisoners were extremely malnourished, the soup made him very sick and he slipped into a coma for two weeks. After coming out of his coma, Imre set out to find members of his family—traveling from Austria to Romania, then Vienna, and then to the Belsen Displaced Persons camp.</p>\n\n<p>Imre, along with a friend, secured transport to the United States through the American consulate and arrived in New York in the late 1940s. Imre had an aunt, uncle, and cousin in New York who he lived with for a few years prior to being drafted into the American Army during the Korean War. He served as a driver for an officer and turned down the opportunity for a permanent job in the Army in order to return to the US. After returning, Imre attended the Fashion Institute of Technology and worked his way to becoming a designer in the fashion industry. He lived in the Bronx and Manhattan.</p>', 'A painting of an older man with glasses and a light blue shirt. He is looking at the viewer with a subtle knowing smile. (509, 'bc3badb0-9edd-4e3a-882e-3ec4d401421e', 'Ava Kadishson Schieber', '<p>Ava Kadishson Schieber was born Ava Hegedis on April 12, 1926 in Novi Sad, Yugoslavia. Her family was well off and also very cultured. During the 1930s she was aware of the impending war both from the news and also witnessing ships of Jews being deported down the Danube. She had heard of Kristallnacht and knew of Jewish persecution happening elsewhere. Ava was taught by her parents to be prepared for a war that would likely come. Around 1940, her family moved from Novi Sad to Belgrade as they felt it would be better for their anonymity to live in a larger city. Ava remained in Novi Sad for one year to finish her schooling while she lived with her grandmother. She then left to be with her family in Belgrade. On April 6, 1941 the Nazis invaded Yugoslavia and bombarded Belgrade for several days.</p>\n\n<p>On April 12, her birthday, the Nazis entered the city, and put up posters instructing all of the Jews to register with the Germans. Her family knew that this was not a good choice and decided to figure out how to hide. Her father left to hide in Hungary. Ava’s mother used her birth certificate to acquire ID papers as she had converted prior to marriage and her birth certificate said she was Christian. Her sister married a Greek Orthodox man and the priest predated the marriage certificate prior to the war to avoid any suspicion. Ava was the only one who could not get ID papers, so she went to hide at a farm where her father had made arrangements for her. Ava spent four years on the farm, taking on the identity of a farmhand. She did chores and would sneak back to Belgrade to visit her mother and sister when possible. During her time at the farm, she received very little food. On one of her trips back to Belgrade, her mother asked her to get rations. While waiting in line, a German soldier passed by her, and since she was not perfectly aligned with the rest of the line, hit her in the mouth with the butt of his gun, breaking her front teeth. Ava remembers that in the winter of 1942, the Jews of Belgrade were rounded up and drowned under the ice of the Danube. Ava is an artist and had been prior to the war.</p>\n\n<p>During her time on the farm, she completed a few drawings which are at the United States Holocaust Memorial in Washington, DC. Near the end of the war, she decided to return to Belgrade. She found that her sister had died as a result of being taken to war. Ava went looking for her father and grandmother in Novi Sad, however when she arrived she was informed that she was the first Jew to return. A former neighbor told her that her father and grandmother were taken to Auschwitz where they both died. Her father played in the Auschwitz orchestra that performed as people were marched to the gas chambers. She returned to Belgrade with a few belongings from her former life and began to look for a job. She ended up going to the Art Academy in Belgrade and working as a painter and animator for movie studios.</p>\n\n<p>In 1949, Ava and her mother left Yugoslavia for Israel. They arrived at the port of Haifa and quickly made their way south to Tel Aviv. She was immediately hired by a movie studio to do a similar job as she had done while in Belgrade. The man who hired her became her first husband—Yitzhak Kadishson. They were married for thirty years prior to his passing. About a decade later, a man named Nathan Schieber bought some of her art in America. They met several months after and fell in love. She decided to leave Israel and move to the Chicago area. She continued to paint, heavily influenced by her time hiding at the farm.</p>', 'A drawn color portrait of an older woman with short, grey hair, wearing a green turtle neck and up against a white background. She is facing to the left with her face turned forward and smiling subtly at the viewer.', 0, NULL, NULL, NULL, 'image', 'image/jpeg', 'still image', NULL, 'bc3badb0-9edd-4e3a-882e-3ec4d401421e.jpg', '670bc17d-5d1e-4e2d-a41f-d8c55f8006ca_1743449564273_item_media.jpg', 'upload', NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, '[\"a36ddb1f-2141-4e7a-a2d5-e5241d39d4b6\"]', 998755, 'images/bc/3b/bc3badb0-9edd-4e3a-882e-3ec4d401421e.jpg', 'thumbnails/bc/3b/bc3badb0-9edd-4e3a-882e-3ec4d401421e_thumb.jpg', '{\"Software\":\"Adobe Photoshop CS4 Windows\",\"ImageWidth\":1438,\"ImageHeight\":1821,\"MIMEType\":\"image/jpeg\",\"FileType\":\"JPEG\",\"FileSize\":\"999 kB\",\"BitsPerSample\":8,\"DateTimeOriginal\":\"2018-06-28T16:00:28.000\",\"CreateDate\":\"2018-06-28T16:00:28.000\",\"ModifyDate\":\"2018-06-28T16:07:13\"}', 1438, 1821, NULL, '{\"@context\":\"http://iiif.io/api/presentation/3/context.json\",\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/bc3badb0-9edd-4e3a-882e-3ec4d401421e/manifest\",\"type\":\"Manifest\",\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Ava Kadishson Schieber\"]},\"metadata\":[{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Title\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"Ava Kadishson Schieber\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Description\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"<p>Ava Kadishson Schieber was born Ava Hegedis on April 12, 1926 in Novi Sad, Yugoslavia. Her family was well off and also very cultured. During the 1930s she was aware of the impending war both from the news and also witnessing ships of Jews being deported down the Danube. She had heard of Kristallnacht and knew of Jewish persecution happening elsewhere. Ava was taught by her parents to be prepared for a war that would likely come. Around 1940, her family moved from Novi Sad to Belgrade as they felt it would be better for their anonymity to live in a larger city. Ava remained in Novi Sad for one year to finish her schooling while she lived with her grandmother. She then left to be with her family in Belgrade. On April 6, 1941 the Nazis invaded Yugoslavia and bombarded Belgrade for several days.</p>\\n\\n<p>On April 12, her birthday, the Nazis entered the city, and put up posters instructing all of the Jews to register with the Germans. Her family knew that this was not a good choice and decided to figure out how to hide. Her father left to hide in Hungary. Ava’s mother used her birth certificate to acquire ID papers as she had converted prior to marriage and her birth certificate said she was Christian. Her sister married a Greek Orthodox man and the priest predated the marriage certificate prior to the war to avoid any suspicion. Ava was the only one who could not get ID papers, so she went to hide at a farm where her father had made arrangements for her. Ava spent four years on the farm, taking on the identity of a farmhand. She did chores and would sneak back to Belgrade to visit her mother and sister when possible. During her time at the farm, she received very little food. On one of her trips back to Belgrade, her mother asked her to get rations. While waiting in line, a German soldier passed by her, and since she was not perfectly aligned with the rest of the line, hit her in the mouth with the butt of his gun, breaking her front teeth. Ava remembers that in the winter of 1942, the Jews of Belgrade were rounded up and drowned under the ice of the Danube. Ava is an artist and had been prior to the war.</p>\\n\\n<p>During her time on the farm, she completed a few drawings which are at the United States Holocaust Memorial in Washington, DC. Near the end of the war, she decided to return to Belgrade. She found that her sister had died as a result of being taken to war. Ava went looking for her father and grandmother in Novi Sad, however when she arrived she was informed that she was the first Jew to return. A former neighbor told her that her father and grandmother were taken to Auschwitz where they both died. Her father played in the Auschwitz orchestra that performed as people were marched to the gas chambers. She returned to Belgrade with a few belongings from her former life and began to look for a job. She ended up going to the Art Academy in Belgrade and working as a painter and animator for movie studios.</p>\\n\\n<p>In 1949, Ava and her mother left Yugoslavia for Israel. They arrived at the port of Haifa and quickly made their way south to Tel Aviv. She was immediately hired by a movie studio to do a similar job as she had done while in Belgrade. The man who hired her became her first husband—Yitzhak Kadishson. They were married for thirty years prior to his passing. About a decade later, a man named Nathan Schieber bought some of her art in America. They met several months after and fell in love. She decided to leave Israel and move to the Chicago area. She continued to paint, heavily influenced by her time hiding at the farm.</p>\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Format\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"still image\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Media Type\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"image\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Date Created\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"2018-06-28T16:00:28.000\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Dimensions\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"1438 × 1821\"]}}],\"requiredStatement\":{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Attribution\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"University of Denver\"]}},\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/bc3badb0-9edd-4e3a-882e-3ec4d401421e/manifest/canvas/1\",\"type\":\"Canvas\",\"width\":1438,\"height\":1821,\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Ava Kadishson Schieber\"]},\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/bc3badb0-9edd-4e3a-882e-3ec4d401421e/manifest/canvas/1/page\",\"type\":\"AnnotationPage\",\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/bc3badb0-9edd-4e3a-882e-3ec4d401421e/manifest/canvas/1/page/annotation\",\"type\":\"Annotation\",\"motivation\":\"painting\",\"body\":{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/bc3badb0-9edd-4e3a-882e-3ec4d401421e/full/max/0/default.jpg\",\"type\":\"Image\",\"format\":\"image/jpeg\",\"width\":1438,\"height\":1821,\"service\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/bc3badb0-9edd-4e3a-882e-3ec4d401421e\",\"type\":\"ImageService3\",\"profile\":\"level1\"}]},\"target\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/bc3badb0-9edd-4e3a-882e-3ec4d401421e/manifest/canvas/1\"}]}]}],\"summary\":{\"en\":[\"<p>Ava Kadishson Schieber was born Ava Hegedis on April 12, 1926 in Novi Sad, Yugoslavia. Her family was well off and also very cultured. During the 1930s she was aware of the impending war both from the news and also witnessing ships of Jews being deported down the Danube. She had heard of Kristallnacht and knew of Jewish persecution happening elsewhere. Ava was taught by her parents to be prepared for a war that would likely come. Around 1940, her family moved from Novi Sad to Belgrade as they felt it would be better for their anonymity to live in a larger city. Ava remained in Novi Sad for one year to finish her schooling while she lived with her grandmother. She then left to be with her family in Belgrade. On April 6, 1941 the Nazis invaded Yugoslavia and bombarded Belgrade for several days.</p>\\n\\n<p>On April 12, her birthday, the Nazis entered the city, and put up posters instructing all of the Jews to register with the Germans. Her family knew that this was not a good choice and decided to figure out how to hide. Her father left to hide in Hungary. Ava’s mother used her birth certificate to acquire ID papers as she had converted prior to marriage and her birth certificate said she was Christian. Her sister married a Greek Orthodox man and the priest predated the marriage certificate prior to the war to avoid any suspicion. Ava was the only one who could not get ID papers, so she went to hide at a farm where her father had made arrangements for her. Ava spent four years on the farm, taking on the identity of a farmhand. She did chores and would sneak back to Belgrade to visit her mother and sister when possible. During her time at the farm, she received very little food. On one of her trips back to Belgrade, her mother asked her to get rations. While waiting in line, a German soldier passed by her, and since she was not perfectly aligned with the rest of the line, hit her in the mouth with the butt of his gun, breaking her front teeth. Ava remembers that in the winter of 1942, the Jews of Belgrade were rounded up and drowned under the ice of the Danube. Ava is an artist and had been prior to the war.</p>\\n\\n<p>During her time on the farm, she completed a few drawings which are at the United States Holocaust Memorial in Washington, DC. Near the end of the war, she decided to return to Belgrade. She found that her sister had died as a result of being taken to war. Ava went looking for her father and grandmother in Novi Sad, however when she arrived she was informed that she was the first Jew to return. A former neighbor told her that her father and grandmother were taken to Auschwitz where they both died. Her father played in the Auschwitz orchestra that performed as people were marched to the gas chambers. She returned to Belgrade with a few belongings from her former life and began to look for a job. She ended up going to the Art Academy in Belgrade and working as a painter and animator for movie studios.</p>\\n\\n<p>In 1949, Ava and her mother left Yugoslavia for Israel. They arrived at the port of Haifa and quickly made their way south to Tel Aviv. She was immediately hired by a movie studio to do a similar job as she had done while in Belgrade. The man who hired her became her first husband—Yitzhak Kadishson. They were married for thirty years prior to his passing. About a decade later, a man named Nathan Schieber bought some of her art in America. They met several months after and fell in love. She decided to leave Israel and move to the Chicago area. She continued to paint, heavily influenced by her time hiding at the farm.</p>\"]},\"thumbnail\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/bc3badb0-9edd-4e3a-882e-3ec4d401421e/full/!400,400/0/default.jpg\",\"type\":\"Image\",\"format\":\"image/jpeg\",\"width\":400,\"height\":400}]}', 0, 5, '2025-03-31 15:32:50', '2026-04-14 19:21:51', 'Madison Sussmann', 'migration_script'),

Howe, the red sandstone building was completed in 1894 and features a 20-inch Clark-Saegmuller refractor telescope beneath its iconic silver dome. Images from Mariner 6 appear in two horizontal rows near the top of the globe, while Mariner 7’s images extend from the center downward to the bottom right, including the south polar cap. The Visual Imaging Investigation, or TV experiment, aboard Mariner 6 and 7 used two cameras on each spacecraft to achieve both broad coverage and high-resolution detail. Camera A, with a wide-angle lens, captured large areas measuring 1,000 by 1,000 kilometers and could resolve features as small as 3,000 meters during close encounters. Camera B, equipped with a telephoto lens, focused on smaller 100 by 100 kilometer regions and resolved details down to 300 meters. Phoenix confirmed the presence of water ice beneath the surface and laid the foundation for future polar exploration of Mars.\"]},\"thumbnail\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/fa027a2a-d0c3-4025-bae0-eb20bab739c9/full/!400,400/0/default.jpg\",\"type\":\"Image\",\"format\":\"image/jpeg\",\"width\":400,\"height\":400}]}', 0, 5, '2025-07-14 19:36:28', '2026-04-14 19:21:51', 'Madison Sussmann', 'migration_script'),

(572, 'd9c3d3f0-742e-4230-8cca-f070eaaff4cc', 'cbbc9a5f-a356-4c67-8f4d-f891c06fe1de_1752691961633_item_media.jpg', 'Bradford A. Smith was a pioneering planetary astronomer who played a central role in shaping NASA\'s early Mars exploration efforts. He served on the imaging teams for the Mariner 6 and 7 missions, which conducted some of the first successful flybys of Mars in 1969 and returned the earliest close-up images of the planet’s surface. His involvement in these missions helped lay the foundation for scientific imaging in planetary exploration. Smith’s work was deeply connected to Colorado’s scientific community through his collaborations with researchers and institutions contributing to planetary imaging and mission planning. (575, 'cc04e852-43af-4c26-9be5-c669e2b7f01b', '1_j4x6lqo4', '<p><strong>Transcript</strong><p>\n\n<p>\"Four, perhaps 64. Yeah, I think it was 1964. Bob Layton came to the lab to visit, with Clyde, and with me because they had a spacecraft headed toward Mars. They had a team that was to do an analysis of the Mars images and realized that they had nobody on their team that knew anything about Mars at all. That was the way things were done in those days. NASA essentially picked teams for their expertise in instrumentation, and so Leighton and Murray and other members of that group, including some geologists, were involved, but nobody who had any experience in looking at Mars from the Earth. And, Leighton came out to talk with us. And, he became very interested in the cumulative record that we had for Mars at that time in our photographic record and in Clyde\'s drawings. And, it was that point that we and the Mariner team and NASA itself began to realize the extreme importance of good ground-based records in tying together the very brief close-up views that a planetary probe is able to accomplish. And then tying that in what can be observed from the ground. By having essentially close-up views that you could then compare with the ground-based observations that were made at that same moment, then one might be able to infer things from the past and into the future.\"</p>', NULL, 0, NULL, NULL, NULL, 'video', NULL, 'moving image', NULL, NULL, '1_j4x6lqo4', 'kaltura', NULL, NULL, '1_j4x6lqo4', NULL, '[\"1a37ef6b-863e-45b5-8fdf-37ec54654fc9\"]', NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, '{\"@context\":\"http://iiif.io/api/presentation/3/context.json\",\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/cc04e852-43af-4c26-9be5-c669e2b7f01b/manifest\",\"type\":\"Manifest\",\"label\":{\"en\":[\"1_j4x6lqo4\"]},\"metadata\":[{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Title\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"1_j4x6lqo4\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Description\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"<p><strong>Transcript</strong><p>\\n\\n<p>\\\"Four, perhaps 64. Both spacecraft carried scientific instruments, including cameras, spectrometers, and radiometers. Mariner 6 flew past Mars on July 31, 1969, and Mariner 7 followed on August 5, 1969. Together, they returned over 200 images of the Martian surface, including detailed views of the equatorial and southern polar regions, greatly expanding scientific understanding of Mars and informing later missions such as Mariner 9.\"]},\"thumbnail\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/4bb94db5-864a-4675-8521-87fce437ef98/full/!400,400/0/default.jpg\",\"type\":\"Image\",\"format\":\"image/jpeg\",\"width\":400,\"height\":400}]}', 0, 5, '2025-07-16 15:34:39', '2026-04-14 19:21:51', 'Madison Sussmann', 'migration_script'),

It entered orbit around Mars on November 14, 1971, during a massive global dust storm. Once the atmosphere cleared, Mariner 9 mapped over 80% of the Martian surface, revealing unprecedented details of its geology. The mission discovered vast canyons (including Valles Marineris), extinct volcanoes (like Olympus Mons), dried riverbeds, and signs of past water activity. Mariner 9 also provided the first close-up views of Mars’ moons, Phobos and Deimos. (581, 'c264194f-c493-46f7-a007-7cdcfcba000c', '1_oidma8om', '<p><strong>Transcript</strong></p>\n\n<p>\"By the time I had worked on both Mariners 6 and 7, and when Mariner 8 and 9 were launched, Mariner 8 ended up in the Indian Ocean somewhere, but Mariner 9 did make it to Mars. While on its way to Mars, our ground-based program discovered the outbreak of an enormous dust storm on Mars, which we realized would be planet-wide by the time Mariner 9 reached there. That was first seen by us, and then the Lowell group confirmed it. But we discovered it. And here again, this is where Clyde\'s much longer visual observing experience really came in. Clyde had seen a number of these things happen over the years, and when he saw the intensity of this storm—which I think began somewhere in the Hellas region, if I recall; I’m not sure of that—but when he saw what we had just discovered, he said, “This is gonna be a really bad one.” And it was a really bad one. The Russians, the Soviets, arrived at roughly the same time we did, and they unfortunately weren’t prepared to spend a lot of time around Mars waiting it out. And so, their mission produced very little. Fortunately, Mariner 9 was able to wait out the dust storm, and we had a very successful mission. But that again illustrates the complementarity between the space program and a good ground-based planetary program. town in Connecticut and the other from the nation’s capital advertise the upcoming broadcast of the opening night performance of Camille. Following in the trend set by the Metropolitan Opera, radio broadcasts helped to democratize the opera, making it accessible to more than just the elite(Wilk, 2006). Central City Opera exemplifies this trend as it was revived by the elite for the lower classes.</p>\\n\\n<p><em>Courtesy Library of Congress</em></p>\"]},\"thumbnail\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/d335e81e-2d1c-421b-94be-06646ca31a4d/full/!400,400/0/default.jpg\",\"type\":\"Image\",\"format\":\"image/jpeg\",\"width\":400,\"height\":400}]}', 0, 5, '2025-07-30 15:04:54', '2026-04-15 15:24:00', 'Madison Sussmann', 'migration_script'),

(699, '8317d3f5-e41b-4132-836c-d20d461af382', '<strong>Eusebio Silverio</strong><br>\n<em>(Puebla, Mexico)</em>', '\"Bueno, cuando llegué a Brooklyn, era otro mundo. Venir de un pueblo chiquito a una ciudad grande, pavimentada. Pero es como otro mundo, porque puede ser una ciudad con edificios grandes, gente caminando, pero al mismo tiempo hay basura en la calle, bolsas de basura... Son mundos diferentes. Mi hermano estaba viviendo en Brooklyn, él es chef ejecutivo de la compañía donde trabaja. él estaba trabajando ahí y pues ahí se dio la oportunidad de que el me ofreció trabajo y me metió al restaurante. No empecé a cocinar. Empecé como lavaplatos. El restaurante era súper ocupado, atendíamos casi 500 personas en un día, desde las 10 de la mañana hasta las 12 de la noche. Entonces, pues te imaginas, lavando ollas, grandes sartenes, platos... dije: \'Wow, me quiero regresar a mi pueblo. Ya no quiero hacer esto.\' Hablé con mi hermano y le dije que no me imaginaba que fuera así, pensaba que sería más fácil. Contaban que los trabajos eran más fáciles y que ganabas dinero fácil, pero no era así. No quería regresar a México, aparte debía los 6000 dólares del coyote. Tenía que trabajar para pagar eso, no había vuelta atrás. <strong>English Translation</strong>:\nWell, when I arrived in Brooklyn, it was another world. Coming from a small town to a big, paved city. But it’s like another world, because it can be a city with tall buildings, people walking, but at the same time there’s trash on the street, garbage bags… They’re different worlds. My brother was living in Brooklyn; he’s the executive chef at the company where he works. He was working there, and that’s where the opportunity came up—he offered me a job and got me into the restaurant. I didn’t start cooking. I started as a dishwasher. The restaurant was super busy—we served almost 500 people in a day, from 10 in the morning until midnight. So, you can imagine, washing pots, big pans, plates… I said: “Wow, I want to go back to my town. I don’t want to do this anymore.” I talked to my brother and told him I hadn’t imagined it would be like this; I thought it would be easier. People used to say that jobs were easier and that you made easy money, but it wasn’t like that. I didn’t want to go back to Mexico—besides, I still owed the $6,000 to the coyote. Coming from a small town to a big, paved city. But it’s like another world, because it can be a city with tall buildings, people walking, but at the same time there’s trash on the street, garbage bags… They’re different worlds. My brother was living in Brooklyn; he’s the executive chef at the company where he works. He was working there, and that’s where the opportunity came up—he offered me a job and got me into the restaurant. I didn’t start cooking. I started as a dishwasher. The restaurant was super busy—we served almost 500 people in a day, from 10 in the morning until midnight. So, you can imagine, washing pots, big pans, plates… I said: “Wow, I want to go back to my town. I don’t want to do this anymore.” I talked to my brother and told him I hadn’t imagined it would be like this; I thought it would be easier. People used to say that jobs were easier and that you made easy money, but it wasn’t like that. I didn’t want to go back to Mexico—besides, I still owed the $6,000 to the coyote. I had to work to pay that off; there was no turning back.\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Format\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"moving image\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Media Type\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"video\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Kaltura Entry ID\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"1_vjbw774q\"]}}],\"requiredStatement\":{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Attribution\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"University of Denver\"]}},\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/8317d3f5-e41b-4132-836c-d20d461af382/manifest/canvas/1\",\"type\":\"Canvas\",\"width\":1920,\"height\":1080,\"duration\":0,\"label\":{\"en\":[\"<strong>Eusebio Silverio</strong><br>\\n<em>(Puebla, Mexico)</em>\"]},\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/8317d3f5-e41b-4132-836c-d20d461af382/manifest/canvas/1/page\",\"type\":\"AnnotationPage\",\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/8317d3f5-e41b-4132-836c-d20d461af382/manifest/canvas/1/page/annotation\",\"type\":\"Annotation\",\"motivation\":\"painting\",\"body\":{\"id\":\"https://cdnapisec.kaltura.com/p/2357732/sp/235773200/playManifest/entryId/1_vjbw774q/format/applehttp/protocol/https\",\"type\":\"Video\",\"format\":\"video/mp4\",\"width\":1920,\"height\":1080,\"duration\":0},\"target\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/8317d3f5-e41b-4132-836c-d20d461af382/manifest/canvas/1\"}]}],\"rendering\":[{\"id\":\"https://cdnapisec.kaltura.com/p/2357732/sp/235773200/embedIframeJs/uiconf_id/41433762/partner_id/2357732?iframeembed=true&entry_id=1_vjbw774q\",\"type\":\"Video\",\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Kaltura Player\"]},\"format\":\"text/html\"}]}],\"summary\":{\"en\":[\"\\\"Bueno, cuando llegué a Brooklyn, era otro mundo. My brother was living in Brooklyn; he’s the executive chef at the company where he works. He was working there, and that’s where the opportunity came up—he offered me a job and got me into the restaurant. I didn’t start cooking. I started as a dishwasher. The restaurant was super busy—we served almost 500 people in a day, from 10 in the morning until midnight. (701, 'a86c6b25-b0ea-4117-a9b2-ece077d2ba19', '1_5invurbq', '<p>Spanish Transcript:<br>\nYo creo que educando, primero en el gusto, y segundo educando y dando a conocer tus raíces. Es lo más importante. Y lo que hicimos ahorita aquí en Xiquita cuando hicimos el menú fue precisamente eso: que todo lo que nosotros íbamos a ofrecer al cliente tuviera una historia. Que no solamente fuera una ocurrencia de decir: \"Ah, vamos a poner esto en el menú un ceviche de camarón, porque sí\". No. No. Bueno. Entonces, ¿qué vamos a hacer? ¿De dónde viene? Por ejemplo, el maíz que utilizamos aquí, lo traemos de México. De una parte de Nayarit, de una parte de Tlaxcala, porque es maíz nativo. Entonces, se respeta de ahí todo eso. El proceso del maíz, la molienda del maíz, la cocción del maiz, todo lo que se tiene que hacer para llegar a un plato tiene una historia. No es solamente ir y comprar tortillas. Nos hemos topado mucho con el tema de no poder conseguir algún ingrediente. Porque no es lo mismo cocinar en México que cocinar aquí. Y más en la parte de aquí de Colorado, que a veces las cosas no son tan fáciles de conseguir. Si estuviéramos en otra parte fronteriza, pues es un poquito más fácil. Pero sí ha sido un reto bonito: mostrar la identidad, mostrar la cultura, enseñar.</p>\nEnglish Translation:<br>\nI think it’s about educating—first in taste, and second in teaching and sharing your roots. That’s the most important thing. And what we did here at Xiquita when we created the menu was precisely that: making sure that everything we offered the customer had a story. That it wasn’t just a random idea like, “Oh, let’s put shrimp ceviche on the menu, just because.” No. No. Well then, what are we going to do? Where does it come from?\nFor example, the corn we use here—we bring it from Mexico. From a region in Nayarit, from a region in Tlaxcala, because it’s native corn. So we honor all of that. The process of the corn, the grinding, the cooking—everything that has to be done to arrive at a dish has a story. It’s not just going and buying tortillas.\nWe’ve run into the issue many times of not being able to get certain ingredients. Because cooking in Mexico is not the same as cooking here. And especially here in Colorado, sometimes things aren’t so easy to find. If we were somewhere near the border, it would be a little easier. Me ha generado oportunidades para conocer a personas tan maravillosas—personas como tú, Julia, y mucha gente. De hecho, quiero mantenerlas. No quiero que se pierdan. Mantengo viva mi lengua materna, que es el mixteco. Es mi primer dialecto, mi lengua materna, con la que crecí antes de aprender español. Y es lo que me ha mantenido fuerte.</p>\\nEnglish Translation:<br>Food has helped me a lot. It has given me opportunities to meet such wonderful people—people like you, Julia, and so many others. Through cooking, through what I represent, I consider myself an Oaxacan ambassador, because I embody much of Oaxaca’s gastronomy, its culture, its living traditions. In fact, I want to preserve them. I don’t want them to be lost. I keep my mother tongue alive, which is Mixtec. It is my first dialect, my native language, the one I grew up with before learning Spanish. And it is what has kept me strong.\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Format\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"moving image\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Media Type\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"video\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Kaltura Entry ID\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"1_o3hbqos4\"]}}],\"requiredStatement\":{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Attribution\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"University of Denver\"]}},\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/d665e3cc-cab9-4134-9fcb-db7e7afe0bea/manifest/canvas/1\",\"type\":\"Canvas\",\"width\":1920,\"height\":1080,\"duration\":0,\"label\":{\"en\":[\"1_o3hbqos4\"]},\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/d665e3cc-cab9-4134-9fcb-db7e7afe0bea/manifest/canvas/1/page\",\"type\":\"AnnotationPage\",\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/d665e3cc-cab9-4134-9fcb-db7e7afe0bea/manifest/canvas/1/page/annotation\",\"type\":\"Annotation\",\"motivation\":\"painting\",\"body\":{\"id\":\"https://cdnapisec.kaltura.com/p/2357732/sp/235773200/playManifest/entryId/1_o3hbqos4/format/applehttp/protocol/https\",\"type\":\"Video\",\"format\":\"video/mp4\",\"width\":1920,\"height\":1080,\"duration\":0},\"target\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/d665e3cc-cab9-4134-9fcb-db7e7afe0bea/manifest/canvas/1\"}]}],\"rendering\":[{\"id\":\"https://cdnapisec.kaltura.com/p/2357732/sp/235773200/embedIframeJs/uiconf_id/41433762/partner_id/2357732?iframeembed=true&entry_id=1_o3hbqos4\",\"type\":\"Video\",\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Kaltura Player\"]},\"format\":\"text/html\"}]}],\"summary\":{\"en\":[\"<p>Spanish Transcript:<br>La comida me ha ayudado mucho. (705, '4f2067c9-a092-4784-bdb8-35116b8a5553', '1_ptfu52su', '<p>Spanish Transcript:<br>\nAl cocinar, siempre queremos mostrar lo que es Chile. Como no hay otro lugar que lo haga, queremos dar a conocer, por ejemplo, lo que es una empanada de pino, que es clásica de Chile. Sacar todo lo que es Chile. En el sur se cocina muy rico, en el norte, en el centro… y la gente no lo conoce. A veces te preguntan: “¿Pero la comida chilena? ¿Dónde es Chile?” Porque no conocen, es un país pequeñito, y dicen: “No me suena.” Entonces yo digo: “Bueno, te lo demuestro acá. Por favor, prueba mi comida. Te va a gustar. Te lo recomiendo y si no, te devuelvo la plata.” Pero la verdad es que la gente queda muy contenta.\nTambién he conocido mucha gente que ha visitado Chile —gente de acá, no solo latinos. A veces estadounidenses me dicen: “Sí, me encanta tu comida. Yo he ido a Chile. Conozco los completos, conozco las empanadas.” Y eso es súper lindo. Uno se enorgullece, porque sientes que estás representando a tu país a través de la comida. Se siente bien.</p>\nEnglish Translation:<br>\nWhen we cook, we always want to showcase what Chile is. Since there’s nowhere else that does it, we want to introduce, for example, the empanada de pino, which is a Chilean classic. To bring out everything that is Chile. In the south the food is delicious, in the north, in the center… and people don’t know it.\nSometimes they ask you: “But Chilean food? Where is Chile?” Because they don’t know—it’s a small country—and they say, “Doesn’t ring a bell.” So I say, “Well, I’ll show you right here. Please try my food. You’re going to like it. I recommend it, and if not, I’ll give you your money back.” But the truth is people leave very happy.\nI’ve also met many people who have visited Chile—people from here, not just Latinos. Sometimes Americans tell me: “Yes, I love your food. I’ve been to Chile. I know completos, I know empanadas.” And that’s really lovely. Te lo recomiendo y si no, te devuelvo la plata.” Pero la verdad es que la gente queda muy contenta.\\nTambién he conocido mucha gente que ha visitado Chile —gente de acá, no solo latinos. A veces estadounidenses me dicen: “Sí, me encanta tu comida. Yo he ido a Chile. Conozco los completos, conozco las empanadas.” Y eso es súper lindo. Uno se enorgullece, porque sientes que estás representando a tu país a través de la comida. Se siente bien.</p>\\nEnglish Translation:<br>\\nWhen we cook, we always want to showcase what Chile is. Since there’s nowhere else that does it, we want to introduce, for example, the empanada de pino, which is a Chilean classic. To bring out everything that is Chile. In the south the food is delicious, in the north, in the center… and people don’t know it.\\nSometimes they ask you: “But Chilean food? Where is Chile?” Because they don’t know—it’s a small country—and they say, “Doesn’t ring a bell.” So I say, “Well, I’ll show you right here. Please try my food. You’re going to like it. I recommend it, and if not, I’ll give you your money back.” But the truth is people leave very happy.\\nI’ve also met many people who have visited Chile—people from here, not just Latinos. Sometimes Americans tell me: “Yes, I love your food. I’ve been to Chile. I know completos, I know empanadas.” And that’s really lovely. You feel proud, because you feel you’re representing your country through food. It feels good.\"]},\"thumbnail\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/4f2067c9-a092-4784-bdb8-35116b8a5553/full/!400,400/0/default.jpg\",\"type\":\"Image\",\"format\":\"image/jpeg\",\"width\":400,\"height\":400}]}', 0, 8, '2025-09-18 18:53:56', '2026-04-14 19:21:52', 'Kayla Stutz', 'migration_script');
