(706, '5e68cb0d-af93-4656-b9ac-4255f0a4e6ea', '<strong>René González Méndez</strong><br>\n<em>(Mexico City, Mexico)</em>', '“Yo creo que educando, primero en el gusto, y segundo educando y dando a conocer tus raíces. Es lo más importante. Y lo que hicimos ahorita aquí en Xiquita cuando hicimos el menú fue precisamente eso: que todo lo que nosotros íbamos a ofrecer al cliente tuviera una historia. Que no solamente fuera una ocurrencia de decir: ‘Ah, vamos a poner esto en el menú un ceviche de camarón, porque sí.’ No. No. Bueno. Entonces, ¿qué vamos a hacer? ¿De dónde viene? Por ejemplo, el maíz que utilizamos aquí, lo traemos de México. De una parte de Nayarit, de una parte de Tlaxcala, porque es maíz nativo. Entonces, se respeta de ahí todo eso. El proceso del maíz, la molienda del maíz, la cocción del maiz, todo lo que se tiene que hacer para llegar a un plato tiene una historia. No es solamente ir y comprar tortillas. Nos hemos topado mucho con el tema de no poder conseguir algún ingrediente. Porque no es lo mismo cocinar en México que cocinar aquí. Y más en la parte de aquí de Colorado, que a veces las cosas no son tan fáciles de conseguir. Si estuviéramos en otra parte fronteriza, pues es un poquito más fácil. Pero sí ha sido un reto bonito: mostrar la identidad, mostrar la cultura, enseñar. English Translation: I think it’s about educating—first in taste, and second in teaching and sharing your roots. That’s the most important thing. And what we did here at Xiquita when we created the menu was precisely that: making sure that everything we offered the customer had a story. That it wasn’t just a random idea like, “Oh, let’s put shrimp ceviche on the menu, just because.” No. No. Well then, what are we going to do? Where does it come from? For example, the corn we use here—we bring it from Mexico. From a region in Nayarit, from a region in Tlaxcala, because it’s native corn. So we honor all of that. The process of the corn, the grinding, the cooking—everything that has to be done to arrive at a dish has a story. It’s not just going and buying tortillas. We’ve run into the issue many times of not being able to get certain ingredients. Because cooking in Mexico is not the same as cooking here. And especially here in Colorado, sometimes things aren’t so easy to find. If we were somewhere near the border, it would be a little easier. (709, 'f158af14-7737-41fb-ab4b-74dc3d155cf8', '<strong>Esly Mirelly Divas Cruz</strong><br>\n<em>(Guatemala City, Guatemala)</em>', '“Aunque encontré ese lugar pequeño, fue un crecimiento, porque —como le decía anteriormente— trataba de encajar en todo aquí. Y aún así lo inicié como Brunch on the Go, y muchos cuando decía mi nombre era como: ‘¿Cómo te llamas?’ ‘Brunch on the go.’ (risas). Me puse ese nombre porque yo decía: ‘Bueno, es desayuno, es almuerzo, que sea rápido y que ellos sepan.’ Pero no me definía. No me identificaba. La realidad es que me daba vergüenza mi nombre. Y aún me sigue dando risa, pero es parte de mi proceso de aprender y de crecer. Entonces empiezo. Sirvo café, arepas, pupusas —cosas que no son de Guatemala— pero yo tratando una vez más de acomodarme a todo mi entorno. Y olvidándome de mis raíces casi por completo, porque no tenía esa confianza, no tenía esa seguridad de: ‘A lo mejor sí les va a gustar. ¿A lo mejor no les llama la atención?’ Siempre ese miedo de uno tratando de tomar decisiones con la mente y no tanto con el corazón.” English Translation: Although I found that small place, it was a step forward because—as I mentioned before—I was trying to fit into everything here. And even so, I started it as “Brunch on the Go,” and when I’d say my name, many people were like, “What’s your name?” “Brunch on the Go.” (laughs). I chose that name because I thought, “Well, it’s breakfast, it’s lunch—let it be quick so they understand.” But it didn’t define me. I didn’t identify with it. The truth is, I was embarrassed by the name. And it still makes me laugh, but it’s part of my process of learning and growing. So I began. I served coffee, arepas, pupusas—things that aren’t from Guatemala—but once again I was trying to adapt to everything around me. And I was almost completely forgetting my roots, because I didn’t have that confidence, that assurance of: “Maybe they’ll like it—maybe it won’t catch their attention.” Always that fear of making decisions with your head and not so much with your heart.', NULL, 0, NULL, NULL, NULL, 'video', NULL, 'moving image', NULL, NULL, '1_9hzydqzq', 'kaltura', NULL, NULL, '1_9hzydqzq', NULL, '[\"3c654572-bca2-425c-ae1e-206223d25b51\"]', NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, '{\"@context\":\"http://iiif.io/api/presentation/3/context.json\",\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/f158af14-7737-41fb-ab4b-74dc3d155cf8/manifest\",\"type\":\"Manifest\",\"label\":{\"en\":[\"<strong>Esly Mirelly Divas Cruz</strong><br>\\n<em>(Guatemala City, Guatemala)</em>\"]},\"metadata\":[{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Title\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"<strong>Esly Mirelly Divas Cruz</strong><br>\\n<em>(Guatemala City, Guatemala)</em>\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Description\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"“Aunque encontré ese lugar pequeño, fue un crecimiento, porque —como le decía anteriormente— trataba de encajar en todo aquí. Me puse ese nombre porque yo decía: ‘Bueno, es desayuno, es almuerzo, que sea rápido y que ellos sepan.’ Pero no me definía. No me identificaba. La realidad es que me daba vergüenza mi nombre. (710, 'f923d842-61eb-41fe-a594-245b3e0cd1f9', '<strong>Mayra Chacón</strong><br>\n<em>(Santiago, Chile)</em>', '“Al cocinar, siempre queremos mostrar lo que es Chile. Como no hay otro lugar que lo haga, queremos dar a conocer, por ejemplo, lo que es una empanada de pino, que es clásica de Chile. Sacar todo lo que es Chile. En el sur se cocina muy rico, en el norte, en el centro… y la gente no lo conoce. A veces te preguntan: ‘¿Pero la comida chilena? ¿Dónde es Chile?’ Porque no conocen, es un país pequeñito, y dicen: ‘No me suena.’ Entonces yo digo: ‘Bueno, te lo demuestro acá. Por favor, prueba mi comida. Te va a gustar. Te lo recomiendo y si no, te devuelvo la plata.’ Pero la verdad es que la gente queda muy contenta. También he conocido mucha gente que ha visitado Chile —gente de acá, no solo latinos. A veces estadounidenses me dicen: ‘Sí, me encanta tu comida. Yo he ido a Chile. Conozco los completos, conozco las empanadas.’ Y eso es súper lindo. Uno se enorgullece, porque sientes que estás representando a tu país a través de la comida. Se siente bien. English Translation: When we cook, we always want to showcase what Chile is. Since there’s nowhere else that does it, we want to introduce, for example, the empanada de pino, which is a Chilean classic. To bring out everything that is Chile. In the south the food is delicious, in the north, in the center… and people don’t know it. Sometimes they ask you: “But Chilean food? (711, '5d9b9fe1-c88e-496b-8aa2-332555576e7f', '<strong>Jorge Ramirez</strong><br>\n<em>(Guadalajara, Mexico)</em>', '“I think it was probably a little after high school—around 17 or 18. I never really thought of myself as, ‘Oh yeah, I’m an immigrant.’ But it felt like I didn’t belong here—and I felt like I can’t go back to where I belong. So I was in this weird in-between. People talk about working in kitchens and there is this big sense of camaraderie, but on top of that, we’re all from the same struggle. There’s a deeper layer of camaraderie there, because we all come from the same thing. (712, 'b795b22a-bce3-41ed-840d-ccb764db961e', '<strong>Milagros Vega Valverde</strong><br>\n<em>(Lima, Peru)</em>', '“Conozco a una señora y le cuento mi situación. Me dice: ‘Sabes qué, Erika, aquí hay una iglesia, hay una institución donde dan comida gratis.’ Me llevó, me inscribió, me dio la mano. Con la comida que ellos me habían regalado, yo de agradecimiento, preparé un buffet en mi casa y lo llevé. Les encantó. Me dijeron: ‘Sabes qué, vamos a hacer otro evento y te vamos a contratar para que puedas vender tu comida y todos puedan conocer tu comida.’ Me motiva el querer hacer conocer mis raíces. Estaba un poco preocupada porque no sabía dónde encontrar los condimentos y los productos peruanos. Hasta que en los mercados mexicanos encontré algunos productos. Entonces dije: ‘No. Yo tengo que cocinar mi comida y que la gente la conozca.’ El desafío me motivó a que las personas prueben mi comida con mi cultura. También empecé a fusionar con la comida mexicana, con la americana. Y es lo que me motiva, que la gente sepa que se pueden hacer muchas cosas con la comida, con mis raíces, con mis condimentos y con los productos peruanos que están aquí en Estados Unidos.” English Translation: I met a woman and told her about my situation. She said, “You know what, Erika, there’s a church here, an organization where they give out free food.” She took me there, registered me, and gave me a hand. With the food they had given me, out of gratitude I prepared a buffet at home and brought it over. They loved it. They said, “You know what, we’re going to do another event and we’re going to hire you so you can sell your food and everyone can get to know your food.”\nI’m motivated by wanting to make my roots known. I was a little worried because I didn’t know where to find Peruvian seasonings and products—until I found some items at Mexican markets. Then I said, “No. I have to cook my food so people can get to know it.” The challenge motivated me to have people try my food with my culture. I also started fusing it with Mexican food and with American food. And that’s what motivates me—that people know there’s so much you can do with food, with my roots, with my seasonings, and with the Peruvian products that are here in the United States.', NULL, 0, NULL, NULL, NULL, 'video', NULL, 'moving image', NULL, NULL, '1_3f1y4jme', 'kaltura', NULL, NULL, '1_3f1y4jme', NULL, '[\"3c654572-bca2-425c-ae1e-206223d25b51\"]', NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, '{\"@context\":\"http://iiif.io/api/presentation/3/context.json\",\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/b795b22a-bce3-41ed-840d-ccb764db961e/manifest\",\"type\":\"Manifest\",\"label\":{\"en\":[\"<strong>Milagros Vega Valverde</strong><br>\\n<em>(Lima, Peru)</em>\"]},\"metadata\":[{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Title\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"<strong>Milagros Vega Valverde</strong><br>\\n<em>(Lima, Peru)</em>\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Description\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"“Conozco a una señora y le cuento mi situación. Me dice: ‘Sabes qué, Erika, aquí hay una iglesia, hay una institución donde dan comida gratis.’ Me llevó, me inscribió, me dio la mano. Con la comida que ellos me habían regalado, yo de agradecimiento, preparé un buffet en mi casa y lo llevé. Les encantó. Me dijeron: ‘Sabes qué, vamos a hacer otro evento y te vamos a contratar para que puedas vender tu comida y todos puedan conocer tu comida.’ Me motiva el querer hacer conocer mis raíces. Estaba un poco preocupada porque no sabía dónde encontrar los condimentos y los productos peruanos. Hasta que en los mercados mexicanos encontré algunos productos. Entonces dije: ‘No. Yo tengo que cocinar mi comida y que la gente la conozca.’ El desafío me motivó a que las personas prueben mi comida con mi cultura. También empecé a fusionar con la comida mexicana, con la americana. Y es lo que me motiva, que la gente sepa que se pueden hacer muchas cosas con la comida, con mis raíces, con mis condimentos y con los productos peruanos que están aquí en Estados Unidos.” English Translation: I met a woman and told her about my situation. She said, “You know what, Erika, there’s a church here, an organization where they give out free food.” She took me there, registered me, and gave me a hand. With the food they had given me, out of gratitude I prepared a buffet at home and brought it over. They loved it. They said, “You know what, we’re going to do another event and we’re going to hire you so you can sell your food and everyone can get to know your food.”\\nI’m motivated by wanting to make my roots known. I was a little worried because I didn’t know where to find Peruvian seasonings and products—until I found some items at Mexican markets. Then I said, “No. I have to cook my food so people can get to know it.” The challenge motivated me to have people try my food with my culture. I also started fusing it with Mexican food and with American food. And that’s what motivates me—that people know there’s so much you can do with food, with my roots, with my seasonings, and with the Peruvian products that are here in the United States.\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Format\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"moving image\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Media Type\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"video\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Kaltura Entry ID\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"1_3f1y4jme\"]}}],\"requiredStatement\":{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Attribution\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"University of Denver\"]}},\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/b795b22a-bce3-41ed-840d-ccb764db961e/manifest/canvas/1\",\"type\":\"Canvas\",\"width\":1920,\"height\":1080,\"duration\":0,\"label\":{\"en\":[\"<strong>Milagros Vega Valverde</strong><br>\\n<em>(Lima, Peru)</em>\"]},\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/b795b22a-bce3-41ed-840d-ccb764db961e/manifest/canvas/1/page\",\"type\":\"AnnotationPage\",\"items\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/b795b22a-bce3-41ed-840d-ccb764db961e/manifest/canvas/1/page/annotation\",\"type\":\"Annotation\",\"motivation\":\"painting\",\"body\":{\"id\":\"https://cdnapisec.kaltura.com/p/2357732/sp/235773200/playManifest/entryId/1_3f1y4jme/format/applehttp/protocol/https\",\"type\":\"Video\",\"format\":\"video/mp4\",\"width\":1920,\"height\":1080,\"duration\":0},\"target\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/b795b22a-bce3-41ed-840d-ccb764db961e/manifest/canvas/1\"}]}],\"rendering\":[{\"id\":\"https://cdnapisec.kaltura.com/p/2357732/sp/235773200/embedIframeJs/uiconf_id/41433762/partner_id/2357732?iframeembed=true&entry_id=1_3f1y4jme\",\"type\":\"Video\",\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Kaltura Player\"]},\"format\":\"text/html\"}]}],\"summary\":{\"en\":[\"“Conozco a una señora y le cuento mi situación. (713, 'c2046ec0-23ec-4d8f-9527-46f7f3349a76', '<strong>Manny Barella</strong><br>\n<em>(Monterrey, Mexico)</em>', '“Quiero que la gente pruebe y decir: ‘Ah, me recuerda a cuando estaba en Monterrey y comía eso.’  Tengo un postre, el Carlota de limón, por ejemplo. Era el postre que cuando estaba en la prepa y hacíamos fiestas, había como cinco versiones del mismo postre. Como los brownies, haz de cuenta. Todo el mundo hacía brownies. Todo el mundo hacía Carlota. Y me ha pasado, cuando tenía mi restaurante de cocina mexicana, hace varios años, gente de Monterrey que iba porque escuchaban que el chef era de Monterrey. Y aprovechaban, ‘pues, déjame ir a ver.’ Probaban la barbacoa, probaban el carlota, y decían: ‘Es que transporté a cuando estaba en Monterrey.’ Y para mí, esa es la frase que estoy buscando: que la gente diga ‘me transportaste a México.’ No quiere decir que todos los platillos van a ser exitosos desde esa perspectiva, pero esa es la meta. Que alguien pueda probar algo y decir: ‘Ah, sí, sabe como cuando estuve en Guanajuato y probé esto,’ o ‘sabe como cuando estuve en Oaxaca y probé un mole…’ Eso, para mí, es la meta: que la gente viaje a través de mi comida de la misma manera que yo he viajado a través de la comida.” English Translation: I want people to taste it and say, “Ah, this reminds me of when I was in Monterrey and ate this.” I have a dessert—Carlota de limón, for example. Back in high school, whenever we had parties, there were like five versions of the same dessert. Like brownies, you know. Everyone made brownies. Everyone made Carlota.\nAnd it’s happened that, when I had my Mexican restaurant years ago, people from Monterrey would come because they’d heard the chef was from Monterrey. They’d think, “Well, let me go check it out.” They’d try the barbacoa, they’d try the carlota, and they’d say, “It transported me back to when I was in Monterrey.” And for me, that’s the phrase I’m looking for: for people to say, “You transported me to Mexico.” It doesn’t mean every dish will be successful from that perspective, but that’s the goal: that someone can taste something and say, “Ah, yes, it tastes like when I was in Guanajuato and I tried this,” or “it tastes like when I was in Oaxaca and I tried a mole…” That, for me, is the goal: that people travel through my food in the same way I have traveled through food.', NULL, 0, NULL, NULL, NULL, 'video', NULL, 'moving image', NULL, NULL, '1_oisgnnuw', 'kaltura', NULL, NULL, '1_oisgnnuw', NULL, '[\"3c654572-bca2-425c-ae1e-206223d25b51\"]', NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, '{\"@context\":\"http://iiif.io/api/presentation/3/context.json\",\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/c2046ec0-23ec-4d8f-9527-46f7f3349a76/manifest\",\"type\":\"Manifest\",\"label\":{\"en\":[\"<strong>Manny Barella</strong><br>\\n<em>(Monterrey, Mexico)</em>\"]},\"metadata\":[{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Title\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"<strong>Manny Barella</strong><br>\\n<em>(Monterrey, Mexico)</em>\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Description\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"“Quiero que la gente pruebe y decir: ‘Ah, me recuerda a cuando estaba en Monterrey y comía eso.’  Tengo un postre, el Carlota de limón, por ejemplo. (714, 'b0a15f85-fb2f-4924-82b7-0b408d592e35', '<strong>Dana Rodriguez</strong><br>\n<em>(Chihuahua, Mexico)</em>', '“Yo me acuerdo de mi papá y mi mamá. Vivíamos en una casa chiquita. A veces, hacía mi mamá hacía un guisado —o no sé, mi papá, que era el que cocinaba más. Las tortillas, un caldo de pollo, un caldo de res, un pozole. Y había como veinte personas comiendo ahí.  Dije: ‘¿Cómo nunca se les acaba la comida con una cazuelita que tienen?’ Y todos se van contentos. Es algo que traes desde cuando creciste: el siempre ser parte del dar. Yo soy parte de 30 nonprofits en Colorado. Yo dono mi tiempo. Yo dono mi cocina. Mi gente me ayuda a cocinar. Y así yo veo que es la cocina. So, la comida es lo que te ayuda, es a, a lograr todo lo que tú no puedes lograr por ti misma. La comida lo hace por ti.” English Translation: I remember my mom and dad. We lived in a tiny house. Sometimes my mom would make a stew—or maybe my dad, who was the one who cooked more. Tortillas, a chicken soup, a beef soup, a pozole. And there would be about twenty people eating there. I’d think, “How does the food never run out with that little pot they have?” And everyone would leave happy. It’s something you carry from how you grew up: always being part of giving. I’m involved with 30 nonprofits in Colorado. I donate my time. I donate my kitchen. My team helps me cook. And that’s how I see cooking. (715, '95726adb-30e2-454e-8ebd-db1706baf1a6', '<strong>Carlos Blanco</strong><br>\n<em>(San Salvador, El Salvador)</em>', '“El inmigrante no es lo que la mayoría piensa. Porque la mayoría piensa que el Latino es malo. Yo les he demostrado con la comida que hay cultura, que hay producción, que hay talento. Yo soy de El Salvador. Entonces, pues, yo les estoy demostrando que hay comida buena, como en México, como aquí también. Porque sé cocinar las tres comidas. Cocino comida salvadoreña, mexicana y americana. Los inmigrantes venimos a trabajar, no a aprovecharnos del país. Queremos salir adelante con nuestros propios esfuerzos. Y en el food truck, la gente se sienta, platica, se conocen. Eso es lo que estamos tratando de hacer. Nosotros estamos trabajando para nosotros, con nuestro negocito. Y tener la comunidad unida. A veces llegan de diferentes nacionalidades. Ahi se sientan a la mesa, se unen y se ponen a platicar. Eso a veces se está perdiendo, la comunidad. Y eso es lo que estamos tratando: de servirle a la gente un plato de comida para que pasen a gusto y platiquen una o dos horas. Eso es lo que tratamos de hacer con la comunidad. El cocinar es demostrar que hay talento en El Salvador. Porque la gente escucha ‘El Salvador’ y piensa en las maras, piensa en cosas malas. Pero hay gente trabajadora, gente buena allá. Con talento. Nomás hay que darles oportunidades.” English Translation: The immigrant is not what most people think. Because most people think Latinos are bad. I’ve shown them through food that there is culture, that we produce, that there is talent. I’m from El Salvador. So I’m showing them that there’s good food, just like in Mexico, and here too. Because I know how to cook all three cuisines. I cook Salvadoran, Mexican, and American food. Immigrants come to work, not to take advantage of the country. We want to get ahead through our own efforts. And at the food truck, people sit down, talk, get to know each other. That’s what we’re trying to do. We’re working for ourselves, with our little business. And to bring the community together. Sometimes people from different nationalities show up. They sit at the table, come together, and start chatting. That sense of community is sometimes being lost. And that’s what we’re trying to do: serve people a plate of food so they can relax and chat for an hour or two. That’s what we try to do with the community. Cooking is about showing that there’s talent in El Salvador. Because people hear “El Salvador” and think of gangs, think of bad things. But there are hardworking people, good people there. With talent. (717, '09a4b0ce-9d9d-40f3-9727-7eb429942a2b', '<strong>Jesús Ramón Lizarraga</strong><br>\n<em>(Mazatlán, Mexico)</em>', '“Suena muy ambicioso. Fuera de mi esposa y mis suegros, que son nuestros incondicionales, los que más nos apoyan… Fuera de ellos, nunca le he platicado a nadie los planes que tengo para mi negocio en mi cabeza. Yo he agarrado a mi hijo mayor, que tiene 14 años, y lo que yo le digo a mi esposa es que esto rompe completamente la cadena de carencias que traíamos —y si así lo quieres llamar, que así sea—, rompe esa cadena y comienza algo diferente. A mí me gusta decir que es un legado. Me gustaría dejar un legado para mi familia. Y yo le digo a mi hijo mayor: ‘Mira, cuando yo llegué a este país, no traía dinero. Llegué con una deuda de mil dólares, que fue lo que me costó venir para acá.’ Después me di cuenta que…pagas esos mil dólares, y ahora tienes mil dólares más… pasaste de negativo a positivo. Mil dólares tal vez no representan mucho, pero la ideología que te ponen en la cabeza tiene mucho que ver. Si ya tuviste mil dólares en tu bolsa después de estar en negativo, ¿quién dice que no puedes tener diez mil? Y si consigues diez mil, ¿qué te dice que no puedes conseguir cien mil o un millón? Estás en un país que te da la oportunidad de trabajar duro y de tener buenos resultados. Lo que yo le comento a mi hijo es que las oportunidades que él tiene ahora son muy diferentes a las que yo tenía. ‘A ti ya no te toca comenzar desde negativo. Tal vez empiezas desde dos, tres, cuatro… y puedes llegar hasta el diez.’” English Translation: It sounds very ambitious. Aside from my wife and my in-laws, who are our unconditional supporters, the ones who back us the most… aside from them, I’ve never told anyone the plans I have in my head for my business. I’ve taken my oldest son, who is 14, and what I tell my wife is that this completely breaks the chain of scarcity we carried—and if that’s what you want to call it, so be it—breaks that chain and starts something different. I like to say it’s a legacy. I’d like to leave a legacy for my family. And I tell my oldest son: “Look, when I came to this country, I didn’t have money. I arrived with a debt of a thousand dollars—that’s what it cost me to come here.” Later I realized that… you pay those thousand dollars, and now you have another thousand dollars… you’ve gone from negative to positive. A thousand dollars might not mean much, but the mindset you put in your head matters a lot. If you’ve already had a thousand dollars in your pocket after being in the red, who says you can’t have ten thousand? And if you get ten thousand, what says you can’t get a hundred thousand or a million? You’re in a country that gives you the opportunity to work hard and see good results. What I tell my son is that the opportunities he has now are very different from the ones I had. “You don’t have to start from the negative anymore. Maybe you start at two, three, four… and you can get to ten.”', NULL, 0, NULL, NULL, NULL, 'video', NULL, 'moving image', NULL, NULL, '1_3gvp8npz', 'kaltura', NULL, NULL, '1_3gvp8npz', NULL, '[\"3c654572-bca2-425c-ae1e-206223d25b51\"]', NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, '{\"@context\":\"http://iiif.io/api/presentation/3/context.json\",\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/09a4b0ce-9d9d-40f3-9727-7eb429942a2b/manifest\",\"type\":\"Manifest\",\"label\":{\"en\":[\"<strong>Jesús Ramón Lizarraga</strong><br>\\n<em>(Mazatlán, Mexico)</em>\"]},\"metadata\":[{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Title\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"<strong>Jesús Ramón Lizarraga</strong><br>\\n<em>(Mazatlán, Mexico)</em>\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Description\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"“Suena muy ambicioso. Llegué con una deuda de mil dólares, que fue lo que me costó venir para acá.’ Después me di cuenta que…pagas esos mil dólares, y ahora tienes mil dólares más… pasaste de negativo a positivo. Tal vez empiezas desde dos, tres, cuatro… y puedes llegar hasta el diez.’” English Translation: It sounds very ambitious. Aside from my wife and my in-laws, who are our unconditional supporters, the ones who back us the most… aside from them, I’ve never told anyone the plans I have in my head for my business. I’ve taken my oldest son, who is 14, and what I tell my wife is that this completely breaks the chain of scarcity we carried—and if that’s what you want to call it, so be it—breaks that chain and starts something different. I like to say it’s a legacy. I’d like to leave a legacy for my family. And I tell my oldest son: “Look, when I came to this country, I didn’t have money. I arrived with a debt of a thousand dollars—that’s what it cost me to come here.” Later I realized that… you pay those thousand dollars, and now you have another thousand dollars… you’ve gone from negative to positive. A thousand dollars might not mean much, but the mindset you put in your head matters a lot. If you’ve already had a thousand dollars in your pocket after being in the red, who says you can’t have ten thousand? And if you get ten thousand, what says you can’t get a hundred thousand or a million? You’re in a country that gives you the opportunity to work hard and see good results. What I tell my son is that the opportunities he has now are very different from the ones I had. “You don’t have to start from the negative anymore. Maybe you start at two, three, four… and you can get to ten.”\"]},\"thumbnail\":[{\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/09a4b0ce-9d9d-40f3-9727-7eb429942a2b/full/!400,400/0/default.jpg\",\"type\":\"Image\",\"format\":\"image/jpeg\",\"width\":400,\"height\":400}]}', 0, 5, '2025-09-23 13:11:23', '2026-04-14 19:21:52', 'Madison Sussmann', 'migration_script'),
(718, 'bd051f81-7ea3-44da-8aa7-30b6ca8d54cf', '<strong>Edwin Sandoval</strong><br><em>(El Progreso, Honduras)</em>', '“Cuando me pongo a pensar en mis años, cuando tenía 12, 13 años, creo que me hubiese gustado tener a alguien que me hubiera dicho: ‘No tienes que cambiar quién eres para poder ser parte de algo.’ A veces—y hay un dicho en español que dice ‘mejor solo que mal acompañado’—entonces creo que en mis años creciendo, ese dicho se me ha pegado un poco más. Y creo que si yo le pudiera decir algo a esa persona de 12, 13 años es: ‘No tienes que ser otra persona. Tú eres quien eres y eres excepcional. No tienes que cambiar tu cultura, tu lenguaje, tu forma de vestir, lo que comes, para ser otra persona.’ Y creo que ahora, siendo una persona más mayor, ¿no? La cocina me ha dado esa esencia de orgullo, de decir: ‘Mira, esta es la comida que yo como. Ahora quiero compartirla con otras personas.’ Porque otra vez, no es solo comida, es una forma de darle una vista a las personas, de decir: ‘Ah, ¿pero por qué te gusta comer arroz y frijoles y plátano?’ — ‘Ah, pues mira, esta es la historia por la cual.’ Y hacerlo ahora, ahora no es una necesidad, ahora es un gusto. Es como quien dice una forma de recordarme de esos momentos, que no necesariamente eran los mejores… pero como quien dice, probar esos sabores otra vez y decir: ‘Wow, la realidad es diferente ahora.’” Englsih Translation: When I start to think about my years, when I was 12, 13 years old, I think I would have liked to have someone who told me: “You don’t have to change who you are in order to be part of something.” Sometimes—and there’s a saying in Spanish that goes “better alone than in bad company”—so I think that in my years growing up, that saying stuck with me a little more. And I think that if I could tell something to that 12- or 13-year-old person it would be: “You don’t have to be another person. You are who you are and you are exceptional. You don’t have to change your culture, your language, your way of dressing, what you eat, in order to be another person.” And I think that now, being an older person, right? Cooking has given me that essence of pride, of saying: “Look, this is the food that I eat. Now I want to share it with other people.” Because again, it’s not only food, it’s a way of giving people a view, of saying: “Ah, but why do you like to eat rice and beans and plantain?” — “Ah, well look, this is the story why.” And doing it now, now it isn’t a necessity, now it’s a pleasure. It’s like they say, a way of reminding myself of those moments, which weren’t necessarily the best… but like they say, to taste those flavors again and say: “Wow, reality is different now.”', NULL, 0, '', 'Albums (books)', '', 'video', NULL, 'moving image', NULL, NULL, '1_ewyekww5', 'kaltura', NULL, NULL, '1_ewyekww5', NULL, '[\"3c654572-bca2-425c-ae1e-206223d25b51\"]', NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, NULL, '{\"@context\":\"http://iiif.io/api/presentation/3/context.json\",\"id\":\"https://exhibits.dev/exhibits-dashboard/iiif/bd051f81-7ea3-44da-8aa7-30b6ca8d54cf/manifest\",\"type\":\"Manifest\",\"label\":{\"en\":[\"<strong>Edwin Sandoval</strong><br><em>(El Progreso, Honduras)</em>\"]},\"metadata\":[{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Title\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"<strong>Edwin Sandoval</strong><br><em>(El Progreso, Honduras)</em>\"]}},{\"label\":{\"en\":[\"Description\"]},\"value\":{\"en\":[\"“Cuando me pongo a pensar en mis años, cuando tenía 12, 13 años, creo que me hubiese gustado tener a alguien que me hubiera dicho: ‘No tienes que cambiar quién eres para poder ser parte de algo.’ A veces—y hay un dicho en español que dice ‘mejor solo que mal acompañado’—entonces creo que en mis años creciendo, ese dicho se me ha pegado un poco más. You don’t have to change your culture, your language, your way of dressing, what you eat, in order to be another person.” And I think that now, being an older person, right? Cooking has given me that essence of pride, of saying: “Look, this is the food that I eat. Now I want to share it with other people.” Because again, it’s not only food, it’s a way of giving people a view, of saying: “Ah, but why do you like to eat rice and beans and plantain?” — “Ah, well look, this is the story why.” And doing it now, now it isn’t a necessity, now it’s a pleasure. As a young man, Max left his hometown to study medicine at the University of Munich in Germany. Beginning in 1908, he served as a ship\'s doctor in the Middle East, Africa, and throughout the Far East. In 1910, he moved to Berlin, Germany to serve as Assistant Doctor at the Berlin-Moabit City hospital and later as a doctor at Berlin\'s Charité Hospital.</p>\n<p>Max submitted his dissertation, \"Diagnosis of Children\'s Leukemia,\" on May 8, 1913 to the Friedrich-Wilhelm University in Berlin and received his medical degree. During World War I, Max served as a doctor for four years with the rank of a commissioned officer. He cared for German troops on the front lines, on both the Eastern and Western fronts in Galicia and France. Max was awarded the Iron Cross First Class for his bravery. In 1919 he settled in Berlin, where he specialized in dermatology and venereal disease.</p>\n<p>It was in Berlin that Max met Maria Steinberg (née Bäetge), a widowed artist raising a daughter, Karin, from her first marriage. Max and Maria married in 1925, had a son Henry (Heinrich), and built a life together. The two remained in Berlin throughout World War II, surviving the Holocaust and several attempts to transport Max to Eastern death camps.</p>\n<p>After the War ended, the couple and Karin immigrated to the United States in 1946, making their new home in Williamsport, Pennsylvania. He served in the Russian army as an architect designing buildings on the Sweaborg Island Fortress in Helsinki\'s harbor. Maria and Erich were married in 1914, and Maria gave birth to their only child, Karin, in Helsinki, Finland in 1915. In 1917, as the Russian Revolution began, the Steinberg family escaped to Germany. When Erich died in 1920, Maria moved to Berlin where she could support herself and Karin working as an artist and designer. Maria met Dr. Max Loewenstein and they were married in January of 1925. Maria and her daughter Karin suffered persecution and the dangers of living in a Jewish household during World War II.</p>\n<p>After the war, the Loewensteins immigrated to the United States. However, she made a point of refusing to change the obviously Jewish last name that had made her the subject of persecution. She said, “I have suffered with this name, and I am proud of it.” She became a well-known artist and teacher in both Pennsylvania and Colorado. (12, '3b64d4f6-9ca7-4db9-8409-d0957285dea0', 'bea561ce-5746-4adb-b7d2-b9f997fbcd37', '', NULL, '', 'Identification card issued by the Polizeipräsident in Berlin, the Gestapo, to Heinrich Loewenstein [Henry Loewenstein] in Berlin, Germany, March 31, 1939.', 'image', 'image&#x2F;jpeg', '3b64d4f6-9ca7-4db9-8409-d0957285dea0_1740000185532_item_media.jpg', '3f81cbcc-5b96-4241-b9c5-d8ea0638529e', '<p>Nazi violence and persecution had escalated since Hitler\'s rise to power in 1933. Kristallnacht (November 9-10, 1938) made it clear that Jews in Germany were doomed.</p>\n<p>Following Kristallnacht, Jews were required to carry identity cards issued by the Gestapo. Henry later recalled that this was the moment he truly realized the hopelessness of the family\'s situation.</p>\n<p>Nazi violence and persecution had escalated since Hitler\'s rise to power in 1933. Kristallnacht (November 9-10, 1938) made it clear that Jews in Germany were doomed. The Loewensteins fled their home, taking shelter at the apartment of Max\'s brother Georg, who had already been seized by the Nazis. Two days later, Henry went back to check if the Nazis had been in the apartment. Then, with his mother, loaded a box with the family silver and tried to leave it with Aryan friends until things calmed down. “We pushed the box around on my bike for several hours, but no one wanted to open their door for us, being more concerned with their own safety,” recalled Henry.</p>\n<p>Following Kristallnacht, Jews were required to carry identity cards issued by the Gestapo. Dr. Max Loewenstein and Henry were issued their cards at Gestapo headquarters on March 31, 1939 in Berlin. For Henry it was a significant moment as he watched his father, who had always been a commanding presence, endure the insults screamed at him by the Gestapo\'s bureaucrats. Henry later recalled that this was the moment he truly realized the hopelessness of the family\'s situation.</p>\n<p>Jews, desperate to leave Germany, Austria, and occupied Czechoslovakia, were only allowed to take a very small amount of money out of the country. Other countries, still struggling to feed their own people at the end of the Great Depression, could not afford to take these penniless refugees. Henry\'s cousin, Ingrid Lind, was lucky enough to have Danish citizenship and escaped to Denmark. When the Germans occupied Denmark in 1940, she escaped alone to Sweden and Finland. At age nineteen, she crossed Russia into the Middle East on foot, where she joined the British Army.</p>\n<p>Max had previously applied for visas to the United States and investigated other emigration options for the family, but nothing appeared to be possible for at least another three years. Jews were not likely to be able to survive that long under Nazi rule. The family barely survived in Berlin until the end of World War II.</p>\n<p>Henry was able to escape to England through a British refugee program known as the Kindertransport.</p>', 1, NULL, 'item', 'media_right', 50, 1, NULL, 0, 1, NULL, 'item1', 16, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, NULL, 0, 8, '2025-01-13 16:19:12', '2026-04-17 18:53:52', 'Madison Sussmann', NULL),

Taken in May 1939, weeks before Heinrich left on the Kindertransport.', 'image', 'image&#x2F;tiff', '1b09177b-b149-4214-af98-f3b7f3af2ff0', 'aa3d710f-1d69-467e-87b0-13fa3b45b7b0', '<p>In early February 1939, Herr Rosenberg, one of the few teachers still at Wilsnacker Strasse Jewish School, asked Henry to stay after class. He told Henry that he knew of a committee that would sponsor one boy for admittance to England. Kristallnacht had prompted a wave of British sympathy for the suffering of the Jews under Nazi rule, and refugee aid groups had successfully persuaded Parliament to admit an unspecified number of Jewish children, if a £50 bond was posted for each child. Between December 1, 1938 and September 1, 1939, a series of trains, ships, and planes brought approximately 10,000 Jewish children into England. The program eventually became known as the Kindertransport.</p>\n<p>Henry\'s mother Maria applied immediately, and received a letter a few weeks later announcing Henry\'s acceptance into this program. It took several months for the committee to handle all the paperwork with both British and German governments, but on May 29th the Loewensteins received a letter with Henry\'s travel date - June 5, 1939. Thirteen year old Henry and his family knew that his move to England would save his life, but it also meant a wrenching separation from his family. Max and Maria hoped that it would not be long before the whole family could leave Germany. However, the war broke out within 12 weeks and the family would be separated for eight years, the only communication coming infrequently through the Red Cross.</p>\n<p>Henry spent two months in a refugee camp, then briefly attended a school in London, before being evacuated with all of London\'s children into the countryside on the eve of war. He ended up in Whipsnade, a beautiful English village, adjacent to a branch of the London Zoo, where he soon found part time work while attending the village\'s one room school. Later he worked on a farm and served with the British Home Guard until the end of the war.</p>\n<p>A plaque, thanking the British Parliament for enabling 10,000 Jewish children to escape to England in 1939, was donated in 1999 by Kindertransport survivors and is prominently mounted adjacent to the entrance of the House of Commons.</p>', 1, 'In 1939, thirteen-year-old Henry escaped Nazi Germany through the Kindertransport, a British effort that saved about 10,000 Jewish children, but left him separated from his family for eight years. They sent their eldest child, Erna to the France on March 30, 1939 when she was just 15 years old. She later moved to the Netherlands. After the Netherlands fell to Germany, Erna was on one of the first transports to the Auschwitz Concentration Camp and was murdered there in 1942. In 1943, Ernst and Elisabeth were transported to the Theresienstadt Concentration Camp. Their young son Walter (age 13) would be sent there too about six months later.</p>\n<p>Karin and Maria attempted to relieve the Eylenburgs’ suffering by sending packages of food and personal care items. Doctors Ernst and Elisabeth replied with three pre-printed cards of thanks, the only form of communication allowed the prisoners of Theresienstadt. Due to examination by prison guards, each card is postmarked several days or weeks after the date of the card.</p>\n<p>In August 1944, Dr. Elisabeth found a way to smuggle out an actual message to the Loewensteins. In it, she thanks them for their package and manages to sound positive, reporting that her husband is well and her son has grown almost as tall as his father. That message, dated August 5, was written in response to a package received on July 11, and is postmarked August 31. The final card received from the Eylenburgs, is signed in a weak hand by Elisabeth and dated September 22, 1944. This was the last the Loewensteins heard from them.</p>\n<p>Dr. Ernst and Walter Eylenburg were taken to the Auschwitz Death Camp and, according to the Central Database of Shoah Victims\' names, murdered soon thereafter. Miraculously, they all survived until May 7, 1945, when Germany finally surrendered and the war came to an end.</p>\n\n\n<p>World War II began with Germany’s attack on Poland on September 1, 1939, after which point it became almost impossible for German Jews to emigrate and even that stopped completely in October 1941. After that date, few Jews in German-occupied areas escaped with their lives. Max, Maria, and Karin remained in Berlin for the rest of the war – Maria’s courage saved the family from deportation and certain death on numerous occasions - miraculously, they all survived.</p>\n\n\n<p>Loewensteins’ Jewish family and friends were taken to ghettoes and concentration camps where they were killed.</p>\n\n\n<p>The Loewensteins’ home was classified as a “Jewish household” and as a result, the family suffered the effects of anti-Jewish German measures.</p>\n\n\n<p>Maria’s status as an Aryan, as well as her bravery and wisdom, saved Max many times. Most notably she and Karin joined the only successful demonstration of Germans opposing the Nazi’s attempts to take Jews to their deaths, during what later became known as the \"Rosenstrasse Factory Event\". The deportation threat was always there, but when in March 1943, the British began their night time ”1,000 bomber” raids and the U.S. Eighth Air Force followed with massive daylight raids on Berlin, the Nazis were too busy to thoroughly follow-up with the deportations.</p>\n\n\n<p>The Russians battled their way into the ruins of Berlin in the winter and spring of 1945. The war had started and there was little hope that she would ever see her son again. She had been aware for some time that her husband, Dr. Max Loewenstein was addicted to morphine and she now insisted that something had to be done about it.</p>\r\n<p>Max’s addiction began as the result of a foot injury he sustained in 1933 while hiking with friends in the Harz Mountains in Southern Germany. Over the years, the break continued to cause Max pain, and he became addicted to the morphine. Maria had wanted Max committed to a drug rehabilitation facility for years.</p>\r\n<p>Finally, on September 25, 1941, Max agreed to admit himself to the Wittenauer Sanatorium in Berlin. It was then that fifteen top Nazis met to finalize plans for the systematic annihilation of the Jews with industrial efficiency.</p><p>Dr. Max Loewenstein was still in the Wittenauer Sanatorium when the major roundup of Berlin’s Jews began. Several thousand men, women, and children (including Max’s brother Georg and sister-in-law Alice) had been sent to the Łódź and Riga Ghettos, or taken to concentration camps. Construction of the death camps was almost complete, making it possible to murder many thousands each day. An incredibly evil and efficient method was now used to gather up Berlin’s Jews for transportation to the East.</p><p>After Kristallnacht, the Nazis had taken over Jewish cultural organizations, forcing them to do their “dirty” work. They now used these organizations\' staff and letterhead to send Nazi orders to Berlin’s Jews. In 1942, the Nazis used the Jüdische Kultusvereinigung zu Berlin e.V., one such organization, to send “Eviction Notices” to every Jewish household in Berlin, including the Loewensteins.</p><p>Everyone in the household, without exception, was ordered to appear at a designated time and location. An estimated 60,000 Jews who obeyed the order to appear were packed in cattle cars under the most inhumane conditions and sent to the death camps to be gassed. This “notice” was, in effect, a death warrant. This document is extremely rare – almost everyone who received it was sent to the Auschwitz death camp. Those who dared to ignore it would not have carried this order with them, since that alone would have meant instant death if caught.</p><p>When Maria received the order, she pretended not to know where Max was, refused to vacate the apartment, and appealed the order to the local Building Department. She produced transcripts of her parents’ marriage certificate, her birth and baptismal records to prove her Aryan heritage and receipts and bills to prove residence at her address. The order was temporarily rescinded, most likely the only time that happened.</p><p>Max was discharged from the Wittenauer Sanatorium in April 1942. Thanks to Maria’s courage and quick thinking Max had escaped deportation.</p><p>Max had to report to the police after leaving the Wittenauer Sanatorium, and both he and Maria were assigned to work at forced labor. Maria had to work a twelve-hour night shift sewing military uniforms, and Max was made to work twelve hours each day, sorting shoes and other personal possessions that came back from the death camps by freight car loads.</p><p>Karin continued to work for an insurance company during the war until air raids and the Russians advances made that impossible.</p>', 1, '', 'item', 'media_right', 50, 1, NULL, 0, 1, NULL, 'item1', 40, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, NULL, 0, 8, '2025-01-15 13:27:02', '2026-04-17 18:53:52', 'Madison Sussmann', 'Fernando Reyes'),

The soldiers had never seen such a car and made the chauffer drive them around to show off the limousine to their comrades, enjoying a drink at every stop. Fortunately at one stop the soldier left behind to guard Karin got annoyed when his buddies did not come out and he went looking for them. Karin’s driver seized this opportunity and drove immediately to her American friends.</p>\r\n<p>It turned out that the information Karin had passed on, had been leaked and was the front page story in the morning newspaper. A secretary, who worked with Karin, but had known nothing of Karin’s actions, had already been arrested and executed.</p>\r\n<p>Once again the Loewenstein family was in danger, but this time they had powerful allies. The Americans speeded up the immigration paperwork for Karin, Maria, and Max and protected them until they could depart for safety in the United States.</p>', 0, NULL, 'item', 'media_right', 50, 1, NULL, 0, 1, NULL, 'item1', 65, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, NULL, 0, 8, '2025-01-15 17:56:09', '2026-04-17 18:53:52', 'Madison Sussmann', NULL),

Due to the circumstances of their departure, his family could not inform Henry of their emigration until they were sailing through the English Channel when they sent him a telegram from the ship. Settler-colonialism as a system has its goal to eliminate (typically through genocide), replace, and/or subjugate the communities indigenous to the land that the colonizers inhabit; this system is how the United States was \"settled.\" </p><p>The state of Colorado saw one of the most brutal examples of settler colonialism in the Sand Creek Massacre, which took place in November 1864, just months after the University of Denver was founded. This image and the rest of the exhibit show that the \"Pioneer\" nickname, used since 1925, has been associated with the glorification of settler colonialism and related cultural appropriation of images of Native peoples.</p><p>Other schools in the American West with similar mascot and nickname histories have changed their mascots in response to student protest and engagement. In 2016, the student newspaper of Whitman College (founded 1859) in Walla Walla, Washington changed their name from \"The Pioneer\" to \"The Whitman Wire.\" The editors wrote, \"At the time the paper was founded, the college was in the midst of reinforcing a telling of history that celebrated the arrival of white invaders–pioneers–to the Columbia Plateau. According to this story of the pioneers, they arrived in an empty and savage land. In reality, they were coming into the homelands of the Cayuse, Walla Walla, Umatilla and other peoples who have inhabited this land for as long as any can remember.\" (Johnson, Cornett, and Clay, 2016). The California State University campus at Long Beach removed a statue of \"Prospector Pete\" from the courtyard (Del Real, 2018). Another campus of California State University at East Bay removed Pioneer Pete as their mascot in 2018. The University has, however, retained the \"pioneer\" moniker (Diaz, 2018).</p><p>While the Pioneer moniker persists, continued harm to the original inhabitants of the land that the University of Denver sits on, and all Indigenous peoples, is inevitable.</p>', 1, NULL, 'item', 'media_right', 50, 1, NULL, 0, 1, NULL, '{}', 5, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, NULL, 0, 8, '2025-01-22 19:35:13', '2026-04-14 21:27:12', 'Madison Sussmann', NULL),

Davis-Delano, Gone, and Fryberg (2020) explored the psychosocial effects of mascots on Native youth through a comprehensive review. (52, 'd5b80640-f03f-4000-8e3a-33cc141c23f7', '69a96c10-a37c-42e3-b31b-bc6c59ddea77', '', NULL, '', 'Painting by Maria Sibylla Merian. Image courtesy of the Biodiversity Heritage Library. Visit their website to see a  collection of works  by women in Natural History.', 'image', 'image&#x2F;jpeg', 'd5b80640-f03f-4000-8e3a-33cc141c23f7_1738705815736_item_media.jpg', '9ca4f505-5465-4cf8-8695-83d2a4998e1b', '<p>The first known botanical illustration is from approximately 70 CE when the Greek botanist Pedanius Dioscorides illustrated De Materia Media. His drawings helped readers identify medicinal plants. As printing presses advanced to include color, the practice grew and diversified. With the growing interest in botany in the eighteenth century, botanical illustration became professionalized. </p>\n\n<p>Few women botanical illustrators or natural scientists from the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries were widely recognized, credited, or published under their own names. However, women permeated the natural sciences, whether they worked as independent illustrators or uncredited artists. Maria Sybilla Merian and Anna Botsford Comstock, featured in this exhibit, are two women who broke the mold and earned widespread recognition for their work.</p>\n\n<p>Through time, the art of botanical illustration changed to widely accept women and various new styles and mediums. In a time of high-definition photography, illustrations are still used today as scientific renderings of botanicals because artists can emphasize elements for diagnostic or identification purposes. Botanical illustration has also expanded into the fields of fine art and fashion. As seen in the artist books by Deborah Bryan and Radha Pandey in this exhibit, botanical illustrations can be used to tell a story, share a perspective, or highlight an issue.</p><p>\n\n</p><p>This exhibit brings attention to women who <strong>succeeded</strong> in studying and practicing botanical art. The examples span the late 1600s to 2022, but this collection of books only partially represents women\'s impact in the botanical arts.\n</p>', 1, 'The first known botanical illustration dates to around 70 CE, when the Greek botanist Pedanius Dioscorides created drawings for De Materia Medica to help readers identify medicinal plants. As printing technology advanced to include color, botanical illustration expanded in both scope and popularity, and by the eighteenth century, amid growing interest in botany, the field became increasingly professionalized. Although few women botanical illustrators or natural scientists of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries were widely recognized or published under their own names, women were nonetheless deeply involved in the natural sciences as independent illustrators and often uncredited artists. Maria Sibylla Merian and Anna Botsford Comstock, featured in this exhibit, were among those who broke convention and achieved significant recognition for their work. Over time, botanical illustration evolved to more widely include women and to embrace new styles and media. Even in the age of high definition photography, illustration remains vital for scientific study because artists can emphasize diagnostic features for identification. The practice has also expanded into fine art and fashion, as seen in the artist books by Deborah Bryan and Radha Pandey included in this exhibit, where botanical imagery tells stories, expresses perspectives, and highlights critical issues. This exhibit brings attention to women who succeeded in studying and practicing botanical art, spanning the late seventeenth century to 2022, while acknowledging that this collection represents only part of women’s enduring impact on the botanical arts.', 'item', 'media_right', 50, 0, 'A color drawing of a brown patterned moth in the top right above a plant with an orange fruit and white flowers. She attended the newly opened Cornell University to study the natural sciences. At Cornell University, she met John Henry Comstock, an entomologist, and they later married and became partners. She illustrated many of his publications and co-authored A Manual for the Study of Insects in 1895.</p>\n\n<p>In 1897, Anna Botsford Comstock was hired as a professor of nature study at Cornell University. She was a part of the Nature Study Movement that sought to educate people in the natural sciences to encourage conservation. (69, 'd66ab629-f270-464e-b59b-2a3d9d7b89fa', '087b09e9-ab83-4599-bfe0-127d6fc459ee', '', NULL, '', 'Hanya Holm hikes in the Rocky Mountains, circa 1950', 'image', 'image&#x2F;tiff', '6ba18e61-674b-4abb-9827-e80476cb9446', 'c22dcc50-70e1-44f4-92ff-ec5cb23cbd38', '<p>Holm’s first visit to Colorado was in 1933, when she taught at the Perry Mansfield Dance Camp in Steamboat Springs, Colorado. This visit would prove to be significant - several of the students in that class (most significantly Martha Wilcox) would arrange, three years later, for the Denver debut of Holm\'s company. After 1941, Holm would spend the next forty-three summers teaching, working, and living in Colorado. In 1913, she climbed Mount Robson, the highest peak in the British Columbia Rockies, and in 1915 she married Charles Doolittle Walcott, a paleontologist and Secretary of the Smithsonian Institute. They met in the field while Walcott searched for invertebrate fossils in the Burgess Shale. Mary Vaux Walcott continued to travel to the Rocky Mountains every summer well into her old age.</p>\n\n<p>Between 1925 and 1928, Mary Vaux Walcott published four hundred drawings in five volumes of North American Wild Flowers. They were sold as part of her husband’s fund-raising campaign, and all proceeds went to the Smithsonian Institute’s endowment. Mount Mary Vaux in Jasper National Park in Alberta, Canada, was named in her honor.</p>:\n\n<p><b>Below is a <i>North American Wildflowers, Volume 1</i>, and six prints from the volume from DU Libraries\' Special Collections & Archives.</b> </p>', 1, NULL, 'item', 'text_only', 50, 1, NULL, 0, 1, NULL, '{}', 17, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, NULL, 1, 8, '2025-03-26 15:13:45', '2026-03-18 18:37:50', 'Madison Sussmann', NULL),
(90, '56759207-d13a-430c-ba7d-d1d306cd69cc', '69a96c10-a37c-42e3-b31b-bc6c59ddea77', '', NULL, '', '', 'text', '', '', NULL, '<p>Maria Sibylla Merian was born in Frankfort am Main, a German territory, in 1647. After the death of her father, Matthaus Merian, an owner of printing and publishing houses, her mother married her second husband, Jacob Marrell, a Dutch still-life painter. He inspired Merian to become a skilled still-life artist specializing in flowers. </p>\n\n<p>In 1665, Maria Sibylla Merian married Johann Andreas Graff, an architectural painter. In 1691, she, her husband, and her daughters, Johanna Helena and Dorothea Maria, settled in Amsterdam, The Netherlands, where Meria could own her own business selling artist supplies, mounted insects, and preserved animals. In 1714, Merian suffered a stroke, and in 1717, she died.</p>\n\n<p>Maria Sibylla Merian is one of the most important contributors to the natural sciences. After she published her book Metamorphosis of the Insects of Suriname, her work became renowned in The Netherlands, England, Denmark, Germany, and France. Then, around twenty years after her death, Carl Linnaeus developed a new way to classify the natural world. In 1758, in the tenth edition of his Systema Naturae, he included around a hundred species using Maria Sibylla Merian’s illustrations.</p>\n\n<p><b>Below is a selection of books from DU Libraries\' circulating collections. </b></p>', 1, NULL, 'item', 'text_only', 50, 1, NULL, 0, 1, NULL, '{}', 26, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, NULL, 1, 8, '2025-03-26 15:49:36', '2026-03-18 18:37:50', 'Madison Sussmann', NULL),

(106, '0f52a916-4cf0-4d91-b16d-717d587a2477', 'a36ddb1f-2141-4e7a-a2d5-e5241d39d4b6', '', NULL, '', 'Deborah Howard drawing Arthur and Edith Stern in their home, 2004', 'image', 'image&#x2F;jpeg', '0f52a916-4cf0-4d91-b16d-717d587a2477_1743184426470_item_media.jfif', 'b2a4b280-1a4c-43e0-a66a-ae8f6c28ec96', '<p>“In 2003, sparked by a discussion in my Figure Painting class on racial stereotypes and aging I began developing the Portrait Project: Child Survivors Of The Holocaust. After wrestling with the subject of the Holocaust in my art beginning in graduate school, I had finally found a way to deal with a subject of such magnitude through the simplicity of a drawn portrait.</p>\n\n<p>I have interviewed twenty-five people in Denver, Chicago and Los Angeles. Finding people to pose can be a challenge; it was especially so in the beginning. I found people by networking through friends, family and colleagues. I met them in chance encounters at my son’s Wednesday night orchestra rehearsals and an antique store. One survivor was selling hot dogs from a cart on the University of Denver campus. Many of the people describe the reasons they survived as chance, fate and luck. I see a parallel in the serendipitous way I find survivors.</p>\n\n<p>As an artist I feel the media of drawing is ideal. It can be very intimate and can capture the intangible qualities of an individual. A drawing, with all its quirky lines, marks and smudges, is a document not only of the subject, but of the artist as well. The artist becomes a witness, and as the number of survivors dwindles artists can take an important role in the documentation of the Jewish Holocaust.</p>\n\n<p>My goal in this project is to create portraits of people at this moment in time. I am not interested in making then look like victims or heroes. I am interested in capturing their humanity and in depicting very real people who have lived extraordinary lives and also very ordinary lives.</p>\n\n<p>This project is dedicated to my uncle Harry Lopas who was a child Holocaust Survivor and to his father Aaron Lopas who was murdered in Auschwitz.”</p>\n\n<p><i> - Deborah Howard</i></p>', 1, '', 'item', 'media_left', 50, 0, 'A color photo of three people at a table. Her family was well off and also very cultured. During the 1930s she was aware of the impending war both from the news and also witnessing ships of Jews being deported down the Danube. She had heard of Kristallnacht and knew of Jewish persecution happening elsewhere. Ava was taught by her parents to be prepared for a war that would likely come. Around 1940, her family moved from Novi Sad to Belgrade as they felt it would be better for their anonymity to live in a larger city. Ava remained in Novi Sad for one year to finish her schooling while she lived with her grandmother. She then left to be with her family in Belgrade. On April 6, 1941 the Nazis invaded Yugoslavia and bombarded Belgrade for several days.</p>\n\n<p>On April 12, her birthday, the Nazis entered the city, and put up posters instructing all of the Jews to register with the Germans. Her family knew that this was not a good choice and decided to figure out how to hide. Her father left to hide in Hungary. Ava’s mother used her birth certificate to acquire ID papers as she had converted prior to marriage and her birth certificate said she was Christian. Her sister married a Greek Orthodox man and the priest predated the marriage certificate prior to the war to avoid any suspicion. Ava was the only one who could not get ID papers, so she went to hide at a farm where her father had made arrangements for her. Ava spent four years on the farm, taking on the identity of a farmhand. She did chores and would sneak back to Belgrade to visit her mother and sister when possible. During her time at the farm, she received very little food. On one of her trips back to Belgrade, her mother asked her to get rations. While waiting in line, a German soldier passed by her, and since she was not perfectly aligned with the rest of the line, hit her in the mouth with the butt of his gun, breaking her front teeth. Ava remembers that in the winter of 1942, the Jews of Belgrade were rounded up and drowned under the ice of the Danube. Ava is an artist and had been prior to the war.</p>\n\n<p>During her time on the farm, she completed a few drawings which are at the United States Holocaust Memorial in Washington, DC. Near the end of the war, she decided to return to Belgrade. She found that her sister had died as a result of being taken to war. Ava went looking for her father and grandmother in Novi Sad, however when she arrived she was informed that she was the first Jew to return. A former neighbor told her that her father and grandmother were taken to Auschwitz where they both died. Her father played in the Auschwitz orchestra that performed as people were marched to the gas chambers. She returned to Belgrade with a few belongings from her former life and began to look for a job. She ended up going to the Art Academy in Belgrade and working as a painter and animator for movie studios.</p>\n\n<p>In 1949, Ava and her mother left Yugoslavia for Israel. They arrived at the port of Haifa and quickly made their way south to Tel Aviv. She was immediately hired by a movie studio to do a similar job as she had done while in Belgrade. The man who hired her became her first husband—Yitzhak Kadishson. They were married for thirty years prior to his passing. About a decade later, a man named Nathan Schieber bought some of her art in America. They met several months after and fell in love. She decided to leave Israel and move to the Chicago area. She continued to paint, heavily influenced by her time hiding at the farm.</p>', 1, NULL, 'item', 'media_left', 33, 1, NULL, 0, 1, NULL, '{}', 13, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, NULL, 1, 8, '2025-03-28 16:47:51', '2026-04-14 21:26:57', 'Madison Sussmann', NULL),

(132, '179aeeaa-336f-442c-9a60-711dc2e7b13e', 'ce8c91ee-3a3f-402b-91eb-5b81fee2deb0', '', NULL, '', 'Since its 1884 publication, The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn has been repeatedly challenged and banned for its language and racial content, yet it remains a cornerstone of American literature and a subject of ongoing debate.', 'image', 'image&#x2F;jpeg', '179aeeaa-336f-442c-9a60-711dc2e7b13e_1743790912861_item_media.jpg', 'fb6ca2bd-abda-4762-932f-2cd8d6e3dd7a', 'Since its 1884 publication, <i>The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn</i> has been one of the most consistently challenged and banned books in the United States, primarily due to its language and portrayal of race. It was first banned in 1885 by the Concord Public Library for being \"immoral,\" and in 1902, Denver followed suit, calling it \"vulgar.\" In 1957, the NAACP urged New York schools to remove it for its racist content, and in 1969, Miami-Dade Junior College removed it from reading lists for creating an “emotional block” in readers. Efforts to censor the book escalated in the 1970s, with some publishers replacing racial slurs with euphemisms, while others defended keeping the original language with scholarly context. Throughout the 1980s and 1990s, it was removed from or challenged in schools across states like Pennsylvania, Texas, and Arizona, with critics arguing that its language harmed students, particularly African American youth. Some districts, like Enid, Oklahoma, responded with teacher training instead of removal. Toni Morrison defended the novel in a 1996 edition, emphasizing its literary power despite its discomforts. Challenges persisted into the 2000s and 2010s, with <i>Huckleberry Finn</i> appearing on the ALA’s most challenged books list and censored editions being published, including one in 2011 that removed over 200 racial slurs. In 2016, it was briefly suspended in Accomack County, Virginia, alongside <i>To Kill a Mockingbird</i> after parent complaints. The titles in this exhibit—<i>The Grapes of Wrath</i>, <i>Of Mice and Men</i>, <i>Brave New World</i>, <i>The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn</i>, and <i>Anne Frank: The Diary of a Young Girl</i>—have been the subject of intense scrutiny across decades, locations, and political climates. The reasons have varied: objections to language, depictions of race or sexuality, moral content, or simply discomfort with difficult truths. These challenges often reflect the fears, values, and conflicts of the time and place in which they occur. Despite efforts to suppress them, these books have endured, studied and shared for their insight, honesty, and impact. As this exhibit shows, censorship is rarely broad or sweeping; it is often local, driven by individuals or small groups. And yet, the consequences ripple outward. By learning the history of banned books, we better understand the importance of protecting the freedom to read and the role libraries, schools, and readers play in upholding that freedom.', 1, NULL, 'item', 'text_only', 50, 1, NULL, 0, 1, NULL, 'item1', 17, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, NULL, 0, 8, '2025-04-04 15:10:59', '2026-04-16 17:42:44', 'Madison Sussmann', NULL),

(138, '9624990e-791f-40da-8ecd-13a9725874be', 'f4a8ec55-1961-43ca-a805-f7ccc957479b', '', NULL, '', '', 'text', '', '', NULL, 'This exhibit, <i>#NoMorePios: The Legacy of Settler Colonialism and the University of Denver</i>, traces the university’s long and complex relationship with settler colonialism through its use of the \"Pioneer\" nickname and associated mascots. Founded just six months before the Sand Creek Massacre in 1864, DU\'s origin is inextricably linked to acts of violence against Indigenous peoples and a legacy of colonial expansion. The exhibit explores how DU\'s branding, mascots, and traditions—especially the Pioneer identity and figures like Denver Boone—have romanticized settler colonialism and contributed to the erasure and stereotyping of Native peoples. Through archival materials, student activism timelines, and scholarly context, the exhibit highlights how Indigenous students, faculty, and allies have long challenged these representations and pushed for structural change. Efforts like the #NoMorePios campaign, inspired by national movements such as #NotYourMascot, call on the university to reckon with its past and reconsider its symbols. By examining DU’s shifting iconography and student-led resistance, the exhibit invites visitors to consider whose histories are being honored and whose are being ignored—and to ask, as the exhibit concludes: <i>Which side of history do we want to be on?</i>', 1, NULL, 'item', 'text_only', 50, 1, NULL, 0, 1, NULL, 'item2', 11, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, NULL, 0, 8, '2025-04-04 15:24:11', '2026-04-14 21:27:12', 'Madison Sussmann', NULL),

(153, '82cdc764-7bb8-4ae5-b770-94ea5f8bbc6a', '561837a9-2a43-4187-95d6-9376a48b9b85', '', NULL, '<h2><b>Inter-relationship between Nahuatl and Spanish</b></h2>', '', 'text', '', '', NULL, '<p>After the Spanish conquered the Aztecs, Spanish and Nahuatl began to mix. As both languages mixed they strengthened both their presence across Mexico and the south-west region of the U.S. as other indigenous languages became extinct due to cultural diffusion (Castillo 1963, 1-2). However, this process occurred overtime and due to some minor similarities between the two languages, the accentuation and vocabulary of both were able to connect in grammatical and structural ways creating loanwords (Castillo 1963, 4-5). This diffusion between both cultures and languages has resulted in this lasting relationship between Nahuatl and Spanish that has both greatly changed and protected these groups. However, the extent of this divergence between Nahuatl and Spanish has not been explored enough academically (Castillo 1963, 12).</p>\n\n<p style=\"font-size: 80%;\">Díaz del Castillo, Bernal. 1963. <i>The Conquest of New Spain.</i> Translated and with an introduction by John M. Designed to bring together professional astronomers, amateur stargazers, and curious members of the community, the observatory continues to host <a href=\"https://www.denverastro.org/das-events/\"><strong>Public Nights and Open Houses</strong></a>, offering visitors the opportunity to observe stars, the Moon, planets, nebulae, and other celestial phenomena.</p>\n\n<p>The observatory’s origins are closely tied to the work of Professor Herbert Alonzo Howe, one of the University of Denver’s early faculty members. Howe arrived in Denver by train on October 2, 1880, joining a faculty of just six professors. A graduate of the University of Cincinnati, where he earned his master’s degree in astronomy and gained practical experience at the Cincinnati Observatory, Howe chose to accept a position in the relatively undeveloped city of Denver. Though smaller and less academically established than Cincinnati, Denver’s natural beauty and potential for institutional growth appealed to him. Throughout his life, Howe kept detailed journals chronicling his daily activities, offering valuable insight into his early academic work in the region.</p>', 1, NULL, 'item', 'media_left', 33, 0, 'Black-and-white photo of the observatory. These observations and calculations are also known as Kepler’s Problem. Kepler’s Problem is a special case of the two-body problem. The two-body problem refers to the ways in which the movement of two massive objects can be predicted. The problem hypothesizes that two objects interact only with one another and that they only force affecting each object comes from the other. In the case of Kepler’s problem two objects interact based on a central force that varies in strength and may be attractive or repulsive. The equation associated with this problem can be used to find the position and speed of the objects over time based on their masses, positions, and velocities.', 1, NULL, 'item', 'media_bottom', 75, 0, NULL, 0, 1, NULL, '{}', 10, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, NULL, 1, 8, '2025-07-11 14:30:49', '2026-04-14 19:20:58', 'Madison Sussmann', 'Madison Sussmann'),

(176, '4a7ea0b8-4055-469b-8246-65101c5e70bd', '1a37ef6b-863e-45b5-8fdf-37ec54654fc9', '', NULL, '', 'Located in the Large Magellanic Cloud, one of our neighboring dwarf galaxies, this young globular-like star cluster is surrounded by a pattern of filamentary nebulosity. Photo credit: Robert Sullivan.', 'image', 'image&#x2F;jpeg', '4a7ea0b8-4055-469b-8246-65101c5e70bd_1752264590357_item_media.jpg', '4891d1bd-9398-48c6-a8af-4444beecb9f6', 'Most of Howe’s work focused on observing the neglected nebulae from the <i>New General Catalogue of Nebulae and Clusters of Stars (NGC)</i>. The <i>NGC</i> is an inventory of deep-sky objects. It was compiled by John Louis Emil Dreyer in 1888 based on other nebula catalogues that existed at the time. The <i>NGC</i> contains 7,840 objects, known as the <i>NGC</i> objects, and Dreyer has published two supplements to the <i>NGC</i>, known as the <i>Index Catalogues (IC)</i>, describing a further 5,386 astronomical objects. (180, 'f7124d25-dba7-465a-b8cd-e7bc050346c7', '1a37ef6b-863e-45b5-8fdf-37ec54654fc9', '', NULL, '', 'Portrait of Herbert A. Howe, Denver astronomer and founding director of Chamberlin Observatory. Photo credit: DU Special Collections and Archives.', 'image', 'image&#x2F;tiff', 'd755b99e-cc03-40e4-8695-55349649f37e', '533193d3-8c6a-4b37-822c-1bba1c15b122', '<p>When Howe began teaching at DU, the university had no astronomy program or equipment. To initiate astronomical instruction, he borrowed a transit telescope from the U.S. Coast and Geodetic Survey and installed it on campus. In 1881, he taught the university’s first astronomy course. Two years later, in 1883, Howe began doctoral work and oversaw the construction of a small observatory on the roof of the Junior Preparatory Building in downtown Denver.</p>\n\n<p> <a href=\"https://www.jstor.org/stable/1967399?searchText=%28ti%3A%28kepler%27s+problem%29+AND+au%3A%22howe%22%29&searchUri=%2Faction%2FdoAdvancedSearch%3Fq0%3Dkepler%2527s%2Bproblem%26q1%3Dhowe%26f0%3Dti%26c1%3DAND%26f1%3Dau%26acc%3Don%26so%3Drel&ab_segments=0%2Fbasic_phrase_search%2Fcontrol&refreqid=fastly-default%3A6cddb5b5059e0601e1bb801fe29fe691&seq=1\"><strong>Howe’s research</strong></a> focused on Kepler’s Problem, a classical mechanics question that forms a special case of the two-body problem. This problem considers how the positions and velocities of two massive bodies, influenced solely by their mutual gravitational attraction, evolve over time. In Kepler’s formulation, the interaction occurs through a central force that can vary in strength and be either attractive or repulsive. Solving Kepler’s Problem involves calculating the motion of these objects given their masses, positions, and velocities. In recognition of his research, particularly on Kepler’s Problem and his observational work on the Great Comet of 1882, Howe was awarded a Doctorate of Science from Boston University in June 1884.</p>', 1, NULL, 'item', 'media_right', 25, 0, 'Black-and-white portrait of Herbert A. On February 7, 1888, Chancellor David Hastings received a letter from Humphrey B. Chamberlin, a prominent Denver real estate developer, offering to finance the construction of a new observatory and all necessary equipment. Howe, the university’s most experienced astronomer, was asked to design the facility and select its primary instrument. He chose a 20-inch aperture refracting telescope built by Alvan Clark & Sons of Cambridge, Massachusetts, then one of the premier telescope makers in the United States.</p>\n\n<p>The observatory was constructed specifically to house this telescope. Its architecture includes reinforced floors to support the instrument’s substantial weight and a large iron dome with a movable shutter to allow unobstructed views of the night sky. The telescope arrived in 1894, and Howe oversaw its final assembly. Trial observations began on July 14 of that year, marking the official beginning of the Chamberlin Observatory’s scientific operations.</p>', 1, NULL, 'item', 'media_left', 33, 0, 'Black-and-white photo of a large refractor telescope mounted on a tall central pillar inside a domed observatory. While the other craft on Mars missions are spacecraft, <span style=\"color: #d83c06;\"><strong>rovers</strong></span> are designed as land vehicles. Socialites came from across the country to see the show, dressed in the fashion of 1878, when the original iteration of the opera house opened, epitomizing the festival’s nostalgia for a simpler time. The mining origins of the town and the environment in which the opera house first opened, were honored during the festival. The Opera House was originally opened during the gold rush, mirroring the conditions in which it reopened during a smaller gold rush during the Great Depression. The success of the opera house shows the longing that people had for a simpler time. A similar trend has happened in modern recessions, including the current one. Central City opera house also exemplifies how important Colorado is in the national arts scene, rather than as just a state for outdoors activities and mining. (227, '9da73cb7-cdc7-46ab-9032-b097b99e2031', '854efac7-10d5-4c27-a59e-e5b34b70efb9', NULL, NULL, 'Printing-out vs. Developing-out', NULL, 'text', 'text&#x2F;plain', NULL, NULL, '<p>Two terms that are often used in photographic printing are printing-out and developing-out, and there is an important distinction between the two processes: </p>\n\n<p><strong>Printing-out</strong> methods use solely light reactions to create an image and had no chemical development. The light source was most often the sun, which would darken the photo paper. The image could then be placed in toner to adjust the color, sealed with a fixative, and washed to remove excess toner. </p> \n<p><strong>Developing-out</strong> methods use exposure to a weak light source and chemical development to produce an image. The image would be dipped in a developing fluid and fixative, and then placed in a wash to prevent overdeveloping the image. William Henry Fox Talbot discovered this technique in tandem with the positive/negative approach to photography that made multiplication of prints much easier to achieve. The method had its flaws, though, as the print needed to be chemically fixed to preserve the photo. Even with this, the prints would often be a brown or purple color and details would blur with age and exposure (Ware 2003). These shortcomings would soon make way for new and improved methods, many of which are explored in this exhibit.</p>\n\n<p><em>Courtesy of DU Special Collections</em></p>', 1, '', 'item', 'media_right', 33, 0, 'A black and white, sepia-tone photograph of a balding man with a beard.
